title: README

Zone: 7B
Purpose: Medicinal plant cultivation + apothecary + companion design + graph-based herbal knowledge system.

This vault connects:

  • Plants

  • Energetics

🔗 How to Use This Vault

1. Graph View

Open Graph View to explore clusters:

2. Start Here

Begin with index files in 00_Indexes/:

  • Master Plant Index - See all plants by annual/perennial
  • Medicinal Actions Index - Browse by health function

5. Preparations

6. Monthly Planning

  • What to start/sow each month

🏷️ Tag System

Medicinal: #digestive #nervine #respiratory #antimicrobial #wound #immune #circulatory

Growth: #annual #perennial #sun #partial-shade #low-water #moist-soil

  6. Use tags to discover plant clusters

# 🌿 Herbalism Vault

**Zone:** 7B  
**Purpose:** Medicinal plant cultivation + apothecary + companion design + graph-based herbal knowledge system.

This vault connects:
- Plants
- Medicinal actions
- Months
- Garden placement
- Energetics
- Preparation methods

## 🔗 How to Use This Vault

### 1. **Graph View**
Open Graph View to explore clusters:
- Digestive herbs cluster
- Respiratory support network
- Pollinator plants
- Mediterranean dry-soil guild

### 2. **Start Here**
Begin with index files in `00_Indexes/`:
- **Master Plant Index** - See all plants by annual/perennial
- **Medicinal Actions Index** - Browse by health function
- **Month-by-Month Calendar** - Plan your growing season
- **Color & Energetics Index** - Explore plant energetics

### 3. **Plant Files**
Each plant in `01_Plants/` has:
- Growing conditions
- Harvest timing
- Medicinal actions
- Preparation methods
- Companion plants

### 4. **Garden Design**
In `04_Garden_Design/`:
- Height mapping
- Water zone planning
- Companion guilds
- Bed layouts

### 5. **Preparations**
In `03_Preparations/`:
- Tea infusion ratios
- Tincture recipes
- Salve formulas
- Syrup guides

### 6. **Monthly Planning**
In `05_Months/`:
- What to start/sow each month
- Zone 7B frost dates
- Succession planting

## 🏷️ Tag System

Use these tags for graph filtering:

**Medicinal:** `#digestive` `#nervine` `#respiratory` `#antimicrobial` `#wound` `#immune` `#circulatory`

**Growth:** `#annual` `#perennial` `#sun` `#partial-shade` `#low-water` `#moist-soil`

**Color:** `#purple` `#yellow` `#white` `#green`

**Energetics:** `#warming` `#cooling` `#drying` `#moistening`

## 📊 Graph Architecture

Plants automatically link to:
- Months (planting calendar)
- Medicinal categories (action-based)
- Companion guilds (growth synergy)
- Color index (visual organization)
- Preparation methods (usage)

## 🚀 Getting Started

1. Open this folder in **VS Code**
2. Install Obsidian and enable **Graph View**
3. Start with `Master Plant Index`
4. Follow monthly planting guides in `05_Months/`
5. Cross-reference preparations and medicinal uses
6. Use tags to discover plant clusters

---

**Version:** 1.0  
**Last Updated:** February 2026  
**Created for:** Zone 7B Herbalism & Apothecary

title: tagging-system
html:
🏷️ Tag Architecture

This document defines the tagging system used throughout the vault for graph filtering and organization.

Medicinal Tags

Use these tags to categorize herbs by their primary medicinal actions:

  • #digestive - Supports digestion, relieves gas/bloating
  • #nervine - Calms, relaxes, supports nervous system
  • #respiratory - Supports lung function, expectorant
  • #antimicrobial - Antibacterial, antiviral, antifungal
  • #wound - Wound healing, tissue repair
  • #immune - Strengthens immune response
  • #circulatory - Supports blood flow and circulation
  • #hormonal - Supports endocrine balance
  • #detox - Supports body's natural cleansing

Growth Tags

Use these tags to organize plants by growing characteristics:

  • #annual - Completes lifecycle in one year
  • #perennial - Returns year after year
  • #sun - Requires full sun (6+ hours)
  • #partial-shade - Tolerates some shade
  • #low-water - Drought tolerant
  • #moist-soil - Prefers consistent moisture
  • #container - Works well in pots
  • #ground-cover - Good as border plant

Color Tags

Use these tags to organize by flower/leaf color:

  • #purple - Purple flowers or foliage
  • #yellow - Yellow flowers or foliage
  • #white - White flowers or foliage
  • #pink - Pink or rose flowers
  • #green - Green foliage dominant
  • #red - Red flowers or foliage

Energetic Tags

Based on Traditional Chinese Medicine & Herbalism:

  • #warming - Increases body warmth, circulation
  • #cooling - Reduces heat, inflammation
  • #drying - Reduces moisture, fluids
  • #moistening - Adds hydration

Season Tags

For planting & harvest organization:

  • #spring - Spring activities
  • #summer - Summer activities
  • #fall - Fall/autumn activities
  • #winter - Winter activities (year-round care)

Life Cycle Tags

  • #seed-start - Can be started indoors
  • #direct-sow - Direct seed into ground
  • #transplant - Plant as seedling
  • #self-seeding - Reseeds itself

Example Plant Tag Usage

A plant file might have:

Tags: #digestive #perennial #sun #low-water #cooling #purple #spring

This allows filtering for:

  • All digestive herbs
  • All perennials
  • All low-water plants
  • All cooling herbs
  • All purple flowers
  • All spring planting

How to Search by Tag in Obsidian

  1. Click "Open local graph" (bottom left)
  2. Use filter: tag:#digestive
  3. See all connected herbs in that medicinal action cluster

Pro Tip: Combining tags reveals powerful connections:

  • #digestive #warming = warming digestive herbs
  • #nervine #cooling = cooling nervine herbs
  • #low-water #sun = herbs for dry, sunny spots
raw:
# 🏷️ Tag Architecture

This document defines the tagging system used throughout the vault for graph filtering and organization.

## Medicinal Tags

Use these tags to categorize herbs by their primary medicinal actions:

- `#digestive` - Supports digestion, relieves gas/bloating
- `#nervine` - Calms, relaxes, supports nervous system
- `#respiratory` - Supports lung function, expectorant
- `#antimicrobial` - Antibacterial, antiviral, antifungal
- `#wound` - Wound healing, tissue repair
- `#immune` - Strengthens immune response
- `#circulatory` - Supports blood flow and circulation
- `#hormonal` - Supports endocrine balance
- `#detox` - Supports body's natural cleansing

## Growth Tags

Use these tags to organize plants by growing characteristics:

- `#annual` - Completes lifecycle in one year
- `#perennial` - Returns year after year
- `#sun` - Requires full sun (6+ hours)
- `#partial-shade` - Tolerates some shade
- `#low-water` - Drought tolerant
- `#moist-soil` - Prefers consistent moisture
- `#container` - Works well in pots
- `#ground-cover` - Good as border plant

## Color Tags

Use these tags to organize by flower/leaf color:

- `#purple` - Purple flowers or foliage
- `#yellow` - Yellow flowers or foliage
- `#white` - White flowers or foliage
- `#pink` - Pink or rose flowers
- `#green` - Green foliage dominant
- `#red` - Red flowers or foliage

## Energetic Tags

Based on Traditional Chinese Medicine & Herbalism:

- `#warming` - Increases body warmth, circulation
- `#cooling` - Reduces heat, inflammation
- `#drying` - Reduces moisture, fluids
- `#moistening` - Adds hydration

## Season Tags

For planting & harvest organization:

- `#spring` - Spring activities
- `#summer` - Summer activities
- `#fall` - Fall/autumn activities
- `#winter` - Winter activities (year-round care)

## Life Cycle Tags

- `#seed-start` - Can be started indoors
- `#direct-sow` - Direct seed into ground
- `#transplant` - Plant as seedling
- `#self-seeding` - Reseeds itself

## Example Plant Tag Usage

A plant file might have:
```
Tags: #digestive #perennial #sun #low-water #cooling #purple #spring
```

This allows filtering for:
- All digestive herbs
- All perennials
- All low-water plants
- All cooling herbs
- All purple flowers
- All spring planting

---

## How to Search by Tag in Obsidian

1. Click "Open local graph" (bottom left)
2. Use filter: `tag:#digestive`
3. See all connected herbs in that medicinal action cluster

---

**Pro Tip:** Combining tags reveals powerful connections:
- `#digestive #warming` = warming digestive herbs
- `#nervine #cooling` = cooling nervine herbs
- `#low-water #sun` = herbs for dry, sunny spots

title: graph-logic
html:
🧠 Graph Structure Logic

This document explains how the vault is designed to create meaningful connections in Obsidian's graph view.

Link Architecture

Primary Hubs (Index Files)

Each index file serves as a central hub connecting to multiple plants:

  • Master Plant Index → All 30+ plants
  • Medicinal Actions Index → 6 medicinal categories
  • Month-by-Month Calendar → 5 monthly files
  • Color & Energetics Index → Color organization
  • Companion Guilds Index → 4 guild groups

Secondary Connections (Category Pages)

Medicinal action pages link back to plants:

Digestive Support

  • Connects to: Anise, Fennel, Caraway, Dill, Peppermint, etc.
  • Creates a network of herbs with shared function

Nervine & Calming

  • Connects to: Lavender, Lemon Balm, Catnip, Chamomile, etc.
  • Shows complementary nervine herbs

Respiratory Support

  • Connects to: Hyssop, Horehound, Thyme, Oregano, Sage, Echinacea

Antimicrobial Herbs

  • Connects to: Oregano, Thyme, Rosemary, Echinacea, etc.

Wound & Circulation

  • Connects to: Lavender, Yarrow, Echinacea, Ginger, Hawthorn

Tertiary Connections (Companion Guilds)

Companion guilds create synergy clusters:

Pollinator Guild

  • Borage, Bergamot, Echinacea, Lavender, Yarrow
  • All attract beneficial insects

Mediterranean Dry Guild

  • Thyme, Oregano, Rosemary, Sage, Marjoram
  • Share similar growing conditions

Digestive Guild

  • Anise, Fennel, Caraway, Dill, Cilantro
  • All support digestion, similar aromatic family

Mint Containment Guild

  • Peppermint, Lemon Mint, Lemon Balm, Catnip, Mountain Mint
  • Mints that need controlled growth

Link Patterns

Bidirectional Links

Each plant file links TO:

[[Master Plant Index]]
[[Digestive Support]]  (if applicable)
[[Mediterranean Bed]]  (if in garden design)
[[March]]  (if planted in March)
[[Lavender|Companion Plants]]

And each of those files links BACK to the plant.

Graph Clusters This Creates

By Function:

Digestive cluster:
- Anise ↔ Fennel ↔ Caraway ↔ Dill ↔ Peppermint
- All link to: Digestive Support (hub)
- Color: See by tags #digestive

By Season:

April cluster:
- Anise, Borage, Dill, Fennel
- All link to: April.md (month file)
- All can be successively sown together

By Garden Design:

Mediterranean Bed cluster:
- Thyme, Oregano, Rosemary, Sage
- All link to: Mediterranean Bed.md
- All share low-water needs

By Color:

Purple cluster:
- Lavender, Bergamot, Hyssop, Echinacea
- All link to: Color & Energetics Index
- Visual theme linking

Graph View Navigation

What You'll See

Open graph view:

  1. Master Plant Index is the center hub
  2. 30+ plants radiate outward
  3. Medicinal action pages form secondary hubs
  4. Monthly files create seasonal branches
  5. Color tags create visual clusters

How to Explore

  1. Click Master Plant Index → See all plants relative to primary index
  2. Filter by tag: #digestive → See digestive herb network
  3. Click April.md → See all spring-planted herbs
  4. Click Mediterranean Bed → See garden design cluster
  5. Click Lavender → See all connections (actions, months, companions, colors)

Design Benefits

✅ Multi-dimensional navigation - Reach same plant from multiple angles ✅ Thematic clustering - Plants naturally group by function/season ✅ Visual learning - Graph view reveals connections instantly ✅ Cross-referencing - Extract information through linked paths ✅ Planning tool - See garden design, planting schedule, medicinal uses in one view

Tag-Based Filtering

The tagging system (see _config/tagging-system.md) creates invisible graph clusters:

  • Filter #digestive → All digestive herbs visible
  • Filter #low-water → All drought tolerant plants visible
  • Filter #spring → All spring activities visible
  • Filter #warming #digestive → Warming digestive herbs only

Obsidian Plugins That Enhance This

Recommended:

  • Dataview - Create dynamic plant tables
  • Graph Analysis - Quantify cluster strength
  • Smart Typography - Better markdown rendering
  • Tag Wrangler - Manage tags visually

The goal: A living herbal intelligence map where each plant is a node in a larger knowledge network.

title: September
html:
🍂 September Planting Guide (Zone 7B)

Season: Fall Transition
Average Temps: 70–80°F (warm but cooling)
Days of Light: Shortening (12–14 hours)
Frost Risk: None yet (first frost Oct 25–Nov 5)
Soil: Still warm, beginning to cool

This Month's Role

September is cool-season restart month. Plant cilantro, dill, and other cool-season herbs one last time for fall harvest. Begin serious preservation (drying, tinctures) as summer annuals decline. Enjoy peak harvest of perennials.

🌿 Plant Cool-Season Herbs (Fall Crop)

September is perfect for cool-season replanting. Cool nights + warm days = ideal for these herbs.

[[Cilantro]]

  • Sow: Early to mid-September
  • Depth: ¼ inch
  • Spacing: Thin to 6 inches
  • Germination: 7–14 days
  • Harvest window: October–November before frost
  • Preference: Much prefers cool weather to spring/summer
  • Notes: This is actually better crop than spring

[[Dill]]

  • Sow: Early to mid-September
  • Depth: ¼ inch
  • Spacing: Thin to 12 inches
  • Germination: 7–10 days
  • Harvest: October–November
  • Fall advantage: Slower bolting than summer

[[Chamomile]]

  • Option 1: Direct sow (may overwinter, flower next year)
  • Option 2: Let existing plants self-seed
  • Notes: Often self-seeds prolifically; accept volunteers
  • Harvest: If flowering by November, use sparingly

[[Dandelion]] (if growing)

  • Sow: Early September
  • Notes: Overwinters; you'll harvest spring
  • Advantage: Spring greens from fall planting

🌾 Preservation Begins

Summer is peak; time to preserve for winter.

Drying Herbs

Best herbs to dry now:

  • Oregano, Marjoram, Thyme: Peak potency
  • Sage: Excellent dried
  • Basil: Dry before frost kills it
  • Peppermint: Last vibrant leaves

Drying process:

  1. Cut stems (morning after dew dries)
  2. Bundle with twine (6–10 stems per bundle)
  3. Hang upside down in cool, dark, airy place
  4. 2–3 weeks drying typically
  5. Leaves should crumble when dry
  6. Store in sealed glass jars, cool dark place

Making Tinctures

Perfect time to start long-maceration tinctures:

  • Tincture herbs now
  • 4–6 week soak
  • Ready by November
  • Perfect for winter immune season

See [[Tincture Guide]] for full process

Making Syrups & Preparations

Harvest heavily for:

  • Syrups (concentrated remedies)
  • Salves (dried herb infusions)
  • Herbal blends for winter

🌳 Summer Plants Declining

Annuals Winding Down

  • [[Basil]]: Still productive but frost warning coming

    • Harvest heavily now
    • Don't allow to set seed (unless want volunteers)
    • Dry for winter use
    • Make pesto for freezing

  • [[Dill]]: Earlier sowings flowering, going to seed

    • Allow some to go to seed (volunteers next year funny
    • Harvest green dill for fresh use
    • Harvest seed clusters for drying

  • [[Anise]]: Finishing for season

    • Let complete seed cycle
    • Harvest seed for teas, storage
    • Plant won't overwinter

Tender Perennials Under Stress

  • [[Peppermint]], [[Lemon Mint]]: Still productive but slowing
  • Continue harvesting but noting reduced vigor

📋 September Task Checklist

Fall Planting

  • [ ] Sow cilantro (early-mid September)
  • [ ] Sow dill (early-mid September)
  • [ ] Consider chamomile/dandelion sowing
  • [ ] Keep new sowings moist (germination 7–14 days)

Preservation

  • [ ] Dry oregano, marjoram, thyme
  • [ ] Dry basil (heavily before frost)
  • [ ] Make tinctures using peak-potency herbs
  • [ ] Make syrups (especially respiratory, immune)
  • [ ] Make harissa or dried herb blends

Garden Maintenance

  • [ ] Heavy harvesting of summer herbs
  • [ ] Note which herbs performed well (record for next year)
  • [ ] Begin reducing water (cooling season)
  • [ ] Deadhead flowers (unless saving seed)
  • [ ] Weed one final time

Observation

  • [ ] First frost date awareness (Oct 25–Nov 5 Zone 7B)
  • [ ] Watch forecast mid-September onward
  • [ ] Note plant health/pest activity
  • [ ] Photo document changes

🌡️ Frost Awareness

Not Yet!

  • September frost is rare but possible (first week)
  • Watch forecast September 1–10
  • October 20+ is when to really vigilant

Start Planning

  • Which plants to protect? (Tender annuals)
  • Which to let freeze? (Basil, tender annuals serving their function)
  • Frost cloth available? (Have on hand by October)

🌿 Harvest Notes

Peak Harvest for Preservation

Now is premium time for:

  • Oregano (peak essential oils)
  • Marjoram (strongest flavor)
  • Sage (concentrated compounds)
  • Basil (before frost kills it)

Timing:

  • Harvest in morning (after dew dries, before heat)
  • When plants in flower (peak potency)
  • Top ⅓ of growth

What to Preserve

  • Dried herbs (1–2 year storage)
  • Tinctures (5–10 year storage)
  • Syrups (refrigerated, 3–4 months)
  • Oils (infused, 6–12 months)

🚰 Watering Changes

Reduce Frequency

  • Cooling season = less evaporation
  • Rainfall typically increases (September can surprise)
  • Check soil before watering
  • Top inch dry = water time

Focus on New Sowings

  • Fall-planted cilantro + dill need consistent moisture until germinated
  • Established perennials need less

💡 September Tips

Tip Why
Preserve heavily Winter months need dried herbs
Harvest basil aggressively Frost will end it; harvest while thriving
Plant cilantro/dill now Fall crop superior to spring
Watch forecast Early frost possible (rare but watch)
Make tinctures Ready by December for cold/immune season
Keep records Note what worked, what didn't
Enjoy peak flavor September harvests are peak potency

🌸 What's Coming October+

October onward:

  • Frost increasingly likely (Oct 20 watch date)
  • Basil + tender annuals killed by first frost
  • Cool-season herbs (cilantro, dill) thrive
  • Perennials begin preparing dormancy
  • Serious preservation continues
  • Garden activity reducing (preparing for dormancy)

📊 From Garden to Shelf

Preservation pathway this month:

Fresh herb → Bundle → Hang dry → Storable → Winter use
          ↓
        Tincture → 4-6 week → Bottled → 5-10 year shelf life
              ↓
            Syrup → Honey + heat → Cool → 3-4 month fridge
                          ↓
                      Salve → Oil + wax → Bottled → 6-12 months

🔗 Related Pages

Monthly Planning:

  • [[August]] (if created) - Summer peak continues
  • [[October]] (if created) - Frost arrives
  • [[Month-by-Month Calendar (Zone 7B)]] - Full overview

Harvesting & Preservation:

  • [[Tea Infusion Guide]] - Fresh + dried use
  • [[Tincture Guide]] - Alcohol extraction
  • [[Salve Guide]] - Oil + beeswax
  • [[Syrup Guide]] - Honey preserved

Reference:

  • [[Medicinal Actions Index]] - What to preserve for what purpose
  • [[Master Plant Index]] - Herb reference

September Summary: Cool season restarts! Plant cilantro + dill for fall harvest. Heavy preservation time—dry, tincture, make syrups from peak plants. Garden beginning wind-down. Preparation for dormancy season starting.
raw:
# 🍂 September Planting Guide (Zone 7B)

**Season:** Fall Transition  
**Average Temps:** 70–80°F (warm but cooling)  
**Days of Light:** Shortening (12–14 hours)  
**Frost Risk:** None yet (first frost Oct 25–Nov 5)  
**Soil:** Still warm, beginning to cool

---

## This Month's Role

September is **cool-season restart month**. Plant cilantro, dill, and other cool-season herbs one last time for fall harvest. Begin serious preservation (drying, tinctures) as summer annuals decline. Enjoy peak harvest of perennials.

---

## 🌿 Plant Cool-Season Herbs (Fall Crop)

**September is perfect for cool-season replanting.** Cool nights + warm days = ideal for these herbs.

### [[Cilantro]]
- **Sow:** Early to mid-September
- **Depth:** ¼ inch
- **Spacing:** Thin to 6 inches
- **Germination:** 7–14 days
- **Harvest window:** October–November before frost
- **Preference:** Much prefers cool weather to spring/summer
- **Notes:** This is actually better crop than spring

### [[Dill]]
- **Sow:** Early to mid-September
- **Depth:** ¼ inch
- **Spacing:** Thin to 12 inches
- **Germination:** 7–10 days
- **Harvest:** October–November
- **Fall advantage:** Slower bolting than summer

### [[Chamomile]]
- **Option 1:** Direct sow (may overwinter, flower next year)
- **Option 2:** Let existing plants self-seed
- **Notes:** Often self-seeds prolifically; accept volunteers
- **Harvest:** If flowering by November, use sparingly

### [[Dandelion]] (if growing)
- **Sow:** Early September
- **Notes:** Overwinters; you'll harvest spring
- **Advantage:** Spring greens from fall planting

---

## 🌾 Preservation Begins

**Summer is peak; time to preserve for winter.**

### Drying Herbs

**Best herbs to dry now:**
- **Oregano, Marjoram, Thyme:** Peak potency
- **Sage:** Excellent dried
- **Basil:** Dry before frost kills it
- **Peppermint:** Last vibrant leaves

**Drying process:**
1. Cut stems (morning after dew dries)
2. Bundle with twine (6–10 stems per bundle)
3. Hang upside down in cool, dark, airy place
4. 2–3 weeks drying typically
5. Leaves should crumble when dry
6. Store in sealed glass jars, cool dark place

### Making Tinctures

**Perfect time to start long-maceration tinctures:**
- Tincture herbs now
- 4–6 week soak
- Ready by November
- Perfect for winter immune season

**See [[Tincture Guide]] for full process**

### Making Syrups & Preparations

**Harvest heavily for:**
- Syrups (concentrated remedies)
- Salves (dried herb infusions)
- Herbal blends for winter

---

## 🌳 Summer Plants Declining

### Annuals Winding Down
- [[Basil]]: Still productive but frost warning coming
  - Harvest heavily now
  - Don't allow to set seed (unless want volunteers)
  - Dry for winter use
  - Make pesto for freezing

- [[Dill]]: Earlier sowings flowering, going to seed
  - Allow some to go to seed (volunteers next year funny
  - Harvest green dill for fresh use
  - Harvest seed clusters for drying

- [[Anise]]: Finishing for season
  - Let complete seed cycle
  - Harvest seed for teas, storage
  - Plant won't overwinter

### Tender Perennials Under Stress
- [[Peppermint]], [[Lemon Mint]]: Still productive but slowing
- Continue harvesting but noting reduced vigor

---

## 📋 September Task Checklist

### Fall Planting
- [ ] Sow cilantro (early-mid September)
- [ ] Sow dill (early-mid September)
- [ ] Consider chamomile/dandelion sowing
- [ ] Keep new sowings moist (germination 7–14 days)

### Preservation
- [ ] Dry oregano, marjoram, thyme
- [ ] Dry basil (heavily before frost)
- [ ] Make tinctures using peak-potency herbs
- [ ] Make syrups (especially respiratory, immune)
- [ ] Make harissa or dried herb blends

### Garden Maintenance
- [ ] Heavy harvesting of summer herbs
- [ ] Note which herbs performed well (record for next year)
- [ ] Begin reducing water (cooling season)
- [ ] Deadhead flowers (unless saving seed)
- [ ] Weed one final time

### Observation
- [ ] First frost date awareness (Oct 25–Nov 5 Zone 7B)
- [ ] Watch forecast mid-September onward
- [ ] Note plant health/pest activity
- [ ] Photo document changes

---

## 🌡️ Frost Awareness

### Not Yet!
- September frost is rare but possible (first week)
- Watch forecast September 1–10
- October 20+ is when to really vigilant

### Start Planning
- Which plants to protect? (Tender annuals)
- Which to let freeze? (Basil, tender annuals serving their function)
- Frost cloth available? (Have on hand by October)

---

## 🌿 Harvest Notes

### Peak Harvest for Preservation
**Now is premium time for:**
- Oregano (peak essential oils)
- Marjoram (strongest flavor)
- Sage (concentrated compounds)
- Basil (before frost kills it)

**Timing:**
- Harvest in morning (after dew dries, before heat)
- When plants in flower (peak potency)
- Top ⅓ of growth

### What to Preserve
- Dried herbs (1–2 year storage)
- Tinctures (5–10 year storage)
- Syrups (refrigerated, 3–4 months)
- Oils (infused, 6–12 months)

---

## 🚰 Watering Changes

### Reduce Frequency
- Cooling season = less evaporation
- Rainfall typically increases (September can surprise)
- Check soil before watering
- Top inch dry = water time

### Focus on New Sowings
- Fall-planted cilantro + dill need consistent moisture until germinated
- Established perennials need less

---

## 💡 September Tips

| Tip | Why |
|---|---|
| **Preserve heavily** | Winter months need dried herbs |
| **Harvest basil aggressively** | Frost will end it; harvest while thriving |
| **Plant cilantro/dill now** | Fall crop superior to spring |
| **Watch forecast** | Early frost possible (rare but watch) |
| **Make tinctures** | Ready by December for cold/immune season |
| **Keep records** | Note what worked, what didn't |
| **Enjoy peak flavor** | September harvests are peak potency |

---

## 🌸 What's Coming October+

**October onward:**
- Frost increasingly likely (Oct 20 watch date)
- Basil + tender annuals killed by first frost
- Cool-season herbs (cilantro, dill) thrive
- Perennials begin preparing dormancy
- Serious preservation continues
- Garden activity reducing (preparing for dormancy)

---

## 📊 From Garden to Shelf

**Preservation pathway this month:**

```
Fresh herb → Bundle → Hang dry → Storable → Winter use
          ↓
        Tincture → 4-6 week → Bottled → 5-10 year shelf life
              ↓
            Syrup → Honey + heat → Cool → 3-4 month fridge
                          ↓
                      Salve → Oil + wax → Bottled → 6-12 months
```

---

## 🔗 Related Pages

**Monthly Planning:**
- [[August]] (if created) - Summer peak continues
- [[October]] (if created) - Frost arrives
- [[Month-by-Month Calendar (Zone 7B)]] - Full overview

**Harvesting & Preservation:**
- [[Tea Infusion Guide]] - Fresh + dried use
- [[Tincture Guide]] - Alcohol extraction
- [[Salve Guide]] - Oil + beeswax
- [[Syrup Guide]] - Honey preserved

**Reference:**
- [[Medicinal Actions Index]] - What to preserve for what purpose
- [[Master Plant Index]] - Herb reference

---

**September Summary:** Cool season restarts! Plant cilantro + dill for fall harvest. Heavy preservation time—dry, tincture, make syrups from peak plants. Garden beginning wind-down. Preparation for dormancy season starting.

title: May
html:
🌞 May Planting Guide (Zone 7B)

Season: Spring to Summer Transition
Average Temps: 65–75°F (consistently warm)
Days of Light: Long (14–16 hours)
Frost Risk: NONE (safe to plant anything)
Soil: Warm (70°F+), perfect for all herbs

This Month's Role

May is warm-season planting + establishment month. Plant basil and other heat-loving herbs. Succession sow cool-season herbs one final time. Begin regular harvesting. Watch first flowers appear.

🌿 Plant Warm-Season Herbs (Finally!)

May is the month for [[Basil]] and true summer herbs.

[[Thai Basil]]

  • Plant: Early May when soil is warm
  • Spacing: 12–18 inches apart
  • Sun: Full sun preferred
  • Water: Regular (doesn't tolerate dryness)
  • Pinch: Pinch tops regularly to encourage bushiness
  • Flowers: Let some flower (attractive, edible)
  • Harvest: August–September peak

[[Italian Basil]]

  • Plant: Early May when soil warm
  • Spacing: 12–18 inches apart
  • Culinary: Classic pesto basil
  • Water: Regular, consistent
  • Pinch: Top regularly for branching
  • Varieties: "Genovese" classic, "Lettuce Leaf" large leaves

[[Purple Basil]]

  • Plant: Early May
  • Spacing: 12–18 inches apart
  • Visual: Beautiful purple foliage/flowers
  • Taste: Slightly spicier than green
  • Culinary: Interesting in salads, preserved
  • Pinch: For bushiness

General Basil Tips

  • All prefer: Warm soil (65–70°F minimum)
  • All need: Full sun (6+ hours)
  • All want: Regular water (don't dry out)
  • All should: Be pinched regularly (pinch = bushier = more productive)

🌼 Succession Planting (Final Rounds)

[[Cilantro]] - Last Good Sowing

  • Sow: Early May (last good sowing before heat)
  • Spacing: Thin to 6 inches
  • Germination: 5–10 days
  • Notes: Bolts in summer heat; sow early or wait for August resurgence
  • Harvest: May–June before bolting

[[Dill]] - Second/Third Sowing

  • Sow: Every 2–3 weeks through May
  • Notes: Multiple sowings = continuous supply
  • Germination: 7–10 days
  • Harvest: Staggered July–September

[[Chamomile]] - Thin & Monitor

  • If planted March: Thin seedlings to 12–18 inch spacing
  • Self-seeders: Now obvious; thin or accept volunteers
  • Monitor: Remove excess if overwhelming neighboring plants

🌾 Garden Maintenance

Daily Checks

  • [ ] Water new plantings (soil should stay moist, not soggy)
  • [ ] Watch for pests (early intervention best)
  • [ ] Note which plants thriving, which struggling
  • [ ] Enjoy the garden!

Weekly Tasks

  • [ ] Pinch basil tops (encourage bushiness)
  • [ ] Weed before they set seed
  • [ ] Deadhead spent flowers (unless saving seed)
  • [ ] Check mulch (refresh if washing away)
  • [ ] Scout for disease/pests

Start Harvesting!

  • [[Peppermint]] - Can harvest heavily now
  • [[Lemon Balm]] - Pinch tips regularly (tasty + pruning)
  • [[Oregano]], [[Marjoram]] - Now at harvestable size
  • [[Thyme]], [[Sage]] - Can begin light harvest
  • Early Spring-sown direct crops (Cilantro, Chamomile) - Ready to harvest

🌱 First Flowers Appearing

Watch for first blooming:

Early May flowers:

  • [[Bergamot]] not yet (late June typical)
  • [[Lavender]] starting to bloom
  • [[Echinacea]] growing tall, buds forming

Late May/Early June transition:

  • Direct-sown herbs (Dill, Fennel, Anise) starting to flower if sown in April

Expected by June:

  • Peak of early spring perennials
  • Annual herbs approaching full height

🎯 End-of-Month Check

By May 31, your garden should:

  • ✓ All herbs transplanted and establishing
  • ✓ Basil planted and growing vigorously
  • ✓ Some perennials flowering (Lavender, early bloomers)
  • ✓ Ready for serious harvesting June onward
  • ✓ Watering system running smoothly
  • ✓ Basic pests managed or absent
  • ✓ First teas/fresh harvests possible

📋 May Task Checklist

Planting

  • [ ] Plant all basil varieties (early May)
  • [ ] Succession sow: Cilantro (final good sowing)
  • [ ] Sow second round: Dill
  • [ ] Thin seedlings if crowded

General Care

  • [ ] Water consistently (daily check)
  • [ ] Pinch basil tops (encourage bushy growth)
  • [ ] Monitor for pests (daily walk-through)
  • [ ] Weed continuously
  • [ ] Deadhead spent flowers
  • [ ] Stake tall plants if needed

Harvesting Begins

  • [ ] Harvest Peppermint (can go heavy)
  • [ ] Pinch Lemon Balm regularly
  • [ ] Light harvests: Oregano, Marjoram, Thyme, Sage
  • [ ] Harvest cilantro before bolting
  • [ ] If Chamomile flowering: Harvest flowers

Observation

  • [ ] Track which plants thriving
  • [ ] Note pest problems
  • [ ] Watch for disease
  • [ ] Photo document progress
  • [ ] Make notes for next year

🌿 Harvesting Guidelines This Month

Light Harvesting (Perennials first year)

  • Take top ¼–⅓ of growth
  • Leave structure for ongoing vigor
  • Stimulates bushiness
  • Don't strip completely

Aggressive Harvesting (Mints)

  • Peppermint wants heavy harvesting
  • Cut down to 6–12 inches
  • Regrows quickly
  • Multiple harvests per summer possible

What NOT to harvest yet

  • First-year Echinacea, Bergamot (let establish)
  • Lovage (small plants; let grow)
  • Rosemary (if young; wait until established)

🚰 Watering in May

Warm Month = More Water Needed

  • Daily check during dry weather
  • Every 2–3 days typical watering schedule
  • Basil especially: Consistent moisture needed
  • Drought-tolerant (Thyme, Oregano): Less frequent

Signs of Water Stress

  • Under-watering: Wilting despite humid air, slow growth
  • Over-watering: Yellowing, mushy stems, fungal smells

Mulch Benefit

  • 2–3 inches mulch: Conserves moisture 30–50%
  • Keeps soil cooler (important for basil)
  • Suppresses weeds
  • Breaks down into soil organic matter

💡 May Tips

Tip Why
Plant basil late May if unsure Never too late in May for basil
Pinch basil relentlessly Pinching = bushier, more productive
Harvest regularly Growth responds to harvest
Water deeply Encourages deep root development
Mulch heavily Reduces watering, suppresses weeds
Start drying herbs Peak freshness, preserve for winter
Keep enjoying! This is why you grew them

🌸 What's Coming June+

June onward:

  • Peak bloom for perennials
  • Hot weather arrives
  • Harvesting shifts to preservation (drying, tinctures)
  • Some cool-season herbs struggle (heat stress)
  • Basil thrives (true warm-season lover)
  • Watering increases (heat demand)

🔗 Related Pages

Monthly Planning:

  • [[April]] - Spring planting
  • [[June]] (when ready) - Peak harvest season
  • [[Month-by-Month Calendar (Zone 7B)]] - Full overview

Harvesting & Preservation:

  • [[Tea Infusion Guide]] - Fresh tea making
  • [[Tincture Guide]] - Alcohol extraction
  • [[Salve Guide]] - Oil + beeswax preparations

Garden Care:

  • [[Water Needs Map]] - By-herb water requirements
  • [[Height Map]] - Spacing confirmation

May Summary: Warm season begins! Plant basil, succession sow final cool-season crops, begin harvesting seriously. Garden is productive and beautiful. This is what you've been building toward!

title: March
html:
🌱 March Planting Guide (Zone 7B)

Season: Spring Awakening
Average Temps: 45–55°F (still cool)
Days of Light: Growing (10–12 hours)
Frost Risk: Still high (last frost mid-April)
Soil: Warming, workable

This Month's Role

March is peak indoor seed starting month. You'll start the majority of herb transplants indoors for May planting. You'll also direct sow cold-hardy herbs outdoors.

🌱 Indoor Seed Starting (4 weeks before last frost = April 15)

Start These Herbs Indoors Now

Perennials (you'll start once, harvest for years):

  • [[Bergamot]]
  • [[Catnip]]
  • [[Echinacea]]
  • [[Hyssop]]
  • [[Lemon Balm]]
  • [[Lovage]]
  • [[Marjoram]]
  • [[Mountain Mint]]
  • [[Peppermint]]
  • [[Lemon Mint]]
  • [[Sage]]
  • [[Thyme]]
  • [[Oregano]]

Annual Basils:

  • [[Thai Basil]]
  • [[Italian Basil]]
  • [[Purple Basil]]

Seed Starting Process

Equipment:

  • Seed-starting mix (sterile, peat or coco-based)
  • Seed trays, cell packs, or small pots (drainage crucial)
  • Grow lights (LED, 14–16 inches above seedlings)
  • Heat mat (65–70°F optimal for most)
  • Watering can with fine rose (sprinkle not spray)
  • Labels + permanent marker

Steps:

  1. Fill containers with moist seed-starting mix (pre-wet it)

  2. Place seeds:

    • Follow packet directions (some need light, cold treatment, etc.)
    • Most herbs: Light sprinkle of soil on top OR surface-sow (don't bury deep)
    • Pat soil gently

  3. Mist gently with water (don't wash seeds away)

  4. Cover with plastic dome or humidity mat (keeps moist during germination)

  5. Provide warmth: Heat mat underneath improves germination by 30%

  6. Provide light: Once sprouted (usually 5–14 days), remove cover + provide 14–16 hours light daily

  7. Keep moist: Top inch should stay moist but not waterlogged

Germination timeline:

  • Fast: Basilsgarlic, Marjoram (5–7 days)
  • Medium: Lemon Balm, Oregano, Thyme (7–14 days)
  • Slow: Echinacea, Hyssop (14–21 days)

🌿 Direct Sow Outdoors (Cold-Hardy)

These herbs tolerate frost and can be sown directly in garden as soil becomes workable.

[[Caraway]]

  • Sow: Once soil is workable (as soon as you can dig without it clumping)
  • Depth: Scatter on surface, press gently, light soil covering
  • Spacing: Thin seedlings to 12 inches apart later
  • Notes: Biennial; will flower year 2
  • Germination: 2–3 weeks typically

[[Chamomile]]

  • Sow: Direct sow as soon as soil workable
  • Depth: Surface sow (needs light to germinate) OR very light soil covering
  • Spacing: Thin to 12–18 inches apart
  • Notes: Self-sows readily; you may see volunteers for years
  • Germination: 7–14 days

[[Cilantro]]

  • Sow: As soon as soil workable
  • Depth: ¼ inch
  • Spacing: Thin to 6 inches apart
  • Notes: Cool-season herb; prefers spring/fall not summer
  • Succession sow: Every 2 weeks through mid-April for continuous harvest
  • Germination: 7–14 days

[[Dandelion]]

  • Sow: Direct in garden
  • Depth: Surface light covering
  • Spacing: Eventually 12–18 inches
  • Notes: Not traditional but medicinal + edible
  • Germination: 7–14 days

🌾 Garden Prep Tasks

Soil Preparation

  • [ ] Remove winter mulch from perennials (as they emerge)
  • [ ] Loosen/till soil (if not in permanent beds)
  • [ ] Amend with compost (1–2 inches worked in)
  • [ ] Check drainage (water puddles = problem area)
  • [ ] Adjust pH if needed (soil test kit available)

Bed Planning

  • [ ] Mark planting zones (Mediterranean, Digestive, Pollinator)
  • [ ] Plan spacing (consult [[Height Map]], [[Water Needs Map]])
  • [ ] Gather supplies: stakes, markers, measurements

Tool Maintenance

  • [ ] Sharpen pruners, hoes, spades
  • [ ] Check for rust (clean/oil)
  • [ ] Gather watering cans, soaker hoses

🌿 Hardening Off Seedlings

If you started seeds indoors in February:

Hardening off = gradually acclimating indoor seedlings to outdoor conditions.

Week 1 (late March):

  • Day 1–2: Place in shaded outdoor location (sheltered) for 1–2 hours
  • Day 3–4: Same location, 2–3 hours
  • Day 5–7: Increase to 4–6 hours, add partial sun

Week 2 (early April):

  • Full days outdoors in partial shade
  • Bring in before temps drop below 50°F at night

Week 3 (mid-April):

  • Gradually increase sun exposure (morning sun first)
  • Keep bringing in if frost threatening

By late April: Ready for transplanting after last frost

📋 March Task Checklist

Indoor Seed Starting

  • [ ] Start all listed perennials indoors
  • [ ] Start basil varieties indoors
  • [ ] Set up lights + heat mats
  • [ ] Water daily (keep moist, not waterlogged)
  • [ ] Monitor for germination

Outdoor Direct Sowing

  • [ ] Prepare beds for direct sowing
  • [ ] Direct sow: Caraway, Chamomile, Cilantro, Dandelion
  • [ ] Water gently after sowing
  • [ ] Mark crops with labels
  • [ ] Plan succession sowing (cilantro every 2 weeks)

Garden Maintenance

  • [ ] Prune existing perennials (hard prune, remove deadwood)
  • [ ] Remove winter mulch from perennials
  • [ ] Check for winter damage
  • [ ] Harden off February seedlings (if you have them)

Planning & Prep

  • [ ] Finalize April planting plan
  • [ ] Order any missing transplants from nurseries
  • [ ] Scout local nurseries (what's available)

🌡️ Temperature Monitoring

Still Frost Risk!

  • Nighttime temps still dropping to freezing
  • Last frost date: April 5–15 (mid-month typical)
  • Watch weather forecast
  • Be ready to protect new seedlings

Window for Outdoor Work

  • Daytime: Workable (getting warmer)
  • Nighttime: Still cold (protect sensitive plants)
  • Soil: Workable but still cool

🚰 Watering Notes

Indoor Seedlings

  • Keep soil consistently moist (not waterlogged)
  • Daily check needed (small pots dry quickly)
  • Mist gently or water from below
  • Reduce water if fungal issues appear (damping off)

Direct-Sown Seeds

  • Keep soil moist until germinated (7–14 days typically)
  • After germination: Reduce to letting top inch dry slightly
  • Don't over-water (causes rot)

Perennials Breaking Dormancy

  • Provide water as they emerge
  • Natural rainfall usually sufficient
  • Only water if extended dry period

🌿 Observation Notes

Track what's happening:

Germination status (March 15): 
- Basilsfast sprouting
- Echinacea: No sprouts yet (expected)
- Thyme: Slow but emerging

Direct-sown updates:
- Cilantro: _______________
- Chamomile: ______________

Outdoor observations:
- Perennials: Breaking dormancy? ______________
- Frost dates: Any late freezes? ______________
- Soil: Workable yet? ______________

Problems/successes:
- Seedlings stretching? ______________
- Fungal issues? ______________
- Good germination? ______________

💡 March Tips

Tip Reason
Start seeds early Long grow cycle = transplants ready when frost passes
Don't rush outdoor planting Frost still likely; seeds germinate faster in warming April
Harden off gradually Acclimate seedlings to sun/wind slowly
Direct sow multiple times Cilantro every 2 weeks = continuous supply
Keep records Track seed viability for next year
Clean tools Sanitize to prevent disease spread

🔗 Related Pages

Monthly Planning:

  • [[February]] - Early starts (Lavender, Rosemary)
  • [[April]] - Transplanting & April planting
  • [[May]] - Final tender herbs, warm-soil planting

Reference:

  • [[Month-by-Month Calendar (Zone 7B)]] - Full overview
  • [[Master Plant Index]] - Plant details
  • [[Height Map]], [[Water Needs Map]] - Planning

March Summary: Busiest seed-starting month. Start perennials + basils indoors. Direct sow cold-hardy seeds outdoors. Prepare beds. March is when your garden year truly begins!
raw:
# 🌱 March Planting Guide (Zone 7B)

**Season:** Spring Awakening  
**Average Temps:** 45–55°F (still cool)  
**Days of Light:** Growing (10–12 hours)  
**Frost Risk:** Still high (last frost mid-April)  
**Soil:** Warming, workable

---

## This Month's Role

March is **peak indoor seed starting month**. You'll start the majority of herb transplants indoors for May planting. You'll also direct sow cold-hardy herbs outdoors.

---

## 🌱 Indoor Seed Starting (4 weeks before last frost = April 15)

### Start These Herbs Indoors Now

**Perennials (you'll start once, harvest for years):**

- [[Bergamot]]
- [[Catnip]]
- [[Echinacea]]
- [[Hyssop]]
- [[Lemon Balm]]
- [[Lovage]]
- [[Marjoram]]
- [[Mountain Mint]]
- [[Peppermint]]
- [[Lemon Mint]]
- [[Sage]]
- [[Thyme]]
- [[Oregano]]

**Annual Basils:**
- [[Thai Basil]]
- [[Italian Basil]]
- [[Purple Basil]]

### Seed Starting Process

**Equipment:**
- Seed-starting mix (sterile, peat or coco-based)
- Seed trays, cell packs, or small pots (drainage crucial)
- Grow lights (LED, 14–16 inches above seedlings)
- Heat mat (65–70°F optimal for most)
- Watering can with fine rose (sprinkle not spray)
- Labels + permanent marker

**Steps:**

1. **Fill containers** with moist seed-starting mix (pre-wet it)

2. **Place seeds:**
   - Follow packet directions (some need light, cold treatment, etc.)
   - Most herbs: Light sprinkle of soil on top OR surface-sow (don't bury deep)
   - Pat soil gently

3. **Mist gently** with water (don't wash seeds away)

4. **Cover** with plastic dome or humidity mat (keeps moist during germination)

5. **Provide warmth:** Heat mat underneath improves germination by 30%

6. **Provide light:** Once sprouted (usually 5–14 days), remove cover + provide 14–16 hours light daily

7. **Keep moist:** Top inch should stay moist but not waterlogged

**Germination timeline:**
- Fast: Basilsgarlic, Marjoram (5–7 days)
- Medium: Lemon Balm, Oregano, Thyme (7–14 days)
- Slow: Echinacea, Hyssop (14–21 days)

---

## 🌿 Direct Sow Outdoors (Cold-Hardy)

**These herbs tolerate frost and can be sown directly in garden as soil becomes workable.**

### [[Caraway]]
- **Sow:** Once soil is workable (as soon as you can dig without it clumping)
- **Depth:** Scatter on surface, press gently, light soil covering
- **Spacing:** Thin seedlings to 12 inches apart later
- **Notes:** Biennial; will flower year 2
- **Germination:** 2–3 weeks typically

### [[Chamomile]]
- **Sow:** Direct sow as soon as soil workable
- **Depth:** Surface sow (needs light to germinate) OR very light soil covering
- **Spacing:** Thin to 12–18 inches apart
- **Notes:** Self-sows readily; you may see volunteers for years
- **Germination:** 7–14 days

### [[Cilantro]]
- **Sow:** As soon as soil workable
- **Depth:** ¼ inch
- **Spacing:** Thin to 6 inches apart
- **Notes:** Cool-season herb; prefers spring/fall not summer
- **Succession sow:** Every 2 weeks through mid-April for continuous harvest
- **Germination:** 7–14 days

### [[Dandelion]]
- **Sow:** Direct in garden
- **Depth:** Surface light covering
- **Spacing:** Eventually 12–18 inches
- **Notes:** Not traditional but medicinal + edible
- **Germination:** 7–14 days

---

## 🌾 Garden Prep Tasks

### Soil Preparation
- [ ] Remove winter mulch from perennials (as they emerge)
- [ ] Loosen/till soil (if not in permanent beds)
- [ ] Amend with compost (1–2 inches worked in)
- [ ] Check drainage (water puddles = problem area)
- [ ] Adjust pH if needed (soil test kit available)

### Bed Planning
- [ ] Mark planting zones (Mediterranean, Digestive, Pollinator)
- [ ] Plan spacing (consult [[Height Map]], [[Water Needs Map]])
- [ ] Gather supplies: stakes, markers, measurements

### Tool Maintenance
- [ ] Sharpen pruners, hoes, spades
- [ ] Check for rust (clean/oil)
- [ ] Gather watering cans, soaker hoses

---

## 🌿 Hardening Off Seedlings

**If you started seeds indoors in February:**

**Hardening off** = gradually acclimating indoor seedlings to outdoor conditions.

**Week 1 (late March):**
- Day 1–2: Place in shaded outdoor location (sheltered) for 1–2 hours
- Day 3–4: Same location, 2–3 hours
- Day 5–7: Increase to 4–6 hours, add partial sun

**Week 2 (early April):**
- Full days outdoors in partial shade
- Bring in before temps drop below 50°F at night

**Week 3 (mid-April):**
- Gradually increase sun exposure (morning sun first)
- Keep bringing in if frost threatening

**By late April:** Ready for transplanting after last frost

---

## 📋 March Task Checklist

### Indoor Seed Starting
- [ ] Start all listed perennials indoors
- [ ] Start basil varieties indoors
- [ ] Set up lights + heat mats
- [ ] Water daily (keep moist, not waterlogged)
- [ ] Monitor for germination

### Outdoor Direct Sowing
- [ ] Prepare beds for direct sowing
- [ ] Direct sow: Caraway, Chamomile, Cilantro, Dandelion
- [ ] Water gently after sowing
- [ ] Mark crops with labels
- [ ] Plan succession sowing (cilantro every 2 weeks)

### Garden Maintenance
- [ ] Prune existing perennials (hard prune, remove deadwood)
- [ ] Remove winter mulch from perennials
- [ ] Check for winter damage
- [ ] Harden off February seedlings (if you have them)

### Planning & Prep
- [ ] Finalize April planting plan
- [ ] Order any missing transplants from nurseries
- [ ] Scout local nurseries (what's available)

---

## 🌡️ Temperature Monitoring

### Still Frost Risk!
- Nighttime temps still dropping to freezing
- Last frost date: April 5–15 (mid-month typical)
- Watch weather forecast
- Be ready to protect new seedlings

### Window for Outdoor Work
- Daytime: Workable (getting warmer)
- Nighttime: Still cold (protect sensitive plants)
- Soil: Workable but still cool

---

## 🚰 Watering Notes

### Indoor Seedlings
- Keep soil consistently moist (not waterlogged)
- Daily check needed (small pots dry quickly)
- Mist gently or water from below
- Reduce water if fungal issues appear (damping off)

### Direct-Sown Seeds
- Keep soil moist until germinated (7–14 days typically)
- After germination: Reduce to letting top inch dry slightly
- Don't over-water (causes rot)

### Perennials Breaking Dormancy
- Provide water as they emerge
- Natural rainfall usually sufficient
- Only water if extended dry period

---

## 🌿 Observation Notes

**Track what's happening:**

```
Germination status (March 15): 
- Basilsfast sprouting
- Echinacea: No sprouts yet (expected)
- Thyme: Slow but emerging

Direct-sown updates:
- Cilantro: _______________
- Chamomile: ______________

Outdoor observations:
- Perennials: Breaking dormancy? ______________
- Frost dates: Any late freezes? ______________
- Soil: Workable yet? ______________

Problems/successes:
- Seedlings stretching? ______________
- Fungal issues? ______________
- Good germination? ______________
```

---

## 💡 March Tips

| Tip | Reason |
|---|---|
| **Start seeds early** | Long grow cycle = transplants ready when frost passes |
| **Don't rush outdoor planting** | Frost still likely; seeds germinate faster in warming April |
| **Harden off gradually** | Acclimate seedlings to sun/wind slowly |
| **Direct sow multiple times** | Cilantro every 2 weeks = continuous supply |
| **Keep records** | Track seed viability for next year |
| **Clean tools** | Sanitize to prevent disease spread |

---

## 🔗 Related Pages

**Monthly Planning:**
- [[February]] - Early starts (Lavender, Rosemary)
- [[April]] - Transplanting & April planting
- [[May]] - Final tender herbs, warm-soil planting

**Reference:**
- [[Month-by-Month Calendar (Zone 7B)]] - Full overview
- [[Master Plant Index]] - Plant details
- [[Height Map]], [[Water Needs Map]] - Planning

---

**March Summary:** Busiest seed-starting month. Start perennials + basils indoors. Direct sow cold-hardy seeds outdoors. Prepare beds. March is when your garden year truly begins!

title: February
html:
🌸 February Planting Guide (Zone 7B)

Season: Early Spring Preparation
Average Low: 35–45°F
Days of Light: Growing (8–10 hours)
Frost Risk: Still possible; protect tender starts

This Month's Role

February is preparation + early starts. Outdoor planting is limited by cold/frost risk, but indoor seed starting begins for spring transplants.

🌱 Indoor Seed Starting

Start Under Lights (4–6 weeks before transplant)

[[Lavender]]

  • Why now? Slow germinator, needs longest cycle
  • Medium: Seed starting mix, moist but not soggy
  • Light: 14–16 hours daily under grow lights
  • Heat: 65–70°F optimal (heating mat helpful)
  • Germination: 14–21 days typical
  • Transplant outdoors: Mid-May (after last frost)
  • Notes: Lavender seed sometimes needs stratification (cold period); soak seeds in cool water 24 hours before planting

[[Rosemary]]

  • Why now? Very slow germinator, needs 6–8 weeks
  • Medium: Seed starting mix, keep consistently moist
  • Light: 14–16 hours daily
  • Heat: 70–75°F (heating mat recommended)
  • Germination: 21–30 days (sometimes longer)
  • Transplant: June (full establishment after warmth)
  • Notes: Rosemary germinates slowly but reliably if warm; patience needed

🔧 Preparation Tasks

Clean & Organize Seed Arsenal

  • [ ] Inventory seed: Check germination dates
  • [ ] Discard old seeds (over 2–3 years)
  • [ ] Order replacements (supply sourcing)
  • [ ] Organize by month for easy reference

Start Seeds Under Lights

  • [ ] Set up grow light system (if not already established)
  • [ ] Gather seed starting supplies:
    • Seed starting mix (sterile, peat/coco-based)
    • Seed trays or small pots
    • Grow lights (LED ideal)
    • Heat mat (optional but helpful)
    • Watering can with fine spray

Prepare Garden Beds (March-spring)

  • [ ] Check drainage (dig test hole, fill with water)
  • [ ] Note problem areas (wet, compacted, poor soil)
  • [ ] Plan amendments (perlite for drainage, compost)
  • [ ] Order supplies as needed

Get Starts Scouting

  • [ ] Research local nurseries (their selection, hours)
  • [ ] Check what's available (some plants hard to find)
  • [ ] Order plants in advance (rare varieties may need pre-order)
  • [ ] Plan budget (how many transplants to purchase vs. grow from seed?)

🌿 Garden Observations

Check Existing Perennials

  • [ ] Are they showing winter damage? (Dead stems, brown patches)
  • [ ] Do they need mulch adjustment? (Exposed roots = frost damage risk)
  • [ ] Beginning to break dormancy? (Any green shoots emerging?)

Monitor Outdoor Conditions

  • [ ] Note frost dates (watch local forecast)
  • [ ] Observe rainfall (adequate or droughty winter?)
  • [ ] Check snow pack (moisture reserve for spring)

🌾 Outdoor Activities (Few options in Zone 7B)

Only if VERY early spring (Feb 26+) + temps 60°F+

  • Direct sow [[Caraway]] (rare early option)
  • Direct sow [[Cilantro]] (if conditions align)
  • Plant perennial starts (if nursery open + temps allow)

More likely: Wait for March

📋 Seed Starting Checklists

Daily Tasks (Once lights started)

  • [ ] Check soil moisture (keep moist, don't waterlog)
  • [ ] Monitor temperature (maintain 65–75°F)
  • [ ] Watch for first seedlings (check daily after germination day-range passes)
  • [ ] Provide light 14–16 hours once sprouted

Weekly Tasks

  • [ ] Inspect for stretching (seedlings reaching for light = light too far)
  • [ ] Rotate trays (ensures even light)
  • [ ] Check for mold/fungal issues
  • [ ] Make notes on germination rates (track for posterity)

🎯 Seed-Starting Success Tips

Tip Why How
Warmth Seeds germinate best warm Use heat mat: 70–75°F for most herbs
Moisture Too dry = no germination; Too wet = rot Water from below (tray method) or light misting
Light after sprouting Seedlings need light immediately Lights on day of sprouting; within 1–2 days if you miss
Proper distance Too far = stretching; Too close = burning Lights 2–3 inches above seedlings, adjust as they grow
Air circulation Prevents fungal issues Small fan near (but not directly on) seedlings
Sterile medium Prevents damp-off fungus Use seed-starting mix, not garden soil

📊 Herb Start Timing (from February seeds)

Herb Start Germination Transplant Out Mature
Lavender Early Feb 14–21 days Mid-May July–August
Rosemary Early Feb 21–30 days June August (year 1)
Future Months See March for majority of starts — — —

💡 Notes & Observations

Use this space to track:

  • Were seeds viable? (germination rates)
  • Any problems? (fungal, stretching, light)
  • Seedling vigor (color, growth rate)
  • What will you do differently next year?
Lavender start date: ____________
Rosemary start date: ____________
Temperature maintained: ____°F
Light source: ___________________
Germination observed: ____________
Notes: __________________________

🔗 Related Pages

Monthly Planning:

  • [[March]] - Main growing season starts
  • [[Month-by-Month Calendar (Zone 7B)]] - Overview

Seed Information:

  • [[Master Plant Index]]

Preparation Methods:

  • Growing from seed requires understanding [[Tea Infusion Guide]] (once mature for harvest)

February Summary: Mostly indoor prep work. Start Lavender and Rosemary from seed (long cycle). Prepare tools, supplies, and garden beds mentally for spring rush coming March–May.

### [[Lavender]]

### [[Rosemary]]

## 📋 Seed Starting Checklists

### Daily Tasks (Once lights started)

## 🎯 Seed-Starting Success Tips

| Tip | Why | How |
|---|---|---|
| **Warmth** | Seeds germinate best warm | Use heat mat: 70–75°F for most herbs |

title: April
html:
🌿 April Planting Guide (Zone 7B)

Season: Spring in Full Force
Average Temps: 55–65°F (warming rapidly)
Days of Light: Long (12–14 hours)
Frost Risk: LAST FROST April 5–15; diminishing rapidly
Soil: Warm, workable, ideal for planting

This Month's Role

April is transplant month. Move indoor seedlings (started Feb–March) to the garden after last frost. Direct sow warm-weather herbs. This is peak planting action.

📅 Timing: Last Frost

Zone 7B Last Frost Date: April 5–15 (mid-month typical)

Watch Weather!

  • Check local forecast mid-April
  • Wait until nighttime temps consistently above 50°F
  • Cold snap coming? Hold off planting tender herbs
  • Clear week predicted? Plant day of last frost or after

🌱 Transplant Time (After Last Frost)

Plants to Transplant (from indoor starts)

Transplant around April 15–20 (once last frost danger passes):

Move to garden:

  • [[Bergamot]]
  • [[Catnip]]
  • [[Echinacea]]
  • [[Hyssop]]
  • [[Lemon Balm]]
  • [[Lovage]]
  • [[Marjoram]]
  • [[Mountain Mint]]
  • [[Peppermint]]
  • [[Lemon Mint]]
  • [[Sage]]
  • [[Thyme]]
  • [[Oregano]]
  • [[Thai Basil]]
  • [[Italian Basil]]
  • [[Purple Basil]]

Plus February-started perennials:

  • [[Lavender]] (already hardened off, ready to transplant)
  • [[Rosemary]] (tender; may wait for mid-May warmth)

Transplanting Process

Before transplanting:

  1. Harden off seedlings (acclimatize to sun/wind gradually)

    • Week of April 5–12: Part shade, sheltered
    • Week of April 12–19: Increasing sun exposure

  2. Check seedling condition:

    • Healthy green leaves
    • No fungal issues
    • Well-developed roots (visible at soil surface okay)
    • 4–6 weeks old typically (ready)

Transplanting:

  1. Prepare planting holes slightly larger than root ball
  2. Gently remove seedling from tray (squeeze sides gently)
  3. Loosen roots gently with fingers (break up root ball slightly)
  4. Plant at same depth (bury to first leaves, not deeper)
  5. Backfill with garden soil
  6. Water gently immediately after transplanting
  7. Mulch 1–2 inches around (away from stem)

Spacing (consult design guides):

  • See [[Height Map]] for vertical spacing front-to-back
  • See [[Water Needs Map]] for grouping by water needs
  • Spacing: 12"–30" depending on mature size (check [[Master Plant Index]])

🌼 Direct Sow Warm-Weather Herbs

Once soil is warm (April 15+) and frost danger passes:

[[Anise]]

  • Sow: Mid-late April (wait for soil warmth)
  • Depth: ¼–½ inch
  • Spacing: Thin to 12 inches apart
  • Germination: 14–21 days
  • Notes: Annual; plant once per year
  • Harvest: August–September

[[Borage]]

  • Sow: Mid-late April (soil warmth needed)
  • Depth: ½ inch
  • Spacing: Thin to 18–24 inches apart
  • Germination: 5–10 days (fast!)
  • Notes: Self-sows readily; tall, spreading
  • Flowers: Attract bees heavily

[[Dill]]

  • Sow: Mid-late April (first of several sowings)
  • Depth: ¼ inch
  • Spacing: Thin to 12 inches apart
  • Germination: 7–10 days
  • Succession: Sow again every 2–3 weeks through June
  • Notes: Self-sows; you'll have volunteers
  • Harvest: Summer through fall

[[Fennel]]

  • Sow: Mid-late April (once warm)
  • Depth: Surface to light covering
  • Spacing: Thin to 18–24 inches apart
  • Germination: 10–14 days
  • Notes: Biennial; persists; allelopathic (gives space)
  • Harvest: Seed in late summer year 1 or year 2

🌿 Final Planting Tasks

Beds Ready?

  • [ ] Last frost date passed (check calendar)
  • [ ] Soil warm (70°F+ for warm-weather herbs)
  • [ ] Beds prepared + amended
  • [ ] Spacing planned

Plant Tender Herbs Last

Plant in this order:

  1. Frost-hardy perennials first (Thistle, Echinacea, Lemon Balm)
  2. Cool-tolerant annuals next (Cilantro already planted in March; can add more)
  3. Warm-soil lovers last (Basil varieties - wait until late April/early May for soil warmth)

Basil Exception

[[Basil]] varieties need WARMTH:

  • Don't plant until late April or early May
  • Soil should be 70°F+ (warmer, better)
  • Basil in cold soil = rot, slow growth
  • Cold-tolerant perennials: Plant earlier

🌾 Garden Care This Month

Daily Checks

  • [ ] Water transplants well (first week especially)
  • [ ] Check for wilting (indicates underwatering)
  • [ ] Monitor soil moisture (top inch should be moist, not soggy)
  • [ ] Watch for late frost warnings (protect if needed with frost cloth)

Weekly Tasks

  • [ ] Weed emerging weeds (easier now)
  • [ ] Stake tall plants (Lovage, Dill if needed)
  • [ ] Deadhead spring bulbs if any nearby
  • [ ] Scout for pests (early intervention best)

Succession Planting

  • [ ] Cilantro: Plant second crop this month (every 2 weeks)
  • [ ] Dill: First sowing; plan second in May
  • [ ] Chamomile: May need thinning from March direct sow

📋 April Planting Checklist

Transplanting

  • [ ] Harden off seedlings (week before planting)
  • [ ] Prepare garden beds (amendment, spacing marked)
  • [ ] Transplant perennials (mid-late April, after frost passes)
  • [ ] Water well; mulch
  • [ ] Monitor for transplant shock (wilting despite water = shade for recovery)

Direct Sowing

  • [ ] Wait for frost danger to pass
  • [ ] Soil must be warm (70°F+)
  • [ ] Sow: Anise, Borage, Dill, Fennel
  • [ ] Water gently; keep moist until germinated
  • [ ] Thin seedlings to proper spacing

Succession Sowing

  • [ ] Cilantro: Plant every 2 weeks
  • [ ] Dill: First sowing this month
  • [ ] Mark sowings with dates (track for staggered harvest)

Plant Care

  • [ ] Remove row cover if used (cool-season protection)
  • [ ] Stake tall direct-sows if windy (Dill, Fennel)
  • [ ] Add mulch to beds
  • [ ] Set up watering system (soaker hoses, drip irrigation)

🌳 Hardening Off Guide (Late March/Early April)

Timeline Condition Duration Notes
Week 1 (April 5–12) Shade, sheltered 1–3 hours Build tolerance slowly
Week 2 (April 12–19) Dappled sun, protected 4–6 hours Gradually increase
Week 3 (April 19–25) Morning sun, afternoon shade Most of day Nearly acclimated
Week 4 (April 26–30) Full sun (if not tender) All day Ready to transplant

🌡️ Temperature Awareness

Frost Risk April Schedule

Early April (1–10):

  • High frost risk
  • Protect tender seedlings
  • Frost cloth available
  • Transplant only frost-hardy perennials

Mid-April (10–15):

  • Frost risk declining
  • Last frost date typically occurs
  • Check local forecast
  • Once clear, transplant more

Late April (15–30):

  • Frost danger mostly past
  • Warm-season seeds safe
  • Basil can plant by late April/May 1
  • Borage, Anise, Dill, Fennel safe

🚰 Watering in April

Transplants

  • First week: Daily watering (transplant shock = water stress)
  • Week 2+: Every 2–3 days (soil should be moist, not soggy)
  • Monitor: Top inch dry = water time

Direct Sown Seeds

  • Until germination: Keep moist (7–21 days depending on seed)
  • After germination: Let top inch dry between waterings
  • Watch: Too wet = rot

Rainfall

  • April typically gets adequate rain (check forecast)
  • Supplement if dry spell appears
  • Mulch helps retain moisture

🌿 Companion Planting Reminder

Plant herbs near companions they love:

  • See [[Companion Guilds Index]] for guild groupings
  • [[Mediterranean Bed]] for drought-tolerant grouping
  • [[Pollinator Bed]] for bee-attracting arrangement
  • [[Digestive Herb Bed]] for culinary digestive pairing

💡 April Tips

Tip Why
Wait for warmth Cold soil = seed rot; patience pays
Stake tall growers April winds can topple Dill, Fennel
Water deeply Encourages deep rooting
Mulch heavily Conserves moisture, suppresses weeds
Harden off fully Acclimatization prevents plant shock
Keep records Track what planted where, when
Mark everything Months later you'll forget what's what

🌸 End-of-April Expectations

By end of April, you should have:

  • ✓ All tender perennials in ground
  • ✓ Most direct-sown seeds germinated
  • ✓ Basil starting (or will start in May)
  • ✓ Garden beds mulched + established
  • ✓ Watering system set up + functioning
  • ✓ Early growth visible
  • ✓ First weeds appearing (manage now!)

🔗 Related Pages

Monthly Planning:

  • [[March]] - Seeds planted indoors
  • [[May]] - Final warm-season herbs, peak growth starts
  • [[Month-by-Month Calendar (Zone 7B)]] - Full overview

Garden Design:

  • [[Height Map]] - Spacing reference
  • [[Water Needs Map]] - Grouping by water
  • [[Mediterranean Bed]], [[Pollinator Bed]], [[Digestive Herb Bed]] - Specific layouts

April Summary: Transplant time! Move indoor seedlings to garden. Direct sow warm-weather herbs. Space properly. Mulch. Water. You're officially in growing season!
raw:
# 🌿 April Planting Guide (Zone 7B)

**Season:** Spring in Full Force  
**Average Temps:** 55–65°F (warming rapidly)  
**Days of Light:** Long (12–14 hours)  
**Frost Risk:** LAST FROST April 5–15; diminishing rapidly  
**Soil:** Warm, workable, ideal for planting

---

## This Month's Role

April is **transplant month**. Move indoor seedlings (started Feb–March) to the garden after last frost. Direct sow warm-weather herbs. This is peak planting action.

---

## 📅 Timing: Last Frost

**Zone 7B Last Frost Date: April 5–15 (mid-month typical)**

### Watch Weather!
- Check local forecast mid-April
- Wait until nighttime temps consistently above 50°F
- Cold snap coming? Hold off planting tender herbs
- Clear week predicted? Plant day of last frost or after

---

## 🌱 Transplant Time (After Last Frost)

### Plants to Transplant (from indoor starts)

**Transplant around April 15–20** (once last frost danger passes):

Move to garden:
- [[Bergamot]]
- [[Catnip]]
- [[Echinacea]]
- [[Hyssop]]
- [[Lemon Balm]]
- [[Lovage]]
- [[Marjoram]]
- [[Mountain Mint]]
- [[Peppermint]]
- [[Lemon Mint]]
- [[Sage]]
- [[Thyme]]
- [[Oregano]]
- [[Thai Basil]]
- [[Italian Basil]]
- [[Purple Basil]]

**Plus February-started perennials:**
- [[Lavender]] (already hardened off, ready to transplant)
- [[Rosemary]] (tender; may wait for mid-May warmth)

### Transplanting Process

**Before transplanting:**
1. Harden off seedlings (acclimatize to sun/wind gradually)
   - Week of April 5–12: Part shade, sheltered
   - Week of April 12–19: Increasing sun exposure

2. Check seedling condition:
   - Healthy green leaves
   - No fungal issues
   - Well-developed roots (visible at soil surface okay)
   - 4–6 weeks old typically (ready)

**Transplanting:**

1. **Prepare planting holes** slightly larger than root ball
2. **Gently remove** seedling from tray (squeeze sides gently)
3. **Loosen roots** gently with fingers (break up root ball slightly)
4. **Plant at same depth** (bury to first leaves, not deeper)
5. **Backfill** with garden soil
6. **Water gently** immediately after transplanting
7. **Mulch** 1–2 inches around (away from stem)

**Spacing** (consult design guides):
- See [[Height Map]] for vertical spacing front-to-back
- See [[Water Needs Map]] for grouping by water needs
- Spacing: 12"–30" depending on mature size (check [[Master Plant Index]])

---

## 🌼 Direct Sow Warm-Weather Herbs

**Once soil is warm (April 15+) and frost danger passes:**

### [[Anise]]
- **Sow:** Mid-late April (wait for soil warmth)
- **Depth:** ¼–½ inch
- **Spacing:** Thin to 12 inches apart
- **Germination:** 14–21 days
- **Notes:** Annual; plant once per year
- **Harvest:** August–September

### [[Borage]]
- **Sow:** Mid-late April (soil warmth needed)
- **Depth:** ½ inch
- **Spacing:** Thin to 18–24 inches apart
- **Germination:** 5–10 days (fast!)
- **Notes:** Self-sows readily; tall, spreading
- **Flowers:** Attract bees heavily

### [[Dill]]
- **Sow:** Mid-late April (first of several sowings)
- **Depth:** ¼ inch
- **Spacing:** Thin to 12 inches apart
- **Germination:** 7–10 days
- **Succession:** Sow again every 2–3 weeks through June
- **Notes:** Self-sows; you'll have volunteers
- **Harvest:** Summer through fall

### [[Fennel]]
- **Sow:** Mid-late April (once warm)
- **Depth:** Surface to light covering
- **Spacing:** Thin to 18–24 inches apart
- **Germination:** 10–14 days
- **Notes:** Biennial; persists; allelopathic (gives space)
- **Harvest:** Seed in late summer year 1 or year 2

---

## 🌿 Final Planting Tasks

### Beds Ready?
- [ ] Last frost date passed (check calendar)
- [ ] Soil warm (70°F+ for warm-weather herbs)
- [ ] Beds prepared + amended
- [ ] Spacing planned

### Plant Tender Herbs Last
**Plant in this order:**
1. Frost-hardy perennials first (Thistle, Echinacea, Lemon Balm)
2. Cool-tolerant annuals next (Cilantro already planted in March; can add more)
3. Warm-soil lovers last (Basil varieties - wait until late April/early May for soil warmth)

### Basil Exception
**[[Basil]] varieties need WARMTH:**
- Don't plant until late April or early May
- Soil should be 70°F+ (warmer, better)
- Basil in cold soil = rot, slow growth
- Cold-tolerant perennials: Plant earlier

---

## 🌾 Garden Care This Month

### Daily Checks
- [ ] Water transplants well (first week especially)
- [ ] Check for wilting (indicates underwatering)
- [ ] Monitor soil moisture (top inch should be moist, not soggy)
- [ ] Watch for late frost warnings (protect if needed with frost cloth)

### Weekly Tasks
- [ ] Weed emerging weeds (easier now)
- [ ] Stake tall plants (Lovage, Dill if needed)
- [ ] Deadhead spring bulbs if any nearby
- [ ] Scout for pests (early intervention best)

### Succession Planting
- [ ] Cilantro: Plant second crop this month (every 2 weeks)
- [ ] Dill: First sowing; plan second in May
- [ ] Chamomile: May need thinning from March direct sow

---

## 📋 April Planting Checklist

### Transplanting
- [ ] Harden off seedlings (week before planting)
- [ ] Prepare garden beds (amendment, spacing marked)
- [ ] Transplant perennials (mid-late April, after frost passes)
- [ ] Water well; mulch
- [ ] Monitor for transplant shock (wilting despite water = shade for recovery)

### Direct Sowing
- [ ] Wait for frost danger to pass
- [ ] Soil must be warm (70°F+)
- [ ] Sow: Anise, Borage, Dill, Fennel
- [ ] Water gently; keep moist until germinated
- [ ] Thin seedlings to proper spacing

### Succession Sowing
- [ ] Cilantro: Plant every 2 weeks
- [ ] Dill: First sowing this month
- [ ] Mark sowings with dates (track for staggered harvest)

### Plant Care
- [ ] Remove row cover if used (cool-season protection)
- [ ] Stake tall direct-sows if windy (Dill, Fennel)
- [ ] Add mulch to beds
- [ ] Set up watering system (soaker hoses, drip irrigation)

---

## 🌳 Hardening Off Guide (Late March/Early April)

| Timeline | Condition | Duration | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Week 1 (April 5–12)** | Shade, sheltered | 1–3 hours | Build tolerance slowly |
| **Week 2 (April 12–19)** | Dappled sun, protected | 4–6 hours | Gradually increase |
| **Week 3 (April 19–25)** | Morning sun, afternoon shade | Most of day | Nearly acclimated |
| **Week 4 (April 26–30)** | Full sun (if not tender) | All day | Ready to transplant |

---

## 🌡️ Temperature Awareness

### Frost Risk April Schedule

**Early April (1–10):**
- High frost risk
- Protect tender seedlings
- Frost cloth available
- Transplant only frost-hardy perennials

**Mid-April (10–15):**
- Frost risk declining
- Last frost date typically occurs
- Check local forecast
- Once clear, transplant more

**Late April (15–30):**
- Frost danger mostly past
- Warm-season seeds safe
- Basil can plant by late April/May 1
- Borage, Anise, Dill, Fennel safe

---

## 🚰 Watering in April

### Transplants
- **First week:** Daily watering (transplant shock = water stress)
- **Week 2+:** Every 2–3 days (soil should be moist, not soggy)
- **Monitor:** Top inch dry = water time

### Direct Sown Seeds
- **Until germination:** Keep moist (7–21 days depending on seed)
- **After germination:** Let top inch dry between waterings
- **Watch:** Too wet = rot

### Rainfall
- April typically gets adequate rain (check forecast)
- Supplement if dry spell appears
- Mulch helps retain moisture

---

## 🌿 Companion Planting Reminder

**Plant herbs near companions they love:**
- See [[Companion Guilds Index]] for guild groupings
- [[Mediterranean Bed]] for drought-tolerant grouping
- [[Pollinator Bed]] for bee-attracting arrangement
- [[Digestive Herb Bed]] for culinary digestive pairing

---

## 💡 April Tips

| Tip | Why |
|---|---|
| **Wait for warmth** | Cold soil = seed rot; patience pays |
| **Stake tall growers** | April winds can topple Dill, Fennel |
| **Water deeply** | Encourages deep rooting |
| **Mulch heavily** | Conserves moisture, suppresses weeds |
| **Harden off fully** | Acclimatization prevents plant shock |
| **Keep records** | Track what planted where, when |
| **Mark everything** | Months later you'll forget what's what |

---

## 🌸 End-of-April Expectations

By end of April, you should have:
- ✓ All tender perennials in ground
- ✓ Most direct-sown seeds germinated
- ✓ Basil starting (or will start in May)
- ✓ Garden beds mulched + established
- ✓ Watering system set up + functioning
- ✓ Early growth visible
- ✓ First weeds appearing (manage now!)

---

## 🔗 Related Pages

**Monthly Planning:**
- [[March]] - Seeds planted indoors

**Garden Design:**
