title: Water%20Needs%20Map
html:
💧 Water Needs Map

Organize your garden by moisture requirements, grouping plants with similar water needs to create efficient, low-stress watering zones.

Why Group by Water Needs?

Benefits:

  • Use drip irrigation or soaker hoses on single zone
  • Prevent over-watering low-water plants
  • Prevent under-watering moisture-loving plants
  • Save water + effort
  • Reduce plant stress + disease

🏜️ Low-Water Zone (Drought Tolerant)

Watering schedule: Establish 1–2 months, then minimal (natural rainfall)

Best placement: Raised beds, sandy soil, sunny exposed areas

[[Thyme]]

  • Water: Once established, very drought tolerant
  • Soil: Sandy, well-drained
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Frequency: Natural rainfall after establishment
  • Concern: Wet feet = root rot; ensure drainage

[[Oregano]]

  • Water: Drought tolerant once stable
  • Soil: Well-drained, lean soil okay
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Frequency: Natural rainfall sufficient
  • Benefit: Flavor intensifies with water stress (within reason)

[[Rosemary]] (if growing from kitchen spice or purchase)

  • Water: Very drought tolerant
  • Soil: Sandy, well-drained essential
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Frequency: Minimal; prefers dry
  • Note: Worst case for wet soil; ensure perfect drainage

[[Sage]]

  • Water: Drought tolerant
  • Soil: Well-drained
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Frequency: Natural rainfall okay
  • Concern: Won't thrive in soggy soil

[[Lavender]]

(especially important)

  • Water: Drought tolerant (established)
  • Soil: Sandy, well-drained, NOT rich
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Frequency: Natural rainfall after first season
  • CRITICAL: Over-watering kills more Lavender than under-watering

[[Marjoram]]

  • Water: Tolerates dryness
  • Soil: Well-drained
  • Sun: Full sun to partial shade
  • Frequency: Natural rainfall after establishment

💧 Medium-Water Zone

Watering schedule: Regular (1–2 inches per week), consistent

Best placement: Standard garden beds, mulched areas

[[Basil]] (All varieties)

  • Water: Regular, consistent moisture
  • Soil: Rich, well-draining
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Frequency: Don't let dry out; weekly check
  • Note: Annual; replant each spring

[[Echinacea]]

  • Water: Medium (prefers drier than some, lusher than others)
  • Soil: Well-drained
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Frequency: Weekly during heat
  • Note: Tolerates some drought once established

[[Yarrow]] / [[White Yarrow]]

  • Water: Medium; fine with some dryness
  • Soil: Well-drained
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Frequency: Moderate; drought tolerant established

[[Bergamot]]

  • Water: Medium to moist-preferring
  • Soil: Average to rich, consistent moisture nice
  • Sun: Full sun to partial shade
  • Frequency: Regular watering during dry spells
  • Note: Dries out slower in partial shade

[[Lemon Balm]]

  • Water: Medium; prefers consistent
  • Soil: Average to rich
  • Sun: Full sun to partial shade (shade = lusher in heat)
  • Frequency: Regular, consistent moisture
  • Note: Gets leggy/flopping in too-dry conditions

[[Chamomile]]

  • Water: Medium
  • Soil: Average
  • Sun: Full sun to partial shade
  • Frequency: Don't let get bone dry
  • Note: Self-seeds freely if you let it dry between waterings

[[Cilantro]]

  • Water: Medium
  • Soil: Rich, consistent moisture
  • Sun: Full sun to partial shade
  • Frequency: Regular; bolts quickly if dry or hot
  • Note: Cool-season herb; extra water in spring/fall

🌊 Moist-Soil Zone

Watering schedule: Consistently moist (but not waterlogged)

Best placement: Lower garden areas, shade areas, amended soil with compost

[[Peppermint]]

  • Water: Moist; loves water
  • Soil: Rich, moist
  • Sun: Partial shade okay
  • Frequency: Keep consistently moist
  • CRITICAL: Plant in containers or with barriers (aggressive spreader)
  • Note: Shade helps prevent drying out; benefits from afternoon shade

[[Lemon Mint]]

  • Water: Moist
  • Soil: Rich, consistent moisture
  • Sun: Partial shade
  • Frequency: Regular watering
  • CRITICAL: Container or barrier (mint family spreader)

[[Mountain Mint]]

  • Water: Moist
  • Soil: Consistent moisture
  • Sun: Partial shade okay
  • Frequency: Regular watering
  • CRITICAL: Container or barrier (aggressive spreader)

[[Catnip]]

  • Water: Medium to moist
  • Soil: Average to moist
  • Sun: Full sun to partial shade
  • Frequency: Regular
  • Note: Tolerates cat damage but appreciates moisture

[[Horehound]]

  • Water: Medium to moist
  • Soil: Average to moist
  • Sun: Full sun to partial shade
  • Frequency: Regular
  • Note: Less aggressive spreading than mints; can be open-bed

📊 Water Needs by Life Cycle

Annuals (Extra Water Needed)

[[Basil]], [[Cilantro]], [[Dill]], [[Anise]], [[Borage]], [[Chamomile]]

Why: Shallow root systems, don't establish deep roots for drought access

Watering: Weekly minimum, more during heat

Mulch: Help retain moisture

Perennials (Establish, Then Less Water)

[[Lavender]], [[Sage]], [[Oregano]], [[Thyme]], [[Echinacea]], etc.

Year 1: Regular watering (establish root systems)

Year 2+: Reduce to natural rainfall for low-water types

Mulch: Helps transition to low-water

Setting Up Efficient Watering

Drip Irrigation Zones

Zone 1 (Low Water): Thyme, Oregano, Sage, Lavender

  • Drip line or soaker hose with timer
  • Set: Water 1x weekly or less
  • Time: Early morning (before heat)

Zone 2 (Medium Water): Basil, Echinacea, Bergamot, Lemon Balm

  • Drip line or soaker hose
  • Set: Water 2–3x weekly during growth
  • Adjust: More during heat waves, less during cool/rainy

Zone 3 (Moist): Mints (in containers), Peppermint

  • Hand watering or consistent drip
  • Set: Check daily, water if top inch dry
  • Note: Mints in containers dry faster

Seasonal Watering Adjustments

Spring (March–May)

Focus: Establishing new plants

  • Water more frequently than summer (cooler = less evaporation)
  • New perennials: 2–3x weekly
  • Annuals: Plant after frost, then 2–3x weekly

Summer (June–August)

Focus: Peak heat stress

  • Increase frequency (high evaporation)
  • Low-water plants: 1–2x weekly if extended drought
  • Medium/moist: 3–4x weekly typical
  • Early morning (6–8am) optimal (less evaporation)
  • Mulch helps retain moisture

Fall (September–November)

Focus: Slowing growth, rain increases

  • Reduce frequency as temps cool
  • Rainfall usually supplements
  • Reduce annuals (approaching season end)
  • Perennials: Less water (preparing dormancy)

Winter (December–February)

Focus: Dormancy

  • Minimal watering
  • Perennials dormant (need very little)
  • Rare: Snow/rain provides moisture
  • Don't water in freezing conditions

Mulching for Water Retention

Benefits of Mulch

✓ Maintains soil moisture (30–50% reduction in water loss)
✓ Regulates soil temperature
✓ Reduces weeds
✓ Improves soil as it breaks down
✓ Makes garden look finished

Best Mulches for Herbs

  • Straw (not hay): Ideal, breaks down, adds organic matter
  • Wood chips: Good, take longer to break down
  • Aged compost: Excellent, becomes soil
  • Leaves: Free, break down beautifully

Not Recommended for Most Herbs

✗ Fresh grass clippings (mat down, exclude air)
✗ Plastic (blocks air, overheats soil)
✗ Peat moss (dries out, hard to re-wet)

Apply 2–3 inches, keeping away from plant stems (prevents rot)

Soil Amendments for Water Management

For Low-Water Zones

Add to improve drainage:

  • Sand (increases drainage)
  • Perlite (lightweight, drains)
  • Raised beds (natural drainage)

Result: Faster draining = suitable for drought-tolerant herbs

For Moist-Soil Zones

Add to improve water retention:

  • Compost (holds moisture)
  • Aged manure (retains water)
  • Peat moss (excellent retention)
  • Lower elevation/basin (natural moisture sink)

Result: Slower draining = suitable for moisture-lovers

Problem Diagnosis: Too Much/Too Little Water

Signs of Over-Watering

  • Yellowing leaves (not from nutrient lack)
  • Drooping despite moist soil
  • Root rot smell (musty)
  • Fungal issues (powdery mildew)
  • Leaves dropping
  • Plant collapse looking lush but dying

Signs of Under-Watering

  • Wilting even after watering
  • Crispy leaf edges
  • Slow growth
  • Smaller leaves than expected
  • Premature flowering (stress response)
  • Complete collapse when very stressed

Water-Need Reference Table

Plant Water Need Frequency Soil Type
Thyme Low Monthly Sandy
Oregano Low Monthly Well-drained
Sage Low Monthly Well-drained
Lavender Low Monthly Very well-drained
Echinacea Medium Weekly Well-drained
Bergamot Medium Weekly Average
Basil Medium 2–3x weekly Rich
Cilantro Medium Regular Rich
Lemon Balm Medium Weekly Average to rich
Chamomile Medium Weekly Average
Peppermint Moist Daily check Rich, moist
Catnip Medium–Moist 2x weekly Average

🔗 Related Pages

Garden Design:

  • [[Height Map]]
  • [[Mediterranean Bed]]
  • [[Pollinator Bed]]
  • [[Digestive Herb Bed]]

Plant Information:

  • [[Master Plant Index]]
  • [[Companion Guilds Index]]

Seasonal Planning:

  • [[Month-by-Month Calendar (Zone 7B)]]

Summary: Group plants by water needs, water efficiently, and your garden thrives while you use less time and resources maintaining it.

- Soil: Average to rich
- Sun: Full sun to partial shade (shade = lusher in heat)
- Frequency: Regular, consistent moisture
- Note: Gets leggy/flopping in too-dry conditions

### [[Chamomile]]
- Water: Medium
- Soil: Average
- Sun: Full sun to partial shade
- Frequency: Don't let get bone dry
- Note: Self-seeds freely if you let it dry between waterings

### [[Cilantro]]
- Water: Medium
- Soil: Rich, consistent moisture
- Sun: Full sun to partial shade
- Frequency: Regular; bolts quickly if dry or hot
- Note: Cool-season herb; extra water in spring/fall

---

## 🌊 Moist-Soil Zone

**Watering schedule:** Consistently moist (but not waterlogged)

**Best placement:** Lower garden areas, shade areas, amended soil with compost

### [[Peppermint]]
- Water: Moist; loves water
- Soil: Rich, moist
- Sun: Partial shade okay
- Frequency: Keep consistently moist
- **CRITICAL:** Plant in containers or with barriers (aggressive spreader)
- Note: Shade helps prevent drying out; benefits from afternoon shade

### [[Lemon Mint]]
- Water: Moist
- Soil: Rich, consistent moisture
- Sun: Partial shade
- Frequency: Regular watering
- **CRITICAL:** Container or barrier (mint family spreader)

### [[Mountain Mint]]
- Water: Moist
- Soil: Consistent moisture
- Sun: Partial shade okay
- Frequency: Regular watering
- **CRITICAL:** Container or barrier (aggressive spreader)

### [[Catnip]]
- Water: Medium to moist
- Soil: Average to moist
- Sun: Full sun to partial shade
- Frequency: Regular
- Note: Tolerates cat damage but appreciates moisture

### [[Horehound]]
- Water: Medium to moist
- Soil: Average to moist
- Sun: Full sun to partial shade
- Frequency: Regular
- Note: Less aggressive spreading than mints; can be open-bed

---

## 📊 Water Needs by Life Cycle

### Annuals (Extra Water Needed)
[[Basil]], [[Cilantro]], [[Dill]], [[Anise]], [[Borage]], [[Chamomile]]

**Why:** Shallow root systems, don't establish deep roots for drought access

**Watering:** Weekly minimum, more during heat

**Mulch:** Help retain moisture

---

### Perennials (Establish, Then Less Water)
[[Lavender]], [[Sage]], [[Oregano]], [[Thyme]], [[Echinacea]], etc.

**Year 1:** Regular watering (establish root systems)

**Year 2+:** Reduce to natural rainfall for low-water types

**Mulch:** Helps transition to low-water

---

## Setting Up Efficient Watering

### Drip Irrigation Zones

**Zone 1 (Low Water):** Thyme, Oregano, Sage, Lavender
- Drip line or soaker hose with timer
- Set: Water 1x weekly or less
- Time: Early morning (before heat)

**Zone 2 (Medium Water):** Basil, Echinacea, Bergamot, Lemon Balm
- Drip line or soaker hose
- Set: Water 2–3x weekly during growth
- Adjust: More during heat waves, less during cool/rainy

**Zone 3 (Moist):** Mints (in containers), Peppermint
- Hand watering or consistent drip
- Set: Check daily, water if top inch dry
- Note: Mints in containers dry faster

---

## Seasonal Watering Adjustments

### Spring (March–May)
**Focus:** Establishing new plants
- Water more frequently than summer (cooler = less evaporation)
- New perennials: 2–3x weekly
- Annuals: Plant after frost, then 2–3x weekly

### Summer (June–August)
**Focus:** Peak heat stress
- Increase frequency (high evaporation)
- Low-water plants: 1–2x weekly if extended drought
- Medium/moist: 3–4x weekly typical
- Early morning (6–8am) optimal (less evaporation)
- Mulch helps retain moisture

### Fall (September–November)
**Focus:** Slowing growth, rain increases
- Reduce frequency as temps cool
- Rainfall usually supplements
- Reduce annuals (approaching season end)
- Perennials: Less water (preparing dormancy)

### Winter (December–February)
**Focus:** Dormancy
- Minimal watering
- Perennials dormant (need very little)
- Rare: Snow/rain provides moisture
- Don't water in freezing conditions

---

## Mulching for Water Retention

### Benefits of Mulch
✓ Maintains soil moisture (30–50% reduction in water loss)  
✓ Regulates soil temperature  
✓ Reduces weeds  
✓ Improves soil as it breaks down  
✓ Makes garden look finished  

### Best Mulches for Herbs
- **Straw (not hay):** Ideal, breaks down, adds organic matter
- **Wood chips:** Good, take longer to break down
- **Aged compost:** Excellent, becomes soil
- **Leaves:** Free, break down beautifully

### Not Recommended for Most Herbs
✗ Fresh grass clippings (mat down, exclude air)  
✗ Plastic (blocks air, overheats soil)  
✗ Peat moss (dries out, hard to re-wet)  

### Apply 2–3 inches, keeping away from plant stems (prevents rot)

---

## Soil Amendments for Water Management

### For Low-Water Zones
**Add to improve drainage:**
- Sand (increases drainage)
- Perlite (lightweight, drains)
- Raised beds (natural drainage)

**Result:** Faster draining = suitable for drought-tolerant herbs

### For Moist-Soil Zones
**Add to improve water retention:**
- Compost (holds moisture)
- Aged manure (retains water)
- Peat moss (excellent retention)
- Lower elevation/basin (natural moisture sink)

**Result:** Slower draining = suitable for moisture-lovers

---

## Problem Diagnosis: Too Much/Too Little Water

### Signs of Over-Watering
- Yellowing leaves (not from nutrient lack)
- Drooping despite moist soil
- Root rot smell (musty)
- Fungal issues (powdery mildew)
- Leaves dropping
- Plant collapse looking lush but dying

### Signs of Under-Watering
- Wilting even after watering
- Crispy leaf edges
- Slow growth
- Smaller leaves than expected
- Premature flowering (stress response)
- Complete collapse when very stressed

---

## Water-Need Reference Table

| Plant | Water Need | Frequency | Soil Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thyme** | Low | Monthly | Sandy |
| **Oregano** | Low | Monthly | Well-drained |
| **Sage** | Low | Monthly | Well-drained |
| **Lavender** | Low | Monthly | Very well-drained |
| **Echinacea** | Medium | Weekly | Well-drained |
| **Bergamot** | Medium | Weekly | Average |
| **Basil** | Medium | 2–3x weekly | Rich |
| **Cilantro** | Medium | Regular | Rich |
| **Lemon Balm** | Medium | Weekly | Average to rich |
| **Chamomile** | Medium | Weekly | Average |
| **Peppermint** | Moist | Daily check | Rich, moist |
| **Catnip** | Medium–Moist | 2x weekly | Average |

---

## 🔗 Related Pages

**Garden Design:**
- [[Height Map]]
- [[Mediterranean Bed]]
- [[Pollinator Bed]]
- [[Digestive Herb Bed]]

**Plant Information:**
- [[Master Plant Index]]
- [[Companion Guilds Index]]

**Seasonal Planning:**
- [[Month-by-Month Calendar (Zone 7B)]]

---

**Summary:** Group plants by water needs, water efficiently, and your garden thrives while you use less time and resources maintaining it.

title: Pollinator%20Bed
html:
🐝 Pollinator Bed Design

A vibrant herb garden specifically designed to attract and support pollinators—bees, butterflies, hoverflies, and beneficial insects that sustain garden ecosystem health.

Why a Pollinator Garden?

Benefits:

  • ✓ Supports pollinator populations
  • ✓ Improves seed set + fruit production in food garden
  • ✓ Beautiful, colorful beds
  • ✓ Diverse blooms throughout season
  • ✓ Medicinal + culinary herbs at same time
  • ✓ Creates habitat for beneficial insects
  • ✓ Ecological contribution to local ecosystem

Best Pollinator-Attracting Herbs

[[Borage]]

  • Height: 24–36 inches
  • Flowers: Bright blue, star-shaped, abundant
  • Blooms: June–September
  • Bee value: EXCELLENT (bees love borage)
  • Placement: Middle to back
  • Special note: Self-seeds readily
  • Uses: Edible flowers, refreshing taste

[[Bergamot]]

  • Height: 24–36 inches
  • Flowers: Shaggy pink/red/purple spheres
  • Blooms: July–September (peak midsummer)
  • Bee/butterfly value: EXCELLENT (beloved by all)
  • Placement: Middle
  • Medicinal: Digestive, nervine, antimicrobial
  • Varieties: Red, pink, purple

[[Echinacea]] (Purple Coneflower)

  • Height: 24–48 inches
  • Flowers: Pink-purple with prominent orange cone center
  • Blooms: July–October (very long season)
  • Bee/butterfly value: EXCELLENT
  • Placement: Middle to back
  • Medicinal: Immune support, respiratory
  • Hardiness: Very hardy perennial

[[Lavender]]

  • Height: 18–36 inches (varieties vary)
  • Flowers: Purple spikes, fragrant
  • Blooms: June–August
  • Bee value: EXCELLENT (bees swarm lavender)
  • Bee behavior: Watch bees dive into flowers
  • Placement: Middle
  • Medicinal: Calming, wound healing, antimicrobial
  • Varieties: English (hardy), French (less hardy), Spanish

[[White Yarrow]]

  • Height: 24–36 inches
  • Flowers: White/cream cluster flowers
  • Blooms: June–September
  • Bee/butterfly value: EXCELLENT
  • Placement: Middle to back
  • Medicinal: Wound healing, circulation, fever support
  • Note: Available in multiple colors (use white for contrast)

Supporting Pollinator Herbs

[[Sage]]

  • Flowers: Purple to pink spikes
  • Bee value: Good (secondary bloomer)
  • Placement: Middle
  • Perennial: Long-lived, reliable

[[Thyme]]

  • Flowers: Small pink/purple/white flowers
  • Bee value: Good (especially creeping varieties)
  • Placement: Front border
  • Abundant small flowers = multiple landing zones

[[Oregano]]

  • Flowers: Small pink/purple clustered
  • Bee value: Moderate (small flowers)
  • Bumblebee favorite: Heavier bees love oregano
  • Placement: Front-middle

[[Basil]] (Annual, but prolific)

  • Flowers: Small white/pink flowers (if allowed to bolt)
  • Bee value: Moderate-good (flowering varieties)
  • Note: Most people pinch off flowers (culinary focus)
  • Pollinator option: Let some bolt to flower

[[Chamomile]]

  • Flowers: Small daisy-like
  • Bee value: Moderate (small flowers, some floral visitors)
  • Placement: Front to middle
  • Self-sower: Abundant if allowed

[[Dill]]

  • Flowers: Tiny yellow umbrella clusters (umbels)
  • Bee value: EXCELLENT (especially beneficial insects)
  • Parasitic wasp attraction: Supreme
  • Placement: Middle to back
  • Note: Tall, narrow form doesn't shade other plants

[[Fennel]]

  • Flowers: Tiny yellow umbels
  • Bee value: EXCELLENT (especially beneficial insects)
  • Placement: Back (tall)
  • Similar to dill, fennel attracts parasitic wasps

Color Strategy for Pollinator Appeal

Bee Color Preferences

  • Blue/purple: MOST attractive to bees
  • Yellow: Very attractive
  • White: Attractive
  • Red: Less attractive (bees aren't great at seeing red)
  • Orange: Moderate attraction

Butterfly Preferences

  • Purple: Very attractive
  • Pink: Attractive
  • Orange: Butterfly magnet
  • Yellow: Attractive
  • White: Moderate

Design with Color

Purple/Blue Focus: Lavender, Echinacea, Sage, Hyssop (bee paradise) Pink/Red: Bergamot varieties (butterfly + bee attraction) Yellow: Dill, Fennel (beneficial insect heaven) White: Yarrow (creates bright contrast)

📐 Pollinator Bed Design (6' x 12')

BACK (NORTH)
[Echinacea] [Dill] [Fennel] [Borage-tall]

MIDDLE
[Bergamot]  [Sage]  [Lavender]  [Lavender]

FRONT (SOUTH)
[Thyme] [Oregano] [Chamomile] [Yarrow-shorter form]

Total plants: 13–15

Color progression:

  • Back: Purple (Echinacea) + Yellow (Dill/Fennel)
  • Middle: Pink (Bergamot) + Purple (Lavender, Sage)
  • Front: Whites + smaller colorful

Bloom sequence: June–October (continuous blooms)

Creating Ideal Pollinator Habitat

Flower Density

  • Plant densely enough for visual reward (bees see flowers from distance)
  • Space for mature size (crowding = poor air, disease)
  • Odd groupings (3, 5, 7 same plant) = better than scattered

Height Layering

Helps bees navigate:

  • Back: Tall flowers (Borage, Dill, Fennel = landing zones)
  • Middle: Medium access (Bergamot, Echinacea = platforms)
  • Front: Low access (Thyme, Oregano = ground entry)

Soil & Watering

  • Well-prepared bed (healthy plants = better nectar)
  • Regular but not excessive watering
  • Healthy soil = robust growth = abundant flowers

Avoid Pesticides

  • CRITICAL: No synthetic pesticides (kill pollinators)
  • Use organic methods only:
    • Hand-pick pests
    • Encourage beneficial predators
    • Neem oil (organic, less toxic)
    • Companion planting

Succession Planting for Extended Bloom

Early Summer (June)

  • [[Sage]]
  • [[Lavender]]
  • Early [[Thyme]]

Mid-Summer (July)

  • [[Bergamot]]
  • [[Echinacea]] (peak bloom)
  • [[Borage]]

Late Summer (August–September)

  • [[Echinacea]] (continuing)
  • [[Yarrow]]
  • [[Dill/Fennel]] (if allowed to flower)

Fall (October)

  • Late [[Echinacea]]
  • [[Aster]] (if adding)

Goal: Always flowers blooming June–October

Supporting Different Pollinators

Honeybees

  • Prefer blue/purple flowers
  • Need deep flowers or platform shapes
  • Lavender, Echinacea, Borage = gold standard
  • Consistent nectar sources (multiple flowers)

Bumblebees

  • Heavier than honeybees
  • Need sturdy landing platforms
  • Bergamot, Sage, Oregano (wider landing zones)
  • Longer flower tubes acceptable

Butterflies

  • Prefer pink/red/purple
  • Need clustered flowers (flat-tops = better)
  • Bergamot (shaggy clusters = butterfly paradise)
  • Yarrow (flat clusters)
  • Echinacea (purple favorite)

Beneficial Insects (Parasitic Wasps)

  • Hover flies, lacewings, parasitic wasps
  • Need small flower clusters
  • Dill, Fennel (tiny umbels = favorite)
  • Eliminates garden pests naturally

Hoverflies

  • Mimic bees (harmless)
  • Love small flowers
  • Fennel, Dill, Yarrow
  • Beneficial predators

Year-Round Pollinator Garden Care

Spring (March–April)

  • Prepare beds
  • Plant new stock
  • Do not deadhead fall flowers (overwintering insects inside)

Early Summer (May–June)

  • First flowers emerge
  • Begin weekly observation (watch pollinators)
  • Pinch back (if needed) for bushier growth
  • Water regularly

Peak Season (July–August)

  • Maximum bloom
  • Minimal intervention (let flowers be!)
  • Dead-head spent flowers to encourage more
  • Water regularly (flowers = thirsty)
  • Resist pesticides completely

Fall (September–October)

  • Final blooms
  • Reduce deadheading (let seed form)
  • Skip hard pruning (insects need seed heads for overwintering)
  • Leave dead stalks over winter

Winter (November–February)

  • Leave seed heads standing (insect overwintering sites)
  • Don't clean up beds!
  • Cavity-nesting bees overwinter in hollow stems
  • Spring cleanup: Clear debris but preserve structure

What NOT to Do

Avoid

✗ Pesticides (kills pollinators)
✗ Herbicides (kills flowering plants)
✗ Double/heavy-petaled flowers (no nectar access)
✗ Weed-wacking in bloom season (destroys flowers)
✗ Over-tidy beds (insects need leaf litter)
✗ Water-logging (poor bee habitat)

Observing Predator/Prey in Pollinator Garden

Beneficial Observation

  • parasitic wasps (tiny, harmless to humans)
  • Hover flies (look like small bees, harmless)
  • Lacewings (eat aphids, etc.)
  • Harvestmen (eat pest eggs)

These Are Your Allies

  • Presence = sign of healthy ecosystem
  • They control garden pests naturally
  • Eliminate need for pesticides
  • Create resilient growing system

Pollinator Garden Growth Expectations

Year 1

  • Some bloom first year
  • Perennials establish roots (less flower energy)
  • Building ecosystem
  • Moderate pollinator activity

Year 2

  • Full bloom from perennials
  • Self-sown Borage, Chamomile, Dill appear
  • Increased pollinator activity
  • Very rewarding!

Year 3+

  • Peak production
  • Pollinator-dependent plants thriving
  • Seed set excellent (more next-year volunteers)
  • Garden feels "alive"

Companion Blooms (Non-Herbs)

If adding non-herb flowers for visual punch:

  • Zinnias (annual, butterfly magnet)
  • Cosmos (annual, light, airy)
  • Sunflowers (annual, bee giant favorite)
  • Calendula (annual, edible too)
  • Wildflowers (local species native)

🔗 Related Pages

Garden Design:

  • [[Height Map]]
  • [[Water Needs Map]]
  • [[Mediterranean Bed]]
  • [[Digestive Herb Bed]]

Herb Information:

  • [[Master Plant Index]]
  • [[Companion Guilds Index]]
  • [[Color & Energetics Index]]

Seasonal Planning:

  • [[Month-by-Month Calendar (Zone 7B)]]

Summary: A pollinator garden is a gift to the ecosystem while providing abundant medicinal + culinary herbs. Create habitat, watch amazing insect activity, and grow better plants simultaneously.

## Why a Pollinator Garden?

### [[Basil]] *(Annual, but prolific)*

### [[Chamomile]]
- Flowers: Small daisy-like

- Placement: Front to middle

### [[Dill]]
- Flowers: Tiny yellow umbrella clusters (umbels)
- Bee value: EXCELLENT (especially beneficial insects)

### [[Fennel]]
- Flowers: Tiny yellow umbels

---

## Color Strategy for Pollinator Appeal

### Bee Color Preferences
- **Blue/purple:** MOST attractive to bees

### Butterfly Preferences
- **Purple:** Very attractive
- **Pink:** Attractive

- **Yellow:** Attractive

### Design with Color
**Purple/Blue Focus:** Lavender, Echinacea, Sage, Hyssop (bee paradise)
**Pink/Red:** Bergamot varieties (butterfly + bee attraction)

## 📐 Pollinator Bed Design (6' x 12')

```

[Thyme] [Oregano] [Chamomile] [Yarrow-shorter form]
```

**Total plants:** 13–15

**Color progression:** 
- Back: Purple (Echinacea) + Yellow (Dill/Fennel)
- Middle: Pink (Bergamot) + Purple (Lavender, Sage)
- Front: Whites + smaller colorful

**Bloom sequence:** June–October (continuous blooms)

---

## Creating Ideal Pollinator Habitat

### Flower Density

### Height Layering
Helps bees navigate:
- Back: Tall flowers (Borage, Dill, Fennel = landing zones)

### Soil & Watering

- Regular but not excessive watering

  - Hand-pick pests

### Early Summer (June)
- [[Sage]]
- [[Lavender]]
- Early [[Thyme]]

### Mid-Summer (July)
- [[Bergamot]]

- [[Borage]]

### Late Summer (August–September)
- [[Echinacea]] (continuing)
- [[Yarrow]]

## Supporting Different Pollinators

### Honeybees
- Prefer blue/purple flowers

### Bumblebees
- Heavier than honeybees

- Longer flower tubes acceptable

### Butterflies
- Prefer pink/red/purple
- Need clustered flowers (flat-tops = better)
- Bergamot (shaggy clusters = butterfly paradise)
- Yarrow (flat clusters)
- Echinacea (purple favorite)

### Beneficial Insects (Parasitic Wasps)
- Hover flies, lacewings, parasitic wasps
- Need small flower clusters
- Dill, Fennel (tiny umbels = favorite)
- Eliminates garden pests naturally

### Hoverflies
- Mimic bees (harmless)

---

## Year-Round Pollinator Garden Care

### Spring (March–April)
- Prepare beds
- Plant new stock
- Do not deadhead fall flowers (overwintering insects inside)

### Early Summer (May–June)
- First flowers emerge
- Begin weekly observation (watch pollinators)
- Pinch back (if needed) for bushier growth

### Peak Season (July–August)
- Maximum bloom

### Fall (September–October)
- Final blooms

- Leave dead stalks over winter

### Winter (November–February)
- Leave seed heads standing (insect overwintering sites)

---

✗ Pesticides (kills pollinators)

✗ Weed-wacking in bloom season (destroys flowers)  
✗ Over-tidy beds (insects need leaf litter)  
✗ Water-logging (poor bee habitat)  

---

## Observing Predator/Prey in Pollinator Garden

### Beneficial Observation
- parasitic wasps (tiny, harmless to humans)
- Hover flies (look like small bees, harmless)
- Lacewings (eat aphids, etc.)
- Harvestmen (eat pest eggs)

### These Are Your Allies
- Presence = sign of healthy ecosystem
- They control garden pests naturally
- Eliminate need for pesticides
- Create resilient growing system

---

## Pollinator Garden Growth Expectations

### Year 1
- Some bloom first year
- Perennials establish roots (less flower energy)
- Building ecosystem
- Moderate pollinator activity

### Year 2
- Full bloom from perennials
- Self-sown Borage, Chamomile, Dill appear
- Increased pollinator activity
- Very rewarding!

### Year 3+
- Peak production
- Pollinator-dependent plants thriving
- Seed set excellent (more next-year volunteers)
- Garden feels "alive"

---

## Companion Blooms (Non-Herbs)

**If adding non-herb flowers for visual punch:**
- **Zinnias** (annual, butterfly magnet)

## 🔗 Related Pages

**Garden Design:**
- [[Height Map]]

title: Mediterranean%20Bed
html:
🌿 Mediterranean Herb Bed Design

A complete garden bed layout for heat-loving, drought-tolerant Mediterranean herbs that thrive together with minimal maintenance.

What is a Mediterranean Herb Garden?

A Mediterranean bed focuses on herbs originating from dry, sunny regions around the Mediterranean Sea. These herbs share:

  • ✓ Preference for full sun (6+ hours)
  • ✓ Drought tolerance (low water needs)
  • ✓ Excellent drainage requirements
  • ✓ Lean soil preference (avoid over-fertilizing)
  • ✓ Similar harvesting/uses (culinary + medicinal)
  • ✓ Similar pest/disease resistance
  • ✓ Aromatic foliage (volatile oil rich)

Benefits: Low-maintenance, pest-resistant, abundant harvest, beautiful/fragrant

Core Mediterranean Herbs

[[Thyme]]

  • Height: 6–12 inches
  • Spread: 12–18 inches
  • Placement: Front border (cascading over edge nice)
  • Water: Very low (once established)
  • Propagation: Cuttings easily
  • Culinary: Essential for Mediterranean cooking

[[Oregano]]

  • Height: 12–18 inches
  • Spread: 18–24 inches
  • Placement: Front-middle
  • Water: Low
  • Propagation: Division in spring, cuttings
  • Culinary: Core Mediterranean herb; essential

[[Rosemary]]

  • Height: 24–36 inches (varies by form)
  • Spread: 18–30 inches
  • Placement: Middle to back (depending on variety)
  • Water: Low (very drought tolerant established)
  • Propagation: Cuttings (slow rooting, 4–8 weeks)
  • Culinary: Bold flavor, medicinal value
  • Tender: Zone 7B = borderline hardy; protect in winter
  • Varieties: 'Tuscan Blue' (upright), 'Prostrate' (ground cover)

[[Sage]]

  • Height: 18–30 inches
  • Spread: 18–30 inches
  • Placement: Middle
  • Water: Low
  • Propagation: Cuttings, division
  • Culinary: Savory, traditional pairing with rosemary
  • Forms: Green sage (standard), Red sage, Gold sage (color varieties)

[[Marjoram]]

  • Height: 12–18 inches
  • Spread: 18–24 inches
  • Placement: Front-middle
  • Water: Low to medium
  • Propagation: Cuttings, division
  • Culinary: Milder than oregano, sweet undertone
  • Note: Often confused with oregano but distinct flavor

[[Hyssop]] (Optional, slightly less Mediterranean)

  • Height: 18–24 inches
  • Spread: 12–18 inches
  • Placement: Middle
  • Water: Low to medium
  • Medicinal: Respiratory support
  • Culinary: Unique flavor, attractive flowers

📐 Sample Mediterranean Bed Designs

Small Mediterranean Spiral (3' diameter)

            [Rosemary-tall]
        [Sage]      [Oregano]
    [Thyme]     [Marjoram]
[Thyme edge] [Thyme edge]

Placement: Herb spiral (height increases toward center) Size: ~3–4 square feet Plants needed: 7–8 plants (2–3 of each thyme) Full sun: 6+ hours essential Soil: Add perlite/sand for drainage

Standard Rectangle Bed (4' x 8')

BACK (NORTH)
[Rosemary (3ft)] [Sage] [Oregano]

MIDDLE
[Thyme]  [Marjoram]  [Thyme]  [Hyssop]

FRONT (SOUTH)
[Thyme-cascading] [Thyme-cascading] [Marjoram-edge]

Total plants: 11–13 (adjust spacing for mature size) Sun exposure: South-facing bed ideal Drainage: Crucial; raise bed or add drainage amends

Container Mediterranean Mix

For patios, balconies, limited space:

Large Container (14+ inches):

  • Center: 1 Rosemary or Sage
  • Edge: 2–3 Thyme (cascading over sides)
  • Filler: Marjoram

Medium Container (10–12 inches):

  • 1 Oregano or Thyme (single variety)

Drainage: Crucial; use quality potting soil, not garden soil

Soil Preparation (Critical for Mediterranean Success)

Before Planting

Step 1: Test Drainage

  1. Dig hole, fill with water
  2. If water drains in <2 minutes = good
  3. If water pools = needs amendment

Step 2: Amend Soil (if needed)

  • Add 1/3 perlite or coarse sand to existing soil
  • Till in 2–3 inches deep
  • Creates loose, draining medium

Step 3: Avoid Rich Soil

  • Traditional garden compost = TOO rich for Mediterranean herbs
  • Lean, poor soil = stronger flavor, more compact growth
  • Skip adding fertilizer (especially nitrogen)

Step 4: Ensure pH Neutral (6.0–7.5)

  • Most Mediterranean herbs prefer near-neutral
  • Thyme/Oregano tolerate slightly acidic
  • Test kit available at garden centers

Spacing for Mature Size

When Planting, Space for Mature Spread:

  • Thyme: 12–18 inches apart
  • Oregano: 18–24 inches apart
  • Rosemary: 24–30 inches apart
  • Sage: 18–30 inches apart (depending on form)
  • Marjoram: 18–24 inches apart
  • Hyssop: 18–24 inches apart

Avoid: Overcrowding (encourages fungal issues, poor air circulation)

Planting Timeline (Zone 7B)

Spring (April–May)

  • Best time to plant most Mediterranean herbs
  • After last frost (April 15)
  • Soil workable, moderate temps
  • Plant = established by summer

Early Fall (August–September)

  • Secondary planting time
  • Cooler temps help establishment
  • Less watering needed
  • Plant by September 15 (enough time before winter)

Avoid Winter* (December–February)

  • Root establishment minimal in dormancy
  • Winter kill risk

Watering & Establishment

First 4 Weeks (Establishment)

  • Water 2–3x per week (keep soil moist, not soggy)
  • Check soil: Top inch dry = water needed
  • No fertilizer (concentrate on root growth)
  • Mulch 2–3 inches around plants, away from stems

Months 2–3

  • Reduce frequency (1–2x per week)
  • Plant roots growing deeper
  • Begin reducing (Mediterranean plants adapt to dryness)

After 3 Months (Established)

  • Water minimal: 1x weekly or less
  • Rely on rainfall
  • Only water during extended drought
  • Over-watering = root rot
  • Withering leaves = likely over-watering, not under

Mulching Mediterranean Beds

Best Mulches

  • Pea gravel: Mediterranean aesthetic, good drainage
  • Wood chips: Traditional, breaks down into soil
  • Aged bark: Similar to wood chips
  • Straw: Good, breaks down fast

NOT Recommended

✗ Landscape fabric (blocks air, water; Mediterranean plants hate)
✗ Fresh bark (decomposes too quickly, depletes nitrogen)
✗ Thick mulch (Mediterranean plants prefer "breathable" conditions)

Amount

  • 2–3 inches maximum
  • Keep 1–2 inches away from stems (prevent rot)
  • Thin/light application (not heavy)

Pruning & Maintenance

Spring (March–April)

  • Prune hard (remove 1/3 of growth)
  • Stimulates bushy, compact growth
  • Remove any winter-killed stems
  • Timing: Before new growth starts

Summer (June–August)

  • Harvest regularly (acts as pruning)
  • Pinch tops to encourage branching
  • Remove flowers if foliage priority
  • Heat-tolerant; little care needed

Fall (September–October)

  • Light deadheading (spent flowers)
  • Don't hard prune (newly growing plants need structure)
  • Let some flowers set seed (nature's plan)

Winter (November–February)

  • Minimal pruning (dormant = vulnerable)
  • Rosemary/tender herbs: Mulch heavily or container move
  • Remove dead wood in late winter (preparatory)

Harvest & Use

Harvesting Guidelines

  • Harvest regularly = fuller, bushier plants
  • Cut top 1/3 of growth
  • Leave bottom structure intact
  • Morning harvest (post-dew) = peak flavor
  • Dry herbs for winter use

Culinary Combinations

  • Thyme + Rosemary: Classic savory duo
  • Thyme + Oregano + Marjoram: Herb blend for Italian cooking
  • Sage + Rosemary: Pairing for poultry
  • All together: Mediterranean pizza/pasta herb mix

Medicinal Uses

  • Thyme: Respiratory, antimicrobial
  • Oregano: Antimicrobial, antioxidant, digestive
  • Rosemary: Circulation, memory support, antioxidant
  • Sage: Throat, digestion, antimicrobial
  • Marjoram: Digestive, relaxing, warming

Companion Planting in Mediterranean Bed

Beneficial Companions

  • Lavender: Pairs beautifully, similar needs (add for color)
  • Thyme + Oregano: Natural pairing, no conflict
  • Sage + Rosemary: Both prefer dry, love full sun

Avoid Mixing

✗ Mints (too aggressive, will overtake)
✗ Very moisture-loving herbs (they'll compete for different water)
✗ Shade-preferring herbs (wrong light needs)

Problem Solving

Common Issues

Powdery Mildew (white coating on leaves)

  • Cause: Too much moisture, poor air circulation
  • Prevention: Space plants wide, water at soil level (not foliage), ensure drainage
  • Solution: Remove affected leaves, improve circulation, reduce watering

Root Rot (plants dying despite moisture)

  • Cause: Over-watering, poor drainage
  • Prevention: Ensure well-draining soil, water sparingly
  • Solution: Reduce watering immediately; may be too late to save

Leggy Growth (sparse, tall, not bushy)

  • Cause: Insufficient light or over-fertilizing
  • Prevention: Full sun location, avoid nitrogen fertilizer
  • Solution: Prune hard in spring to force branching

Pest Issues (rare but possible)

  • Spider mites, aphids: Uncommon on healthy Mediterranean herbs
  • Prevention: Healthy plants resist; good drainage + air circulation
  • Solution: Spray neem oil if needed; usually not necessary

Year-Round Care Calendar

Month Task
February Plan bed, order plants
March Prepare soil, hard prune existing plants
April Plant new stock, water regularly
May Begin harvesting, reduce water
June–August Harvest regularly, minimal watering
September Light deadheading, prepare for dormancy
October Reduce water, mulch tender plants
November–January Minimal care, plan next year

🔗 Related Pages

Garden Design:

  • [[Height Map]]
  • [[Water Needs Map]]
  • [[Pollinator Bed]]
  • [[Digestive Herb Bed]]

Herb Information:

  • [[Master Plant Index]]
  • [[Companion Guilds Index]]
  • [[Color & Energetics Index]]

Seasonal Planning:

  • [[Month-by-Month Calendar (Zone 7B)]]

Summary: A Mediterranean herb bed is low-maintenance, beautiful, aromatic, and abundantly productive. Once established, it requires minimal care while providing year-round culinary and medicinal harvests.

**For patios, balconies, limited space:**

**Large Container (14+ inches):**

### Before Planting

### Spring (April–May)
- Best time to plant most Mediterranean herbs

### Early Fall (August–September)

### Avoid Winter* *(December–February)*

## Watering & Establishment

### First 4 Weeks (Establishment)
- Water 2–3x per week (keep soil moist, not soggy)

### Months 2–3

---

## Mulching Mediterranean Beds

### Best Mulches
- **Pea gravel:** Mediterranean aesthetic, good drainage
- **Wood chips:** Traditional, breaks down into soil
- **Aged bark:** Similar to wood chips
- **Straw:** Good, breaks down fast

### NOT Recommended
✗ Landscape fabric (blocks air, water; Mediterranean plants hate)

### Amount
- 2–3 inches maximum
- Keep 1–2 inches away from stems (prevent rot)
- Thin/light application (not heavy)

---

## Pruning & Maintenance

### Spring (March–April)
- Prune hard (remove 1/3 of growth)
- Stimulates bushy, compact growth
- Remove any winter-killed stems
- Timing: Before new growth starts

### Summer (June–August)
- Harvest regularly (acts as pruning)
- Pinch tops to encourage branching
- Remove flowers if foliage priority
- Heat-tolerant; little care needed

### Fall (September–October)
- Light deadheading (spent flowers)
- Don't hard prune (newly growing plants need structure)
- Let some flowers set seed (nature's plan)

### Winter (November–February)
- Minimal pruning (dormant = vulnerable)
- Rosemary/tender herbs: Mulch heavily or container move
- Remove dead wood in late winter (preparatory)

---

## Harvest & Use

### Harvesting Guidelines
- Harvest regularly = fuller, bushier plants
- Cut top 1/3 of growth
- Leave bottom structure intact
- Morning harvest (post-dew) = peak flavor
- Dry herbs for winter use

### Culinary Combinations
- **Thyme + Rosemary:** Classic savory duo
- **Thyme + Oregano + Marjoram:** Herb blend for Italian cooking
- **Sage + Rosemary:** Pairing for poultry
- **All together:** Mediterranean pizza/pasta herb mix

### Medicinal Uses
- **Thyme:** Respiratory, antimicrobial
- **Oregano:** Antimicrobial, antioxidant, digestive
- **Rosemary:** Circulation, memory support, antioxidant
- **Sage:** Throat, digestion, antimicrobial
- **Marjoram:** Digestive, relaxing, warming

---

## Companion Planting in Mediterranean Bed

### Beneficial Companions
- **Lavender:** Pairs beautifully, similar needs (add for color)
- **Thyme + Oregano:** Natural pairing, no conflict
- **Sage + Rosemary:** Both prefer dry, love full sun

### Avoid Mixing
✗ Mints (too aggressive, will overtake)  
✗ Very moisture-loving herbs (they'll compete for different water)  
✗ Shade-preferring herbs (wrong light needs)  

---

## Problem Solving

### Common Issues

**Powdery Mildew (white coating on leaves)**
- Cause: Too much moisture, poor air circulation
- Prevention: Space plants wide, water at soil level (not foliage), ensure drainage
- Solution: Remove affected leaves, improve circulation, reduce watering

**Root Rot (plants dying despite moisture)**
- Cause: Over-watering, poor drainage
- Prevention: Ensure well-draining soil, water sparingly
- Solution: Reduce watering immediately; may be too late to save

**Leggy Growth (sparse, tall, not bushy)**
- Cause: Insufficient light or over-fertilizing
- Prevention: Full sun location, avoid nitrogen fertilizer
- Solution: Prune hard in spring to force branching

**Pest Issues (rare but possible)**
- Spider mites, aphids: Uncommon on healthy Mediterranean herbs
- Prevention: Healthy plants resist; good drainage + air circulation
- Solution: Spray neem oil if needed; usually not necessary

title: Height%20Map
html:
📏 Garden Height Map

Use this guide to arrange herbs by mature height, creating visually pleasing beds and ensuring proper sun access to all plants.

Garden Height Layering (Front to Back)

Arrange plants with shortest in front, tallest in back, to prevent shading smaller plants.

🌱 Front Border (0–18 inches)

Best for: Edging garden beds, container planting, visual foreground

[[Thyme]]

  • Height: 6–12 inches
  • Spread: 12–18 inches
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Water: Low
  • Best placement: Along bed edge, pathway border
  • Cascading over edges acceptable

[[Oregano]]

  • Height: 12–18 inches
  • Spread: 18–24 inches
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Water: Low
  • Best placement: Front but not extreme edge (spreads wide)

[[Chamomile]]

  • Height: 12–24 inches
  • Spread: 12–18 inches
  • Sun: Full sun to partial shade
  • Water: Medium
  • Best placement: Front of bed, can soften hard edges
  • Note: Tends to self-seed; embrace or deadhead accordingly

[[Marjoram]]

  • Height: 12–18 inches
  • Spread: 18–24 inches
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Water: Low to medium
  • Best placement: Front-middle, not extreme edge

🌿 Middle Layer (18–36 inches)

Best for: Central bed placement, supporting cast of garden

[[Lavender]]

  • Height: 18–24 inches (can reach 36")
  • Spread: 18–30 inches
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Water: Low
  • Best placement: Middle, grouping 3–5 together for impact
  • Variety note: Compact cultivars available (12–18")

[[Bergamot]]

  • Height: 24–36 inches
  • Spread: 24–36 inches
  • Sun: Full sun to partial shade
  • Water: Medium to moist
  • Best placement: Middle layer
  • Social: Magnet for pollinators; notice increased bee activity

[[Hyssop]]

  • Height: 18–24 inches (can reach 32")
  • Spread: 12–18 inches (narrow)
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Water: Low to medium
  • Best placement: Middle, upright form nice for tight spaces

[[Sage]]

  • Height: 18–30 inches
  • Spread: 18–30 inches
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Water: Low
  • Best placement: Middle, sculptural form adds interest
  • Note: Gets woody with age; prune hard in spring

[[Lemon Balm]]

  • Height: 24–36 inches
  • Spread: 24–30 inches
  • Sun: Full sun to partial shade
  • Water: Medium
  • Best placement: Middle, but contained (can spread aggressively)
  • Caution: Mint family; needs containment strategy

[[Catnip]]

  • Height: 24–36 inches
  • Spread: 18–24 inches
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Water: Low to medium
  • Best placement: Middle, consider cat visitor impacts
  • Note: Cats may damage plants; plant away from seating

🌾 Tall Layer (3–6 feet)

Best for: Back of bed, focal points, wind breaks

[[Lovage]]

  • Height: 4–6 feet
  • Spread: 2–3 feet
  • Sun: Full sun to partial shade
  • Water: Medium (prefers consistent)
  • Best placement: Back, tall focal point
  • Impact: Dramatic height, hollow stems interesting
  • Note: May need staking in windy spots

[[Fennel]]

  • Height: 3–5 feet
  • Spread: 18–24 inches
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Water: Low to medium
  • Best placement: Back, tall but narrow
  • Uses: Culinary, traditional, attracts beneficial insects
  • Caution: Allelopathic (may suppress nearby plants); give space

[[Dill]]

  • Height: 2–4 feet (varieties vary)
  • Spread: 12–18 inches
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Water: Medium
  • Best placement: Back or middle
  • Note: Self-seeds readily; mark/manage accordingly
  • Benefit: Attracts parasitic wasps (beneficial insects)

[[Echinacea]]

  • Height: 2–4 feet
  • Spread: 18–24 inches
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Water: Low to medium
  • Best placement: Back to middle
  • Impact: Flowers on tall stems, pollinator magnet
  • Blooms: Midsummer to fall

🌳 Very Tall Plants (5–8 feet, Use Sparingly)

[[Horehound]]

  • Height: 2–3 feet (less tall, but medicinal)
  • Spread: 18–24 inches
  • Sun: Full sun
  • Water: Low
  • Best placement: Tall middle or back

[[Peppermint]] / [[Mint Family]]

  • Height: 2–4 feet (if trained)
  • Spread: Extremely aggressive (underground runners)
  • Sun: Partial shade okay
  • Water: Moist
  • CRITICAL: Plant in containers only, or use underground barriers
  • Best placement: Contained area, not open bed

📐 Sample Garden Layouts

Small Standard Bed (4' x 8')

BACK
[Lovage]  [Fennel]  [Dill]
[Lavender]  [Bergamot]  [Sage]
[Thyme]  [Oregano]  [Chamomile]
FRONT

Sun Access: Taller plants don't shade shorter ones Spacing: Accounts for mature spread Watering: Grouped by similar needs (left: low-water; center: medium)

Medium Pollinator Bed (6' x 12')

BACK (NORTH FACING)
[Dill] [Echinacea] [Fennel] [Lovage]

MID
[Bergamot]  [Lavender]  [Lavender]  [Sage]

FRONT (SOUTH FACING)  
[Thyme]  [Oregano]  [Chamomile]  [Marjoram]

Total height range: 6" (thyme) to 6' (lovage) Bloom sequence: Spring through fall Pollinator value: High (flowers at various heights all season)

Vertical Container Stacking (Small Space)

Use tiered containers:

  1. Back (tall container, raised): Lovage or Dill
  2. Center (medium container): Lavender + Bergamot
  3. Front (low container): Thyme + Oregano edge

Benefits:

  • Maximizes space
  • Improved drainage
  • Easier harvesting
  • Year-round portability

View Lines Considerations

Sightline Height

  • Standing eye level: ~5'–6' (most people)
  • Plant tall herbs behind sitting areas (to not obstruct view)
  • Plant shorter herbs in front of seating (create frame, don't block sightline)

Visual Impact

  • Odd-numbered groupings (3, 5, 7) are more visually appealing than even
  • Repetition: Plant same herb 3+ places for cohesion
  • Contrast: Tall + short near each other for interest

Spacing & Mature Size Table

Plant Min Height Max Height Mature Spread Front/Mid/Back
Thyme 6" 12" 12–18" Front
Oregano 12" 18" 18–24" Front
Chamomile 12" 24" 12–18" Front
Sage 18" 30" 18–30" Mid
Lavender 18" 36" 18–30" Mid
Bergamot 24" 36" 24–36" Mid
Dill 24" 48" 12–18" Mid–Back
Fennel 36" 60" 18–24" Back
Lovage 48" 72" 24–36" Back
Echinacea 24" 48" 18–24" Mid–Back

Preventing Shade Problems

Shade-Causing Mistakes

✗ Planting tall (Fennel, Lovage) in front of shorter (Thyme, Oregano)
✗ Planting wide-spreading (Bergamot) too close to compact (Sage)
✗ Not removing dead tall stems early spring (blocks spring growth)
✗ Letting self-seeders (Dill, Fennel) spread indiscriminately

Preventing Shade

✓ Arrange front → back by height consistently
✓ Prune taller plants in early spring for cleaner emergence
✓ Thin Dill/Fennel seedlings or pull extras
✓ Allow ample spacing (don't overcrowd)
✓ Monitor and adjust first year (many plants different than expected)

Seasonal Height Changes

Spring: Plants emerge different heights than summer (growth continues)

Early Summer: Most reach mature height by June

Late Summer: Some taller plants may lean/sprawl (staking may help)

Fall: Many perennials shorten as they prepare dormancy

Winter: Cut back hard; minimal height

First-Year Adjustment

Note: Newly planted herbs often grow differently than expected:

  • May stay shorter first year
  • May grow faster than expected
  • May spread wider or narrower
  • Perennials take 2–3 years to mature size

Recommendation: Don't be afraid to adjust spacing year 2 based on actual growth patterns.

🔗 Related Pages

Garden Design:

  • [[Water Needs Map]]
  • [[Mediterranean Bed]]
  • [[Pollinator Bed]]
  • [[Digestive Herb Bed]]

Plant Information:

  • [[Master Plant Index]]
  • [[Month-by-Month Calendar (Zone 7B)]]

Companion Planning:

  • [[Companion Guilds Index]]

Summary: Strategic height arrangement ensures every plant gets adequate sun, creates visually appealing gardens, and makes harvesting easier.
raw:
# 📏 Garden Height Map

Use this guide to arrange herbs by mature height, creating visually pleasing beds and ensuring proper sun access to all plants.

---

## Garden Height Layering (Front to Back)

Arrange plants with shortest in front, tallest in back, to prevent shading smaller plants.

---

## 🌱 Front Border (0–18 inches)

**Best for:** Edging garden beds, container planting, visual foreground

### [[Thyme]]
- Height: 6–12 inches
- Spread: 12–18 inches
- Sun: Full sun
- Water: Low
- Best placement: Along bed edge, pathway border
- Cascading over edges acceptable

### [[Oregano]]
- Height: 12–18 inches
- Spread: 18–24 inches
- Sun: Full sun
- Water: Low
- Best placement: Front but not extreme edge (spreads wide)

### [[Chamomile]]
- Height: 12–24 inches
- Spread: 12–18 inches
- Sun: Full sun to partial shade
- Water: Medium
- Best placement: Front of bed, can soften hard edges
- Note: Tends to self-seed; embrace or deadhead accordingly

### [[Marjoram]]
- Height: 12–18 inches
- Spread: 18–24 inches
- Sun: Full sun
- Water: Low to medium
- Best placement: Front-middle, not extreme edge

---

## 🌿 Middle Layer (18–36 inches)

**Best for:** Central bed placement, supporting cast of garden

### [[Lavender]]
- Height: 18–24 inches (can reach 36")
- Spread: 18–30 inches
- Sun: Full sun
- Water: Low
- Best placement: Middle, grouping 3–5 together for impact
- Variety note: Compact cultivars available (12–18")

### [[Bergamot]]
- Height: 24–36 inches
- Spread: 24–36 inches
- Sun: Full sun to partial shade
- Water: Medium to moist
- Best placement: Middle layer
- Social: Magnet for pollinators; notice increased bee activity

### [[Hyssop]]
- Height: 18–24 inches (can reach 32")
- Spread: 12–18 inches (narrow)
- Sun: Full sun
- Water: Low to medium
- Best placement: Middle, upright form nice for tight spaces

### [[Sage]]
- Height: 18–30 inches
- Spread: 18–30 inches
- Sun: Full sun
- Water: Low
- Best placement: Middle, sculptural form adds interest
- Note: Gets woody with age; prune hard in spring

### [[Lemon Balm]]
- Height: 24–36 inches
- Spread: 24–30 inches
- Sun: Full sun to partial shade
- Water: Medium
- Best placement: Middle, but contained (can spread aggressively)
- Caution: Mint family; needs containment strategy

### [[Catnip]]
- Height: 24–36 inches
- Spread: 18–24 inches
- Sun: Full sun
- Water: Low to medium
- Best placement: Middle, consider cat visitor impacts
- Note: Cats may damage plants; plant away from seating

---

## 🌾 Tall Layer (3–6 feet)

**Best for:** Back of bed, focal points, wind breaks

### [[Lovage]]
- Height: 4–6 feet
- Spread: 2–3 feet
- Sun: Full sun to partial shade
- Water: Medium (prefers consistent)
- Best placement: Back, tall focal point
- Impact: Dramatic height, hollow stems interesting
- Note: May need staking in windy spots

### [[Fennel]]
- Height: 3–5 feet
- Spread: 18–24 inches
- Sun: Full sun
- Water: Low to medium
- Best placement: Back, tall but narrow
- Uses: Culinary, traditional, attracts beneficial insects
- Caution: Allelopathic (may suppress nearby plants); give space

### [[Dill]]
- Height: 2–4 feet (varieties vary)
- Spread: 12–18 inches
- Sun: Full sun
- Water: Medium
- Best placement: Back or middle
- Note: Self-seeds readily; mark/manage accordingly
- Benefit: Attracts parasitic wasps (beneficial insects)

### [[Echinacea]]
- Height: 2–4 feet
- Spread: 18–24 inches
- Sun: Full sun
- Water: Low to medium
- Best placement: Back to middle
- Impact: Flowers on tall stems, pollinator magnet
- Blooms: Midsummer to fall

---

## 🌳 Very Tall Plants (5–8 feet, Use Sparingly)

### [[Horehound]]
- Height: 2–3 feet (less tall, but medicinal)
- Spread: 18–24 inches
- Sun: Full sun
- Water: Low
- Best placement: Tall middle or back

### [[Peppermint]] / [[Mint Family]]
- Height: 2–4 feet (if trained)
- Spread: Extremely aggressive (underground runners)
- Sun: Partial shade okay
- Water: Moist
- **CRITICAL:** Plant in containers only, or use underground barriers
- Best placement: Contained area, not open bed

---

## 📐 Sample Garden Layouts

### Small Standard Bed (4' x 8')

```
BACK
[Lovage]  [Fennel]  [Dill]
[Lavender]  [Bergamot]  [Sage]
[Thyme]  [Oregano]  [Chamomile]
FRONT
```

**Sun Access:** Taller plants don't shade shorter ones
**Spacing:** Accounts for mature spread
**Watering:** Grouped by similar needs (left: low-water; center: medium)

---

### Medium Pollinator Bed (6' x 12')

```
BACK (NORTH FACING)
[Dill] [Echinacea] [Fennel] [Lovage]

MID
[Bergamot]  [Lavender]  [Lavender]  [Sage]

FRONT (SOUTH FACING)  
[Thyme]  [Oregano]  [Chamomile]  [Marjoram]
```

**Total height range:** 6" (thyme) to 6' (lovage)
**Bloom sequence:** Spring through fall
**Pollinator value:** High (flowers at various heights all season)

---

### Vertical Container Stacking (Small Space)

**Use tiered containers:**
1. **Back (tall container, raised):** Lovage or Dill
2. **Center (medium container):** Lavender + Bergamot
3. **Front (low container):** Thyme + Oregano edge

**Benefits:**
- Maximizes space
- Improved drainage
- Easier harvesting
- Year-round portability

---

## View Lines Considerations

### Sightline Height
- **Standing eye level:** ~5'–6' (most people)
- **Plant tall herbs behind sitting areas** (to not obstruct view)
- **Plant shorter herbs in front of seating** (create frame, don't block sightline)

### Visual Impact
- Odd-numbered groupings (3, 5, 7) are more visually appealing than even
- Repetition: Plant same herb 3+ places for cohesion
- Contrast: Tall + short near each other for interest

---

## Spacing & Mature Size Table

| Plant | Min Height | Max Height | Mature Spread | Front/Mid/Back |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Thyme** | 6" | 12" | 12–18" | Front |
| **Oregano** | 12" | 18" | 18–24" | Front |
| **Chamomile** | 12" | 24" | 12–18" | Front |
| **Sage** | 18" | 30" | 18–30" | Mid |
| **Lavender** | 18" | 36" | 18–30" | Mid |
| **Bergamot** | 24" | 36" | 24–36" | Mid |
| **Dill** | 24" | 48" | 12–18" | Mid–Back |
| **Fennel** | 36" | 60" | 18–24" | Back |
| **Lovage** | 48" | 72" | 24–36" | Back |
| **Echinacea** | 24" | 48" | 18–24" | Mid–Back |

---

## Preventing Shade Problems

### Shade-Causing Mistakes
✗ Planting tall (Fennel, Lovage) in front of shorter (Thyme, Oregano)  
✗ Planting wide-spreading (Bergamot) too close to compact (Sage)  
✗ Not removing dead tall stems early spring (blocks spring growth)  
✗ Letting self-seeders (Dill, Fennel) spread indiscriminately  

### Preventing Shade
✓ Arrange front → back by height consistently  
✓ Prune taller plants in early spring for cleaner emergence  
✓ Thin Dill/Fennel seedlings or pull extras  
✓ Allow ample spacing (don't overcrowd)  
✓ Monitor and adjust first year (many plants different than expected)  

---

## Seasonal Height Changes

**Spring:** Plants emerge different heights than summer (growth continues)

**Early Summer:** Most reach mature height by June

**Late Summer:** Some taller plants may lean/sprawl (staking may help)

**Fall:** Many perennials shorten as they prepare dormancy

**Winter:** Cut back hard; minimal height

---

## First-Year Adjustment

**Note:** Newly planted herbs often grow differently than expected:
- May stay shorter first year
- May grow faster than expected
- May spread wider or narrower
- Perennials take 2–3 years to mature size

**Recommendation:** Don't be afraid to adjust spacing year 2 based on actual growth patterns.

---

## 🔗 Related Pages

**Garden Design:**
- [[Water Needs Map]]
- [[Mediterranean Bed]]
- [[Pollinator Bed]]
- [[Digestive Herb Bed]]

**Plant Information:**
- [[Master Plant Index]]
- [[Month-by-Month Calendar (Zone 7B)]]

**Companion Planning:**
- [[Companion Guilds Index]]

---

**Summary:** Strategic height arrangement ensures every plant gets adequate sun, creates visually appealing gardens, and makes harvesting easier.

title: Digestive%20Herb%20Bed
html:
🍽️ Digestive Herb Bed Design

A specialized garden bed focused on herbs that support digestive health, creating a functional apothecary garden dedicated to food as medicine.

Digestive Herb Garden Purpose

This bed brings together plants traditionally used to support:

  • Digestion and digestive function
  • Carminative action (reduces gas/bloating)
  • Antispasmodic effects (relaxes cramping)
  • Bitter toning (stimulates digestive secretions)
  • Anti-inflammatory action (soothes GI tract)

Philosophy: Grow digestive medicine you use daily, harvest fresh, use in meals and medicines.

Core Digestive Herbs

[[Fennel]]

  • Height: 3–5 feet
  • Spread: 18–24 inches
  • Placement: Back (tall, narrow)
  • Water: Low to medium
  • Flavor: Sweet, licorice-like
  • Best use: Seeds for tea, digestive support post-meal
  • Blooms: Yellow tiny umbels attracting beneficial insects
  • Perennial: Yes, lives 2–3 years typically

[[Dill]]

  • Height: 2–4 feet
  • Spread: 12–18 inches
  • Placement: Back to middle
  • Water: Medium
  • Flavor: Fresh, dill-distinctive
  • Best use: Seeds in tea, fresh leaves in cooking
  • Annual: Self-seeds readily
  • Benefit: "Dill water" traditional for infant gas

[[Anise]]

  • Height: 18–24 inches
  • Spread: 12 inches
  • Placement: Middle
  • Water: Medium
  • Flavor: Sweet, similar to fennel (milder)
  • Best use: Seeds for tea, culinary flavoring
  • Annual: Must replant each year

[[Peppermint]]

  • Height: 2–3 feet (if trained)
  • Spread: Aggressive (underground runners)
  • Placement: Container ONLY or with barriers
  • Water: Moist (loves water)
  • Flavor: Cooling, refreshing, strong
  • Best use: Fresh in meals, hot tea post-dinner
  • Perennial: Comes back vigorously
  • CRITICAL: Use 5-gallon pot or in-ground barrier to contain!

[[Cilantro]]

  • Height: 12–18 inches
  • Spread: 8–12 inches
  • Placement: Front to middle
  • Water: Regular
  • Flavor: Fresh, pungent, polarizing (love it or hate it)
  • Best use: Fresh in meals, seeds for spice
  • Annual: Cool-season herb
  • Bolting: Bolts quickly in heat (succession sow)

[[Caraway]]

  • Height: 12–24 inches
  • Spread: 12–18 inches
  • Placement: Middle
  • Water: Medium
  • Flavor: Distinctive, slightly bitter, warming
  • Best use: Seeds for tea, traditional spice
  • Biennial: First year growth, second year flowering/seed
  • Challenge: Takes 2 years to harvest seed

[[Chamomile]]

  • Height: 12–24 inches
  • Spread: 12–18 inches
  • Placement: Front to middle
  • Water: Medium
  • Flavor: Mild, slightly sweet, floral
  • Best use: Flowers dried for tea, soothing
  • Annual: Self-seeds readily
  • Bonus: Anti-inflammatory GI lining

Supporting Digestive Herbs

[[Marjoram]]

  • Height: 12–18 inches
  • Placement: Front-middle
  • Digestion: Mild action, warming
  • Best use: Culinary herb, adds to digestive blends
  • Flavor: Subtle, sweet

[[Oregano]]

  • Height: 12–18 inches
  • Placement: Front-middle
  • Digestion: Stimulates digestive secretions
  • Best use: Culinary herb, fresh or dried
  • Antimicrobial: Bonus benefit

[[Summer Savory]]

  • Height: 12–24 inches (depending on type)
  • Placement: Middle
  • Digestion: Excellent carminative
  • Best use: Fresh with legumes (reduces gas!)
  • Uses: Culinary (meat, beans), medicinal tea

📐 Digestive Bed Design (4' x 12')

BACK (NORTH)
[Fennel] [Dill] [Dill-2nd sowing]

MIDDLE
[Anise]  [Caraway]  [Summer Savory]  [Cilantro]

FRONT (SOUTH)
[Chamomile] [Marjoram] [Oregano]

Total plants: 12–15

Note on Peppermint: Plant separately in 5-gallon pot near (not in) the bed

Seasonal consideration:

  • Cilantro bolt quickly in heat (succession sow every 3 weeks March–April, August–September)
  • Anise + Dill are annuals (replant each year)
  • Fennel, Caraway, Herb perennials provide continuity

Planting Timeline (Zone 7B)

Early Spring (March–April)

  • Direct sow: Cilantro, Chamomile, Caraway
  • Plant: Peppermint in separate container
  • Start indoors: Can be done but not necessary for most

Late Spring (April–May)

  • After last frost (April 15)
  • Direct sow: Anise, Dill (multiple sowings 3 weeks apart)
  • Plant: Any purchased herbs or starts

Early Fall (August–September)

  • Cilantro again: Prefers cool season
  • Dill: Succession sow for fall harvest
  • Chamomile: Can overseed for next year

Winter

  • Peppermint dormant in container
  • Mulch perennials lightly
  • Plan next year

Succession Planting for Continuous Harvest

Cilantro Strategy

  • Sow cilantro every 2–3 weeks (March–April, August–September)
  • This creates continuous supply
  • Bolts quickly in summer heat (stop sowing June–July)
  • Resumes August

Dill Strategy

  • First sowing: Late April
  • Second sowing: Mid-May
  • Third sowing: August–September
  • Creates overlapping maturity for extended harvest

Constant Fresh Leaves

  • Early season: Fresh Cilantro, early Dill
  • Summer: Peppermint (thrives in heat), Oregano, Marjoram, Summer Savory
  • Fall: Cilantro + Dill second crops, Chamomile flowers

Garden Care & Harvesting

Spring Management

  • Thin seedlings (crowded = weak plants)
  • Water regularly (establishing)
  • Pinch back leggy growth
  • No harvesting first month (let establish)

Summer Management

  • Pinch flower buds if you want leaves (otherwise let some flower)
  • Harvest from top 1/3 of plant
  • Peppermint: Harvest vigorously (containment tactic)
  • Cilantro: Will bolt; accept and harvest before OR pull + replant
  • Regular harvesting = bushier plants

Fall Management

  • Harvest cilantro + dill second crops
  • Reduce watering as temps cool
  • Begin drying herbs for winter
  • Chamomile flowers ready

Winter Management

  • Most dormant
  • Peppermint minimal watering (in container)
  • Plan: Order seeds for spring
  • Dry harvested herbs

Culinary & Medicinal Use

Post-Meal Digestive Tea

Recipe:

  • 1 tbsp fresh Fennel + Dill
  • OR ½ tbsp each Caraway, Anise, Marjoram
  • 1 cup hot water, steep 10–15 minutes

Effect: Reduces bloating, supports digestion, pleasant ritual

Fresh-Herb Meal Approach

  • Use Cilantro in salads, salsas
  • Add Oregano, Marjoram, Summer Savory to meals
  • Use Dill in fish dishes
  • Consume herbs with meals for ongoing digestive support
  • Culinary amounts = gentle, regular benefit

Digestive Syrup

  • Infuse Fennel + Ginger in oil (4 weeks)
  • Make syrup with honey
  • Take 1 tsp after meals
  • Stronger than tea, pleasant taste

Digestive Tincture

  • Tincture Fennel + Caraway in alcohol (1:5 ratio)
  • Take 20 drops before meals
  • Concentrated, long shelf life

Design & Aesthetics

Height & Visual Interest

  • Back: Tall Fennel + Dill + Anise (varying shades green + yellow flowers)
  • Middle: Shorter herbs (rolling color gradient)
  • Front: Low Chamomile (white flowers soften edge)

Color During Blooming

  • Yellow umbrella flowers (Fennel, Dill, Anise) = cheerful
  • White Chamomile daisy flowers = delicate
  • Subtle green foliage focus (this bed is green-based)

Fragrance as Benefit

  • Walking through bed = amazing aromatic experience
  • Crush leaves = strong spice / herbal aroma
  • Sensory garden experience

Companion Planting with Digestive Guild

Natural Groupings

The digestive herbs naturally support each other:

  • Similar sun needs (full sun to partial shade)
  • Seeds/flowers attract beneficial insects together
  • Self-seeding creates natural renewal

What to Avoid in This Bed

✗ Mints outside containers (will overrun)
✗ Low-water Mediterranean herbs (water needs differ)
✗ Shade-lovers (these need sun)
✗ Heavy feeders (need balanced soil)

Good Nearby Additions (Not in Bed)

  • Container of Peppermint (controlled)
  • Container of Lemon Balm (adjacent nervine)
  • Oregano (slight different guild but compatible)

Soil & Watering

Soil Preparation

  • Loose, well-draining
  • Rich with organic matter (compost)
  • Slightly moist-preferring (vs. drought herbs)
  • pH neutral (6.5–7.5)

Watering Schedule

  • Establishment (first month): 2–3x weekly (keep moist)
  • Growing season (summer): 1–2x weekly depending on rainfall
  • Not as drought-tolerant as Mediterranean herbs
  • Don't over-water = root rot

Mulching

  • 2–3 inches straw or wood chips
  • Helps retain moisture
  • Keeps soil cooler (Cilantro appreciates this)
  • Suppresses weeds

Pest & Disease Management

Prevention First

  • Healthy plants resist problems
  • Good air circulation (proper spacing)
  • Water at soil level (not foliage)
  • Remove diseased leaves promptly

Common Issues

Powdery mildew (white coating)

  • Cause: Moisture on foliage + humidity
  • Prevention: Don't overhead water, space well
  • Solution: Remove affected leaves, improve air flow

Slugs/snails (on Cilantro)

  • Cause: Cool, moist conditions
  • Prevention: Hand-pick in early morning
  • Solution: Beer traps, copper tape (organic)
  • Less issue in summer, more in spring/fall

Aphids (rare, usually on Dill)

  • Prevention: Encourage beneficial insects (good habitat)
  • Solution: Spray water forcefully (knocks off), neem oil
  • Usually not needed; beneficial insects handle naturally

Year-Round Digestive Herb Calendar

Month Task Harvest
March Plant cilantro, chamomile, caraway No (establishing)
April Plant annuals, prepare containers Minimal (cilantro if grown)
May Sow successive dill First dill, cilantro
June Maintenance, thin seedlings Fresh herbs, flowers
July Support in heat, minimal water Peppermint, Summer Savory
August Second cilantro + dill sow Fresh harvest, start drying
September Fall planting, harvest heavily Cilantro, flowers, seed heads
October Reduce watering, prepare dormancy Final harvests, seeds/drying
November–February Minimal care, plan spring Use dried herbs

Dried Herb Storage for Winter

How to Dry

  1. Harvest in morning after dew dries
  2. Bundle with twine, hang in cool dark place
  3. 2–3 weeks drying typical
  4. Leaves should crumble when fully dry

Storage

  • Glass jars, sealed
  • Cool, dark cupboard
  • 1–2 years good quality
  • Label with herb + date

Winter Use

  • Dried tea infusions (strong + shelf-stable)
  • Tinctures (if making)
  • Cooking

🔗 Related Pages

Medicinal Information:

  • [[Digestive Support]] (comprehensive action guide)

Garden Design:

  • [[Height Map]]
  • [[Water Needs Map]]
  • [[Mediterranean Bed]]
  • [[Pollinator Bed]]

Herb Information:

  • [[Master Plant Index]]
  • [[Companion Guilds Index]] - Digestive Guild

Preparation Methods:

  • [[Tea Infusion Guide]]
  • [[Tincture Guide]]
  • [[Syrup Guide]]

Seasonal Planning:

  • [[Month-by-Month Calendar (Zone 7B)]]

Summary: A digestive herb garden brings functional medicine directly to your table. Grow these herbs, harvest fresh, and integrate digestive support directly into daily meals—the most sustainable herbalism approach.

# 🍽️ Digestive Herb Bed Design

A specialized garden bed focused on herbs that support digestive health, creating a functional apothecary garden dedicated to food as medicine.

---

## Core Digestive Herbs

### [[Fennel]]

### [[Peppermint]]

- Flavor: Cooling, refreshing, strong

- **CRITICAL:** Use 5-gallon pot or in-ground barrier to contain!

### [[Cilantro]]

- Spread: 8–12 inches
- Placement: Front to middle
- Water: Regular
- Flavor: Fresh, pungent, polarizing (love it or hate it)
- Best use: Fresh in meals, seeds for spice
- Annual: Cool-season herb

### [[Caraway]]
- Height: 12–24 inches

### [[Chamomile]]

---

## 📐 Digestive Bed Design (4' x 12')

```
BACK (NORTH)
[Fennel] [Dill] [Dill-2nd sowing]

MIDDLE
[Anise]  [Caraway]  [Summer Savory]  [Cilantro]

FRONT (SOUTH)
[Chamomile] [Marjoram] [Oregano]
```

**Total plants:** 12–15

**Note on Peppermint:** Plant separately in 5-gallon pot near (not in) the bed

**Seasonal consideration:**

### Late Spring (April–May)
