---

## Succession Planting for Continuous Harvest

### Cilantro Strategy
- Sow cilantro every 2–3 weeks (March–April, August–September)
- This creates continuous supply
- Bolts quickly in summer heat (stop sowing June–July)
- Resumes August

### Dill Strategy
- First sowing: Late April
- Second sowing: Mid-May
- Third sowing: August–September
- Creates overlapping maturity for extended harvest

### Constant Fresh Leaves
- Early season: Fresh Cilantro, early Dill
- Summer: Peppermint (thrives in heat), Oregano, Marjoram, Summer Savory
- Fall: Cilantro + Dill second crops, Chamomile flowers

---

## Garden Care & Harvesting

### Spring Management
- Thin seedlings (crowded = weak plants)
- Water regularly (establishing)
- Pinch back leggy growth
- No harvesting first month (let establish)

### Summer Management
- Pinch flower buds if you want leaves (otherwise let some flower)
- Harvest from top 1/3 of plant
- Peppermint: Harvest vigorously (containment tactic)
- Cilantro: Will bolt; accept and harvest before OR pull + replant
- Regular harvesting = bushier plants

### Fall Management
- Harvest cilantro + dill second crops
- Reduce watering as temps cool
- Begin drying herbs for winter
- Chamomile flowers ready

### Winter Management
- Most dormant
- Peppermint minimal watering (in container)
- Plan: Order seeds for spring
- Dry harvested herbs

---

## Culinary & Medicinal Use

### Post-Meal Digestive Tea
**Recipe:**
- 1 tbsp fresh Fennel + Dill
- OR ½ tbsp each Caraway, Anise, Marjoram
- 1 cup hot water, steep 10–15 minutes

**Effect:** Reduces bloating, supports digestion, pleasant ritual

### Fresh-Herb Meal Approach
- Use Cilantro in salads, salsas
- Add Oregano, Marjoram, Summer Savory to meals
- Use Dill in fish dishes
- Consume herbs with meals for ongoing digestive support
- Culinary amounts = gentle, regular benefit

### Digestive Syrup
- Infuse Fennel + Ginger in oil (4 weeks)
- Make syrup with honey
- Take 1 tsp after meals
- Stronger than tea, pleasant taste

### Digestive Tincture
- Tincture Fennel + Caraway in alcohol (1:5 ratio)
- Take 20 drops before meals
- Concentrated, long shelf life

---

## Design & Aesthetics

### Height & Visual Interest
- Back: Tall Fennel + Dill + Anise (varying shades green + yellow flowers)
- Middle: Shorter herbs (rolling color gradient)
- Front: Low Chamomile (white flowers soften edge)

### Color During Blooming
- Yellow umbrella flowers (Fennel, Dill, Anise) = cheerful
- White Chamomile daisy flowers = delicate
- Subtle green foliage focus (this bed is green-based)

### Fragrance as Benefit
- Walking through bed = amazing aromatic experience
- Crush leaves = strong spice / herbal aroma
- Sensory garden experience

---

## Companion Planting with Digestive Guild

### Natural Groupings
The digestive herbs naturally support each other:
- Similar sun needs (full sun to partial shade)
- Seeds/flowers attract beneficial insects together
- Self-seeding creates natural renewal

### What to Avoid in This Bed
✗ Mints outside containers (will overrun)  
✗ Low-water Mediterranean herbs (water needs differ)  
✗ Shade-lovers (these need sun)  
✗ Heavy feeders (need balanced soil)  

### Good Nearby Additions (Not in Bed)
- Container of Peppermint (controlled)
- Container of Lemon Balm (adjacent nervine)
- Oregano (slight different guild but compatible)

---

## Soil & Watering

### Soil Preparation
- Loose, well-draining
- Rich with organic matter (compost)
- Slightly moist-preferring (vs. drought herbs)
- pH neutral (6.5–7.5)

### Watering Schedule
- Establishment (first month): 2–3x weekly (keep moist)
- Growing season (summer): 1–2x weekly depending on rainfall
- Not as drought-tolerant as Mediterranean herbs
- Don't over-water = root rot

### Mulching
- 2–3 inches straw or wood chips
- Helps retain moisture
- Keeps soil cooler (Cilantro appreciates this)
- Suppresses weeds

---

## Pest & Disease Management

### Prevention First
- Healthy plants resist problems
- Good air circulation (proper spacing)
- Water at soil level (not foliage)
- Remove diseased leaves promptly

### Common Issues
**Powdery mildew** (white coating)
- Cause: Moisture on foliage + humidity
- Prevention: Don't overhead water, space well
- Solution: Remove affected leaves, improve air flow

**Slugs/snails** (on Cilantro)
- Cause: Cool, moist conditions
- Prevention: Hand-pick in early morning
- Solution: Beer traps, copper tape (organic)
- Less issue in summer, more in spring/fall

**Aphids** (rare, usually on Dill)
- Prevention: Encourage beneficial insects (good habitat)
- Solution: Spray water forcefully (knocks off), neem oil
- Usually not needed; beneficial insects handle naturally

---

## Year-Round Digestive Herb Calendar

| Month | Task | Harvest |
|---|---|---|
| **March** | Plant cilantro, chamomile, caraway | No (establishing) |
| **April** | Plant annuals, prepare containers | Minimal (cilantro if grown) |
| **May** | Sow successive dill | First dill, cilantro |
| **June** | Maintenance, thin seedlings | Fresh herbs, flowers |
| **July** | Support in heat, minimal water | Peppermint, Summer Savory |
| **August** | Second cilantro + dill sow | Fresh harvest, start drying |
| **September** | Fall planting, harvest heavily | Cilantro, flowers, seed heads |
| **October** | Reduce watering, prepare dormancy | Final harvests, seeds/drying |
| **November–February** | Minimal care, plan spring | Use dried herbs |

---

## Dried Herb Storage for Winter

### How to Dry
1. Harvest in morning after dew dries
2. Bundle with twine, hang in cool dark place
3. 2–3 weeks drying typical
4. Leaves should crumble when fully dry

### Storage
- Glass jars, sealed
- Cool, dark cupboard
- 1–2 years good quality
- Label with herb + date

### Winter Use
- Dried tea infusions (strong + shelf-stable)
- Tinctures (if making)
- Cooking

---

## 🔗 Related Pages

**Medicinal Information:**
- [[Digestive Support]] (comprehensive action guide)

**Garden Design:**
- [[Height Map]]
- [[Water Needs Map]]
- [[Mediterranean Bed]]
- [[Pollinator Bed]]

**Herb Information:**
- [[Master Plant Index]]
- [[Companion Guilds Index]] - Digestive Guild

**Preparation Methods:**
- [[Tea Infusion Guide]]
- [[Tincture Guide]]
- [[Syrup Guide]]

**Seasonal Planning:**
- [[Month-by-Month Calendar (Zone 7B)]]

---

**Summary:** A digestive herb garden brings functional medicine directly to your table. Grow these herbs, harvest fresh, and integrate digestive support directly into daily meals—the most sustainable herbalism approach.

title: Tincture%20Guide
html:
🧪 Tincture Guide

A complete guide to making and using herbal tinctures, concentrated extracts that preserve herbal medicine effectively for months to years.

What is a Tincture?

A tincture is an alcohol-based extract where herbs are macerated (soaked) in alcohol, extracting water-soluble and oil-soluble compounds into the liquid. The result is a shelf-stable, concentrated herbal preparation.

Advantages:

  • Long shelf life (3–10 years)
  • Highly concentrated (small dose = strong effect)
  • Fast absorption (taken sublingually)
  • Preserves volatile oils better than water infusions
  • Easy to travel with

Best for: Most herbs, especially potent herbs

Not ideal for: Large-quantity daily use (cost inefficient vs. tea)

Basic Tincture Chemistry

Alcohol as Extractant

Why alcohol?

  • Extracts both water-soluble and oil-soluble compounds
  • Acts as preservative (prevents spoilage)
  • Faster absorption (small dose, concentrated effect)
  • Long shelf life

Alcohol strengths:

  • High proof (95–190): Best for fresh herbs, drugs
  • Moderate proof (80): Best for dried herbs, balanced extraction
  • Lower proof (40–60): Gentler, suitable for children/sensitive individuals

Extraction Ratios

These indicate herb weight to alcohol volume:

1:2 Ratio (Fresh Herbs)

  • 1 pound fresh herb : 2 quarts alcohol
  • More concentrated
  • For very potent fresh herbs
  • Burns/stimulating plants

1:5 Ratio (Dried Herbs)

  • 1 pound dried herb : 5 quarts alcohol
  • Standard ratio for dried material
  • Most common, easiest
  • General herbs

1:10 Ratio (Very Gentle)

  • 1 pound dried herb : 10 quarts alcohol
  • Most dilute
  • For delicate herbs or sensitive individuals
  • Children's tinctures

Tincture Making: Step-by-Step

Equipment Needed

Essential:

  • Glass jar with tight-fitting lid (quart-sized or larger)
  • Strainer or fine mesh colander
  • Cheesecloth or coffee filters
  • Measuring cups & spoons
  • Bottles for storage (dark glass, dropper bottles preferred)
  • Labels

Optional:

  • Kitchen scale (for accuracy)
  • Mortar & pestle (for breaking up herbs)
  • Coffee grinder (fine grinding)

Method 1: Fresh Herb Tincture (1:2 Ratio)

Best for: [[Hyssop]], [[Echinacea]], [[Catnip]], [[Lemon Balm]] fresh

Ingredients

  • Fresh herb, finely chopped (enough to fill jar halfway)
  • High-proof alcohol (95–190 proof, like everclear) to cover herbs by 2 inches

Process

  1. Chop herb finely to increase surface area for extraction

  2. Pack jar loosely with herb material

  3. Pour alcohol over herbs until covered by 2 inches

  4. Seal jar tightly with lid

  5. Label jar with:

    • Herb name
    • Plant part (leaf, root, flower)
    • Type of alcohol
    • Date started
    • Target finish date (4–6 weeks)

  6. Macerate (soak) in cool, dark place for 4–6 weeks

    • Shake jar vigorously 1–2x daily (increases extraction)
    • After first 2 weeks, can reduce to once daily

  7. Strain thoroughly after 4–6 weeks:

    • Pour through strainer lined with cheesecloth
    • Press herb material to extract remaining liquid
    • Discard herb matter
    • Filter through fine strainer again if cloudiness remains

  8. Bottle & store:

    • Use dark glass bottles with dropper caps
    • Label with herb, ratio, and date made
    • Store in cool, dark place

Shelf Life

5–10 years (alcohol preserves indefinitely)

Method 2: Dried Herb Tincture (1:5 Ratio)

Best for: Most dried herbs you've grown or purchased

Ingredients

  • Dried herb (enough to fill jar 1/3)
  • Moderate-proof alcohol (80 proof, like vodka, or higher)

Process

  1. Measure herb:

    • If using scale: 1 oz dried herb per 5 oz alcohol
    • By volume: Fill 1/3 of jar with herb

  2. Break up herbs slightly (don't powder):

    • Rough chop or crush slightly
    • Increases surface area

  3. Add alcohol:

    • Pour until herbs covered by 2 inches
    • Dried herbs absorb liquid; fill generously

  4. Seal & label (same as fresh method)

  5. Macerate 4–weeks:

    • Shake daily for first 2 weeks
    • Shake 1–2x per week after
    • Watch alcohol level (dried herbs expand then compress)
    • Add more alcohol if herbs absorb and become exposed

  6. Strain (same as fresh):

    • After 4 weeks, strain thoroughly
    • Press herb material to extract
    • Filter if cloudy

  7. Bottle & store in dark glass bottles

Shelf Life

5–10 years

Alcohol Selection

Everclear (95–190 proof)

Pros: Highest extraction, long shelf life, potent
Cons: Not available everywhere (some states), taste harsh, too strong for young children
Best for: Potent fresh herbs, professional-strength tinctures

Vodka (80 proof)

Pros: Readily available, neutral taste, appropriate alcohol content for dried herbs, reasonable cost
Cons: Slightly less extraction than high-proof, doesn't preserve quite as long
Best for: Standard herb tinctures, dried herbs, most accessible option

Brandy (80 proof)

Pros: Readily available, nice taste, good for herbs meant for pleasure (nervines, digestive)
Cons: Doesn't extract quite as well as neutral spirits, cost
Best for: Culinary/medicinal blends meant to taste pleasant

Whiskey/Bourbon (80+ proof)

Pros: Available, adds warming property
Cons: Flavor dominates
Best for: Warming herbs, if you like whiskey taste

NOT Recommended

  • Wine (too low alcohol, spoils easily)
  • Beer (too low alcohol)
  • Store-brand extracts (often not pure alcohol)

Finished Tincture Dosage

Standard Dose

For adults:

  • Acute (sudden need): 20–30 drops, every 2–4 hours
  • Therapeutic (regular use): 20–30 drops, 2–3x daily
  • Prevention/maintenance: 20 drops, 1x daily

Dose Administration

Sublingually (under tongue):

  • Fastest absorption (2–3 minutes to bloodstream)
  • Most efficient
  • Hold under tongue 30 seconds before swallowing
  • Takes: Strong taste, burn for high-proof tinctures

In water:

  • Add dose to 2 oz warm water
  • Drink quickly
  • Masks taste, slower absorption
  • Good for children

In juice or tea:

  • Mix into favorite beverage
  • Completely masks herbal taste
  • Slightly slower absorption
  • Most palatable

Children's Dosing

General rule: 1 drop per year of age

  • 2-year-old: 2 drops
  • 5-year-old: 5 drops
  • 12-year-old: 12 drops
  • Adolescent/adult: 20–30 drops

Or use glycerin tinctures (alcohol-free) for young children

Testing & Quality

Signs of Good Tincture

✓ Darker color (extraction occurred)
✓ Strong herbal aroma
✓ Taste matches herb (bitter, pungent, etc.)
✓ Clear (or slightly cloudy, both fine)
✓ No mold, cloudiness after months (alcohol prevents)

Signs of Problem/Bad Tincture

✗ Pale/light color (under-extracted)
✗ No aroma (weak)
✗ Moldy smell or taste
✗ Separated into layers (failed extraction)
✗ Cloudiness with moisture inside bottle

Tincture Shelf Life & Storage

Optimal Storage

  • Temperature: Cool (50–70°F), consistent
  • Light: Dark place (dark glass bottles help)
  • Container: Glass, not plastic (alcohol dissolves some plastics)
  • Seal: Airtight, dropper cap

Shelf Life

  • High-proof (95+ alcohol): 10+ years
  • Standard (80 proof): 5–10 years
  • Low-proof (40–60): 3–5 years

Note: Effectiveness may diminish slightly over years, but generally remains viable for many years if properly stored.

Popular Tinctures for Herbalists

Digestive Blend Tincture

Herbs:

  • 2 parts dried [[Fennel]] seed
  • 1 part dried [[Ginger]]
  • 1 part dried Peppermint
  • ½ part [[Caraway]] seed

Ratio: 1:5 (dried) in vodka
Dose: 20 drops before meals
Use: Digestive support, prevents bloating

Respiratory Tincture

Herbs:

  • 2 parts dried [[Thyme]]
  • 1 part dried [[Horehound]]
  • 1 part dried [[Echinacea]] root
  • ½ part dried [[Ginger]]

Ratio: 1:5 (dried) in vodka
Dose: 30 drops, 3–4x daily during respiratory support

Nervine Tincture

Herbs:

  • 2 parts dried [[Lavender]]
  • 1 part dried [[Lemon Balm]]
  • 1 part dried [[Catnip]]
  • ½ part [[Peppermint]]

Ratio: 1:5 (dried) in brandy (nicer taste)
Dose: 20–30 drops, 2–3x daily or as needed
Use: Calming, sleep support

Immune Support Tincture

Herbs:

  • 2 parts fresh [[Echinacea]] (leaf + root)
  • 1 part fresh [[Thyme]]
  • ½ part fresh [[Ginger]]

Ratio: 1:2 (fresh) in high-proof alcohol
Dose: 30 drops, 3–4x daily during infection risk
Use: Seasonal immune support

Advantages Over Other Preparations

Preparation Shelf Life Dose Size Taste Cost
Tincture 5–10 years Small drops Strong Medium
Tea 3–5 days 1 cup Mild Low
Dried herbs 1–2 years Variable Mild Very low
Capsules 1–2 years Multiple None Medium

Making Alcohol-Free Tinctures (Glycerin)

Alternative for:

  • Young children
  • Alcohol-free preference
  • Religious/health reasons

Method

Ingredients:

  • Dried herb (1 part by weight)
  • Vegetable glycerin (5 parts by volume)

Process:

  1. Mix herb + glycerin in jar (glycerin dissolves more slowly, so use equal ratio or slightly more herb)
  2. Seal tightly
  3. Macerate 4–6 weeks, shake daily
  4. Strain thoroughly
  5. Bottle, label, store

Dose: Same as alcohol tinctures (20–30 drops)

Shelf life: 2–3 years (glycerin is more prone to fermentation over time)

Taste: Sweet, children often prefer

Dosage Frequency Chart

Situation Frequency Duration Notes
Acute (sudden illness) Every 2–4 hours 3–7 days Strong action, more frequent
Therapeutic (treatment) 2–3x daily 2–4 weeks Regular remedy approach
Maintenance (prevention) 1x daily Ongoing Gentle, consistent support
Winter seasonal 3x daily Oct–Apr Preventive during season
As-needed (nervine) When stressed As needed Take when situation arises

Storage Bottles & Labels

Ideal Bottle

  • Dark amber or blue glass (blocks light)
  • 1–2 oz with dropper cap
  • Airtight, glass dropper (not plastic)
  • Labeled with:
    • Herb name(s)
    • Ratio & alcohol type
    • Date made/finish date
    • Dosage instructions
    • Intended use

Labeling Template

[[Herbal Name]] Tincture
1:5 Dried in Vodka
Date Made: [Month/Year]
Date to Use By: [Expiration]
Dose: 20–30 drops, 2–3x daily
Use: [Digestive/Calming/Respiratory/etc.]
Warnings: [Any specific cautions]

Troubleshooting Tinctures

Problem Cause Solution
Weak extraction (pale, no aroma) Under-maceration or low alcohol Shake daily, macerate longer (6–8 weeks), use higher-proof alcohol next time
Mold/cloudiness Low alcohol or contamination Not normally viable; discard and start fresh
Separation/layers Failed extraction or contamination Discard; ensure jars are clean, alcohol is quality
Evaporation Seal not tight or old alcohol Check lid seals; store tightly; not dangerous, just more concentrated
Sediment at bottom Fine plant particles Normal; shake before use or filter through coffee filter

🔗 Related Pages

Individual Herb Profiles: [[Master Plant Index]]

Medicinal Actions:

  • [[Digestive Support]]
  • [[Nervine & Calming]]
  • [[Respiratory Support]]
  • [[Antimicrobial Herbs]]

Other Preparations:

  • [[Tea Infusion Guide]]
  • [[Salve Guide]]
  • [[Syrup Guide]]

Summary: Tinctures are powerful, shelf-stable medicine that every serious herbalist should master. Start with dried herbs and vodka—simple, accessible, and effective.

# 🧪 Tincture Guide

A complete guide to making and using herbal tinctures, concentrated extracts that preserve herbal medicine effectively for months to years.

---

## What is a Tincture?

A tincture is an alcohol-based extract where herbs are macerated (soaked) in alcohol, extracting water-soluble and oil-soluble compounds into the liquid. The result is a shelf-stable, concentrated herbal preparation.

**Advantages:**
- Long shelf life (3–10 years)
- Highly concentrated (small dose = strong effect)
- Fast absorption (taken sublingually)
- Preserves volatile oils better than water infusions
- Easy to travel with

**Best for:** Most herbs, especially potent herbs

**Not ideal for:** Large-quantity daily use (cost inefficient vs. tea)

---

## Basic Tincture Chemistry

### Alcohol as Extractant

**Why alcohol?**
- Extracts both water-soluble and oil-soluble compounds
- Acts as preservative (prevents spoilage)
- Faster absorption (small dose, concentrated effect)
- Long shelf life

**Alcohol strengths:**
- **High proof (95–190):** Best for fresh herbs, drugs
- **Moderate proof (80):** Best for dried herbs, balanced extraction
- **Lower proof (40–60):** Gentler, suitable for children/sensitive individuals

### Extraction Ratios

These indicate herb weight to alcohol volume:

**1:2 Ratio (Fresh Herbs)**
- 1 pound fresh herb : 2 quarts alcohol
- More concentrated
- For very potent fresh herbs
- Burns/stimulating plants

**1:5 Ratio (Dried Herbs)**
- 1 pound dried herb : 5 quarts alcohol
- Standard ratio for dried material
- Most common, easiest
- General herbs

**1:10 Ratio (Very Gentle)**
- 1 pound dried herb : 10 quarts alcohol
- Most dilute
- For delicate herbs or sensitive individuals
- Children's tinctures

---

## Tincture Making: Step-by-Step

### Equipment Needed

**Essential:**
- Glass jar with tight-fitting lid (quart-sized or larger)
- Strainer or fine mesh colander
- Cheesecloth or coffee filters
- Measuring cups & spoons
- Bottles for storage (dark glass, dropper bottles preferred)
- Labels

**Optional:**
- Kitchen scale (for accuracy)
- Mortar & pestle (for breaking up herbs)
- Coffee grinder (fine grinding)

---

### Method 1: Fresh Herb Tincture (1:2 Ratio)

**Best for:** [[Hyssop]], [[Echinacea]], [[Catnip]], [[Lemon Balm]] fresh

#### Ingredients
- Fresh herb, finely chopped (enough to fill jar halfway)
- High-proof alcohol (95–190 proof, like everclear) to cover herbs by 2 inches

#### Process

1. **Chop herb finely** to increase surface area for extraction

2. **Pack jar** loosely with herb material

3. **Pour alcohol** over herbs until covered by 2 inches

4. **Seal jar tightly** with lid

5. **Label jar** with:
   - Herb name
   - Plant part (leaf, root, flower)
   - Type of alcohol
   - Date started
   - Target finish date (4–6 weeks)

6. **Macerate** (soak) in cool, dark place for 4–6 weeks
   - Shake jar vigorously 1–2x daily (increases extraction)
   - After first 2 weeks, can reduce to once daily

7. **Strain thoroughly** after 4–6 weeks:
   - Pour through strainer lined with cheesecloth
   - Press herb material to extract remaining liquid
   - Discard herb matter
   - Filter through fine strainer again if cloudiness remains

8. **Bottle & store**:
   - Use dark glass bottles with dropper caps
   - Label with herb, ratio, and date made
   - Store in cool, dark place

#### Shelf Life
5–10 years (alcohol preserves indefinitely)

---

### Method 2: Dried Herb Tincture (1:5 Ratio)

**Best for:** Most dried herbs you've grown or purchased

#### Ingredients
- Dried herb (enough to fill jar 1/3)
- Moderate-proof alcohol (80 proof, like vodka, or higher)

#### Process

1. **Measure herb**:
   - If using scale: 1 oz dried herb per 5 oz alcohol
   - By volume: Fill 1/3 of jar with herb

2. **Break up herbs** slightly (don't powder):
   - Rough chop or crush slightly
   - Increases surface area

3. **Add alcohol**:
   - Pour until herbs covered by 2 inches
   - Dried herbs absorb liquid; fill generously

4. **Seal & label** (same as fresh method)

5. **Macerate** 4–weeks:
   - Shake daily for first 2 weeks
   - Shake 1–2x per week after
   - Watch alcohol level (dried herbs expand then compress)
   - Add more alcohol if herbs absorb and become exposed

6. **Strain** (same as fresh):
   - After 4 weeks, strain thoroughly
   - Press herb material to extract
   - Filter if cloudy

7. **Bottle & store** in dark glass bottles

#### Shelf Life
5–10 years

---

## Alcohol Selection

### Everclear (95–190 proof)
**Pros:** Highest extraction, long shelf life, potent  
**Cons:** Not available everywhere (some states), taste harsh, too strong for young children  
**Best for:** Potent fresh herbs, professional-strength tinctures  

### Vodka (80 proof)
**Pros:** Readily available, neutral taste, appropriate alcohol content for dried herbs, reasonable cost  
**Cons:** Slightly less extraction than high-proof, doesn't preserve quite as long  
**Best for:** Standard herb tinctures, dried herbs, most accessible option  

### Brandy (80 proof)
**Pros:** Readily available, nice taste, good for herbs meant for pleasure (nervines, digestive)  
**Cons:** Doesn't extract quite as well as neutral spirits, cost  
**Best for:** Culinary/medicinal blends meant to taste pleasant  

### Whiskey/Bourbon (80+ proof)
**Pros:** Available, adds warming property  
**Cons:** Flavor dominates  
**Best for:** Warming herbs, if you like whiskey taste  

### NOT Recommended
- Wine (too low alcohol, spoils easily)
- Beer (too low alcohol)
- Store-brand extracts (often not pure alcohol)

---

## Finished Tincture Dosage

### Standard Dose

**For adults:**
- **Acute (sudden need):** 20–30 drops, every 2–4 hours
- **Therapeutic (regular use):** 20–30 drops, 2–3x daily
- **Prevention/maintenance:** 20 drops, 1x daily

### Dose Administration

**Sublingually (under tongue):**
- Fastest absorption (2–3 minutes to bloodstream)
- Most efficient
- Hold under tongue 30 seconds before swallowing
- Takes: Strong taste, burn for high-proof tinctures

**In water:**
- Add dose to 2 oz warm water
- Drink quickly
- Masks taste, slower absorption
- Good for children

**In juice or tea:**
- Mix into favorite beverage
- Completely masks herbal taste
- Slightly slower absorption
- Most palatable

### Children's Dosing

**General rule:** 1 drop per year of age

- **2-year-old:** 2 drops
- **5-year-old:** 5 drops
- **12-year-old:** 12 drops
- **Adolescent/adult:** 20–30 drops

**Or use glycerin tinctures (alcohol-free) for young children**

---

## Testing & Quality

### Signs of Good Tincture
✓ Darker color (extraction occurred)  
✓ Strong herbal aroma  
✓ Taste matches herb (bitter, pungent, etc.)  
✓ Clear (or slightly cloudy, both fine)  
✓ No mold, cloudiness after months (alcohol prevents)  

### Signs of Problem/Bad Tincture
✗ Pale/light color (under-extracted)  
✗ No aroma (weak)  
✗ Moldy smell or taste  
✗ Separated into layers (failed extraction)  
✗ Cloudiness with moisture inside bottle  

---

## Tincture Shelf Life & Storage

### Optimal Storage
- **Temperature:** Cool (50–70°F), consistent
- **Light:** Dark place (dark glass bottles help)
- **Container:** Glass, not plastic (alcohol dissolves some plastics)
- **Seal:** Airtight, dropper cap

### Shelf Life
- **High-proof (95+ alcohol):** 10+ years
- **Standard (80 proof):** 5–10 years
- **Low-proof (40–60):** 3–5 years

**Note:** Effectiveness may diminish slightly over years, but generally remains viable for many years if properly stored.

---

## Popular Tinctures for Herbalists

### Digestive Blend Tincture
**Herbs:**
- 2 parts dried [[Fennel]] seed
- 1 part dried [[Ginger]]
- 1 part dried Peppermint
- ½ part [[Caraway]] seed

**Ratio:** 1:5 (dried) in vodka  
**Dose:** 20 drops before meals  
**Use:** Digestive support, prevents bloating  

### Respiratory Tincture
**Herbs:**
- 2 parts dried [[Thyme]]
- 1 part dried [[Horehound]]
- 1 part dried [[Echinacea]] root
- ½ part dried [[Ginger]]

**Ratio:** 1:5 (dried) in vodka  
**Dose:** 30 drops, 3–4x daily during respiratory support  

### Nervine Tincture
**Herbs:**
- 2 parts dried [[Lavender]]
- 1 part dried [[Lemon Balm]]
- 1 part dried [[Catnip]]
- ½ part [[Peppermint]]

**Ratio:** 1:5 (dried) in brandy (nicer taste)  
**Dose:** 20–30 drops, 2–3x daily or as needed  
**Use:** Calming, sleep support  

### Immune Support Tincture
**Herbs:**
- 2 parts fresh [[Echinacea]] (leaf + root)
- 1 part fresh [[Thyme]]
- ½ part fresh [[Ginger]]

**Ratio:** 1:2 (fresh) in high-proof alcohol  
**Dose:** 30 drops, 3–4x daily during infection risk  
**Use:** Seasonal immune support  

---

## Advantages Over Other Preparations

| Preparation | Shelf Life | Dose Size | Taste | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tincture** | 5–10 years | Small drops | Strong | Medium |
| **Tea** | 3–5 days | 1 cup | Mild | Low |
| **Dried herbs** | 1–2 years | Variable | Mild | Very low |
| **Capsules** | 1–2 years | Multiple | None | Medium |

---

## Making Alcohol-Free Tinctures (Glycerin)

**Alternative for:**
- Young children
- Alcohol-free preference
- Religious/health reasons

### Method

**Ingredients:**
- Dried herb (1 part by weight)
- Vegetable glycerin (5 parts by volume)

**Process:**
1. Mix herb + glycerin in jar (glycerin dissolves more slowly, so use equal ratio or slightly more herb)
2. Seal tightly
3. Macerate 4–6 weeks, shake daily
4. Strain thoroughly
5. Bottle, label, store

**Dose:** Same as alcohol tinctures (20–30 drops)

**Shelf life:** 2–3 years (glycerin is more prone to fermentation over time)

**Taste:** Sweet, children often prefer

---

## Dosage Frequency Chart

| Situation | Frequency | Duration | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Acute (sudden illness)** | Every 2–4 hours | 3–7 days | Strong action, more frequent |
| **Therapeutic (treatment)** | 2–3x daily | 2–4 weeks | Regular remedy approach |
| **Maintenance (prevention)** | 1x daily | Ongoing | Gentle, consistent support |
| **Winter seasonal** | 3x daily | Oct–Apr | Preventive during season |
| **As-needed (nervine)** | When stressed | As needed | Take when situation arises |

---

## Storage Bottles & Labels

### Ideal Bottle
- Dark amber or blue glass (blocks light)
- 1–2 oz with dropper cap
- Airtight, glass dropper (not plastic)
- Labeled with:
  - Herb name(s)
  - Ratio & alcohol type
  - Date made/finish date
  - Dosage instructions
  - Intended use

### Labeling Template
```
[[Herbal Name]] Tincture
1:5 Dried in Vodka
Date Made: [Month/Year]
Date to Use By: [Expiration]
Dose: 20–30 drops, 2–3x daily
Use: [Digestive/Calming/Respiratory/etc.]
Warnings: [Any specific cautions]
```

---

## Troubleshooting Tinctures

| Problem | Cause | Solution |
|---|---|---|
| **Weak extraction (pale, no aroma)** | Under-maceration or low alcohol | Shake daily, macerate longer (6–8 weeks), use higher-proof alcohol next time |
| **Mold/cloudiness** | Low alcohol or contamination | Not normally viable; discard and start fresh |
| **Separation/layers** | Failed extraction or contamination | Discard; ensure jars are clean, alcohol is quality |
| **Evaporation** | Seal not tight or old alcohol | Check lid seals; store tightly; not dangerous, just more concentrated |
| **Sediment at bottom** | Fine plant particles | Normal; shake before use or filter through coffee filter |

---

## 🔗 Related Pages

**Individual Herb Profiles:** [[Master Plant Index]]

**Medicinal Actions:**
- [[Digestive Support]]
- [[Nervine & Calming]]
- [[Respiratory Support]]
- [[Antimicrobial Herbs]]

**Other Preparations:**
- [[Tea Infusion Guide]]
- [[Salve Guide]]
- [[Syrup Guide]]

---

**Summary:** Tinctures are powerful, shelf-stable medicine that every serious herbalist should master. Start with dried herbs and vodka—simple, accessible, and effective.

title: Tea%20Infusion%20Guide
html:
🍵 Tea Infusion Guide

A complete guide to preparing herbal infusions, the most accessible and popular herbal preparation method.

What is an Infusion?

An infusion is a preparation where fresh or dried herbs are steeped in hot water, allowing water-soluble compounds (polysaccharides, flavonoids, some volatile oils) to extract into the water.

Best for: Leaves, flowers, soft aerial parts

Not ideal for: Roots, bark, seeds, tough plant material (use decoction instead)

Basic Infusion Recipe

Ingredients

  • 1 tbsp dried herb OR 2–3 tbsp fresh herb
  • 8 oz freshly boiled water
  • Optional: honey, lemon, sweetener

Method

  1. Boil water to 200–212°F (just-boiled)
  2. Add herb to cup or teapot
  3. Pour water over herb
  4. Steep 10–15 minutes (see notes below for timing by herb)
  5. Strain thoroughly to remove all plant material
  6. Drink warm or room temperature

Yield

1 standard cup (8 oz)

Steeping Times by Herb Type

Delicate Flowers (10–12 minutes)

  • [[Chamomile]]
  • [[Lavender]]
  • [[Lemon Balm]] (mild steep okay)
  • [[Bergamot]] (flowers)

Note: Longer steeping = more bitter compounds, potentially less pleasant

Standard Leaf/Aerial Parts (12–15 minutes)

  • [[Peppermint]]
  • [[Thyme]]
  • [[Oregano]]
  • [[Lemon Balm]]
  • [[Sage]]
  • [[Marjoram]]
  • [[Catnip]]

Notes: These tolerate longer steeping well; flavor deepens

Robust Herbs (15+ minutes, or can simmer)

  • [[Hyssop]]
  • [[Horehound]]
  • [[Thyme]] (woody parts)

Notes: Can be simmered lightly 5 minutes, then steeped remaining time

Dried vs. Fresh Herb Ratios

Dried Herbs

Standard: 1 tbsp per 8 oz water
Strong brew: 1.5–2 tbsp per 8 oz water
Mild brew: ½–¾ tbsp per 8 oz water

Dried herbs are more concentrated:

  • Drying reduces water content
  • Compounds become more concentrated
  • 1 tbsp dried ≈ 2–3 tbsp fresh

Fresh Herbs

Standard: 2–3 tbsp per 8 oz water (loosely packed)
Strong brew: 3–4 tbsp per 8 oz water
Mild brew: 1–2 tbsp per 8 oz water

Fresh herbs are more dilute:

  • Higher water content
  • Compounds less concentrated
  • More volume needed for same strength

Temperature Guidelines

Just-Boiled (200–212°F)

Best for: Most herbs
Benefit: Extracts maximum compounds, quick steeping
Herbs: Chamomile, Lavender, Peppermint, most leaves

Cooling Water (175°F, after 5 minutes)

Best for: Very delicate flowers
Benefit: Preserves delicate volatile oils, gentler
Herbs: Jasmine, some rose varieties (if growing)

Room Temperature (for already-prepared tea)

How: Steep as usual, then cool
Best: Summer cold infusions, traveling
Benefit: Refreshing, different extraction profile

Infusion Strength Guide

Mild Infusion

Ratio: 1 tsp dried herb per 8 oz water
Steep: 5–10 minutes
Use: Daily maintenance, delicate herbs, tea for pleasure
Taste: Subtle, light flavor

Standard Infusion

Ratio: 1 tbsp dried herb per 8 oz water
Steep: 12–15 minutes
Use: Medicinal support, therapeutic dose
Taste: Noticeable herbal flavor

Strong Infusion

Ratio: 1.5–2 tbsp dried herb per 8 oz water
Steep: 15–20 minutes
Use: Acute medicinal need, concentrated effect
Taste: Bold, herbal, possibly bitter

Extra-Strong (Medicinal)

Ratio: 2–3 tbsp dried herb per 4–6 oz water
Steep: 20–30 minutes
Use: Maximum medicinal effect, shorter dose time
Taste: Very pronounced, frequently bitter
Note: May taste unpleasant; consider mixing with honey or combining herbs

Single-Herb vs. Herbal Blends

Single-Herb Infusion

Advantages:

  • Simple, pure
  • Easy to assess how you respond
  • Focused medicinal action
  • One flavor profile

Best for:

  • [[Chamomile]] (calming)
  • [[Peppermint]] (digestion)
  • [[Lavender]] (relaxation)

Herbal Blend (2–4 herbs)

Advantages:

  • More complex flavor
  • Multiple medicinal actions
  • Synergistic effect (herbals work better combined)
  • More interesting taste

Best Blends:

Digestive Blend:

  • 2 parts [[Peppermint]]
  • 1 part [[Chamomile]]
  • 1 part [[Dill]] or [[Fennel]]

Calming Blend:

  • 2 parts [[Lavender]]
  • 2 parts [[Lemon Balm]]
  • 1 part [[Chamomile]]

Respiratory Blend:

  • 2 parts [[Thyme]]
  • 1 part [[Horehound]]
  • 1 part [[Hyssop]]

Storage & Shelf Life

Fresh-Made Infusion

Storage: Refrigerated
Shelf life: 3–5 days
Best: Drink fresh, within 24 hours ideally
Note: Compounds degrade over time; fresh is most potent

Dried Herbs (for infusing)

Storage: Sealed glass or paper bags, cool dark place
Shelf life: 1–2 years
Optimal: Within 1 year for maximum potency
Signs of age: Pale color, weak aroma, reduced effect

Herbal Blend (pre-mixed)

Storage: Sealed glass jars, cool dark cupboard
Shelf life: 6–12 months (blends lose potency faster than single herbs)
Best: Prepare blends in small batches

Making Infusions Cold (Sun Tea Method)

Cold Infusion Method

Time: 6–12 hours (overnight is convenient)

Process:

  1. Place 1 tbsp dried herb in closed jar
  2. Add 8 oz cool water
  3. Leave at room temperature or in sun 6–12 hours
  4. Strain thoroughly

Advantages:

  • Gentler extraction
  • Preserves delicate volatile oils
  • Can be left overnight without attention
  • Refreshing served cold

Disadvantages:

  • Longer preparation time
  • Slightly less extraction of some compounds
  • Requires advance planning

Best herbs for cold infusion:

  • [[Mint]] family (especially refreshing cold)
  • [[Chamomile]] (gentle extraction preserves delicate compounds)
  • [[Lavender]]

Herbal Infusion Dosage

Daily Maintenance (Wellness)

Dose: 1 cup of standard infusion, 1–2x daily
Best: Regular consistent use
Example: [[Peppermint]] tea daily for digestive wellness

Therapeutic Dose (Addressing Specific Issue)

Dose: 1–3 cups of standard strength, 2–3x daily
Duration: 2–4 weeks or as needed
Example: [[Lavender]] tea 3x daily for stress support

Acute Support (Sudden Need)

Dose: 1 cup strong infusion, every 2–4 hours as needed
Duration: Until acute situation resolves (usually 3–7 days)
Example: [[Thyme]] tea for respiratory comfort during cold

Infusion Additions for Taste & Function

Honey

  • 1–2 tsp per cup
  • Adds sweetness, soothing, antimicrobial
  • Add after cooling slightly (heat destroys honey enzymes)
  • Pairing: Works with all herbs

Lemon Juice

  • ½–1 tbsp per cup
  • Brightens flavor, aids mineral absorption
  • Assists with iron absorption (if herbal iron-rich)
  • Pairing: Peppermint, Chamomile, Lavender

Ginger (from spice cupboard)

  • Small slice or ¼ tsp powder
  • Warming, improves circulation, aids digestion
  • Pairing: Digestive blends, respiratory blends

Apple Cider Vinegar (1–2 tsp)

  • Slightly sour, mineral-rich
  • Enhances mineral extraction
  • Pairing: Nutritive infusions, digestive blends

Cinnamon (from spice cupboard)

  • Pinch to ¼ tsp
  • Warming, blood-sugar supporting
  • Pairing: Digestive blends, warming infusions

Cayenne Pepper (pinch)

  • Stimulating to circulation
  • Warming
  • Pairing: Respiratory blends, warming digestive

Equipment Needed

Essential

  • Heat source (stove, kettle)
  • Cup or pot (8–16 oz)
  • Strainer (tea strainer, fine mesh, or cheesecloth)
  • Measuring spoon

Optional (Nice to Have)

  • Infuser/steeper (loose-leaf tea infuser)
  • Kettle with temperature control
  • Covered cup (retains heat, maintains temperature)
  • Glass jars (for tea blending + storage)

Common Questions

Q: Can I re-steep the same herbs?

A: Yes, once. The first infusion extracts 70–80% of compounds. Second steeping gets remaining. Usually add 1–2 minutes longer. Third steeping generally too weak to be worthwhile.

Q: Can I make a large batch to drink over several days?

A: Not recommended. Fresh infusions lose potency daily. Better to brew fresh daily, or make tinctures for longer storage.

Q: What if my tea tastes too bitter?

A:

  • Reduce steeping time (over-steeping = bitterness)
  • Use less herb
  • Add honey or lemon
  • Blend with milder herb (add Chamomile)

Q: Is leaving herbs steeping overnight okay?

A: Generally okay, but potency decreases. Flavor may become unpleasant. Better to brew fresh or use tincture for longer-lasting strength.

Herbal Tea Ritual

Making herbal infusions can be more than functional:

  1. Slow down: Take 5 minutes to mindfully prepare tea
  2. Notice: Observe color, aroma, steam
  3. Sip intentionally: Warm cup in hands, small sips
  4. Feel: Notice warmth, taste, subtle effects
  5. Appreciate: Gratitude for plant medicine

Many herbalists find this ritualistic aspect part of the healing power.

🔗 Related Pages

Individual Herb Profiles: [[Master Plant Index]]

Medicinal Actions:

  • [[Digestive Support]]
  • [[Nervine & Calming]]
  • [[Respiratory Support]]

Other Preparations:

  • [[Tincture Guide]]
  • [[Salve Guide]]
  • [[Syrup Guide]]

Summary: Infusions are the foundational herbal preparation. Master this technique and you have access to most medicinal herbs' benefits with just simple equipment and 15 minutes of time.

title: Syrup%20Guide
html:
🥤 Syrup Guide

A complete guide to making and using herbal syrups, sweet preparations that preserve herbs and make medicine palatable.

What is an Herbal Syrup?

An herbal syrup is a concentrated herbal preparation combined with sweetener (honey, sugar, or glycerin), creating a thick, spoon-able medicine that's easier to take than tea, especially for children.

Advantages:

  • Taste-masked (sweet covers herbal bitterness)
  • Easy for children to take
  • Concentrated (high herbal content)
  • Good shelf life (several months refrigerated)
  • Spoon-able dosing (easier than tea)

Best for:

  • Respiratory support (cough syrups)
  • Digestive support (after-meal remedy)
  • Getting children to take medicine

Not ideal for:

  • Long-term storage (3–5 years like tinctures)
  • Diabetic individuals (sugar content)
  • Very heat-sensitive herbs

Basic Syrup Formula

Simple Ratio

1 cup strong herbal infusion + 2 tbsp–½ cup sweetener

How Sweetener Works

Sugar/Honey Role:

  • Preserves (osmotic effect draws water from bacteria)
  • Masks bitter taste
  • Creates proper consistency
  • Adds energy + nourishment

Sweetener Options:

  • Honey: Best (antimicrobial, nourishing, medicinal)
  • Sugar (cane): Traditional, good preservation
  • Maple syrup: Milder taste, more glycemic
  • Agave: Mild, less crystallization
  • Glycerin: For non-sugar option (less preservative)

Syrup Making: Step-by-Step

Method 1: Herbal Tea Syrup

Time: 30 minutes (fastest method)

Ingredients

  • 1 cup strong herbal infusion
  • 2–4 tbsp honey (or up to ½ cup for thicker syrup)
  • Optional: 1 tbsp apple cider vinegar (enhances preservation, flavor)

Process

Step 1: Make Strong Infusion

  1. Brew strong herbal tea: 2–3 tbsp herb per 8 oz water
  2. Steep 15–20 minutes (longer = more extraction)
  3. Strain thoroughly

Step 2: Combine with Sweetener

  1. Heat infusion gently to warm (not boiling, which harms honey)
  2. Add honey while warm (dissolves easier)
  3. Stir until completely dissolved
  4. Optional: Add apple cider vinegar

Step 3: Cool & Bottle

  1. Cool to room temperature
  2. Pour into glass bottles with tight lid
  3. Label with herb name(s), date, suggested use

Shelf Life

3–4 months refrigerated (honey's antimicrobial action)

Method 2: Decoction Syrup (Stronger)

For roots, bark, tougher herbs requiring simmering

Ingredients

  • 1 cup strong herbal decoction (simmered)
  • 3–4 tbsp honey

Process

Step 1: Make Decoction

  1. Use roots/barks: [[Horehound]], [[Licorice]] (if growing), dried ginger
  2. Simmer in water 20–30 minutes
  3. Strain thoroughly, measure 1 cup liquid remaining

Step 2: Reduce (Optional but Recommended)

  1. Pour liquid into pot
  2. Simmer gently until reduced to ¾ cup
  3. This concentrates herbs + helps honey preservation

Step 3: Add Honey

  1. Cool slightly
  2. Add 3–4 tbsp honey, stir until dissolved

Step 4: Bottle

  1. Pour into glass bottles
  2. Label, refrigerate

Shelf Life

3–4 months refrigerated

Popular Herbal Syrups

Classic Cough Syrup

Best for: Cough, respiratory comfort, especially nighttime

Ingredients:

  • 1 tbsp [[Thyme]]
  • 1 tbsp [[Horehound]]
  • ½ tbsp [[Ginger]] (dried, from spice cupboard)
  • 1 cup water

Making:

  1. Decoction: Simmer herbs 20 minutes in 1 cup water
  2. Strain, measure 1 cup liquid
  3. Add 4 tbsp honey (thick consistency for cough comfort)
  4. Optional: Add 1 tbsp apple cider vinegar (enhances)
  5. Cool, bottle, refrigerate

Dose: 1 tsp every 2–4 hours as needed for cough

Use: Best bedtime before sleep

Shelf life: 3–4 months refrigerated

Digestive Sweet Syrup

Best for: After meals, digestive support, pleasant flavor

Ingredients:

  • 1 tbsp [[Fennel]] seed
  • 1 tbsp [[Ginger]] (dried)
  • ½ tbsp [[Lemon Balm]]
  • 1 cup water

Making:

  1. Infusion: Steep lighter herbs ([[Lemon Balm]]) in hot water first (10 min)
  2. Add hardier herbs early: Simmer [[Fennel]] + [[Ginger]]15 minutes more
  3. Strain, collect 1 cup
  4. Add 3 tbsp honey
  5. Cool, bottle, refrigerate

Dose: 1 tsp after meals

Taste: Warm, sweet, digestive-pleasant

Shelf life: 3–4 months refrigerated

Nervine Calming Syrup

Best for: Bedtime, stress support, gentle action

Ingredients:

  • 1 tbsp dried [[Lavender]]
  • ½ tbsp dried [[Lemon Balm]]
  • ½ tbsp dried [[Chamomile]]
  • 1 cup water

Making:

  1. Infusion: Steep all herbs in hot water 15–20 minutes
  2. Strain, collect 1 cup
  3. Add 3 tbsp honey
  4. Cool, bottle, refrigerate

Dose: 1 tsp before bed or 2–3x daily during stress

Taste: Floral, sweet, calming-pleasant

Shelf life: 3–4 months refrigerated

Immune Support Syrup

Best for: General immune support, especially children

Ingredients:

  • 1 tbsp dried [[Echinacea]] root (or leaf if root unavailable)
  • ½ tbsp dried [[Thyme]]
  • ½ tbsp [[Ginger]]
  • 1 cup water

Making:

  1. Decoction: Simmer all herbs 20 minutes
  2. Strain, collect 1 cup
  3. Add 4 tbsp honey
  4. Optional: Add 1 tbsp apple cider vinegar
  5. Cool, bottle, refrigerate

Dose: 1 tsp, 2–3x daily during cold season, or at first symptoms

For children: 1 tsp per year of age, 1–2x daily

Shelf life: 3–4 months refrigerated

Syrup Consistency Guide

Consistency Honey/Sugar Amount Best For Application
Thin (like maple syrup) 2–3 tbsp per cup infusion Drizzle, mix into food Easier to pour
Medium (like honey) 3–4 tbsp per cup infusion Spoon, coating throat Ideal for cough
Thick (like molasses) 4–6 tbsp per cup infusion Slow drip, adheres well Maximum medicinal feel
Extra-thick (paste-like) More heating, some reduction Lozenge-like consistency Effort to consume

Storage & Shelf Life

Ideal Storage

  • Refrigerator: Cool, dark shelf (preferred)
  • Cool cupboard: If airtight container, short-term
  • NOT room temperature: Accelerates spoilage

Signs of Good Syrup

✓ Nice color (darker/herb-hued)
✓ No mold ✓ Smooth consistency
✓ Pleasant herbal smell
✓ No separation

Signs of Spoilage

✗ Mold (blue/white/fuzzy)
✗ Fermented smell (yeasty, off)
✗ Separation into liquid + solids
✗ Crystallization (very old honey)

If moldy: Discard entirely (don't attempt to salvage)

Shelf Life by Storage Method

  • Refrigerated, honey-based: 3–4 months
  • Refrigerated, sugar-based: 2–3 months
  • Refrigerated, glycerin-based: 1–2 months
  • Frozen: 6+ months

Dosage Guide

Children

  • Under 1 year: Consult healthcare provider (honey caution < 1 yr)
  • 1–3 years: ¼ tsp, 1–2x daily
  • 4–6 years: ½ tsp, 1–2x daily
  • 7–12 years: 1 tsp, 1–2x daily
  • Adolescent/adult: 1 tsp, 1–3x daily

Adults

  • Maintenance: 1 tsp, 1x daily
  • Therapeutic: 1 tsp, 2–3x daily
  • Acute cough: 1 tsp every 2–4 hours as needed

Application Methods

  • Direct: Spoon directly in mouth
  • In water: Dissolve in warm water, drink as "medicine tea"
  • On toast: Spread like jam (pleasant for children)
  • In warm milk: Mix for bedtime drink
  • On oatmeal: Drizzle for breakfast

Making Thicker Syrup (Oil Infusion Instead)

Alternative method for longer shelf life:

  1. Make herbal-infused oil (4-week process)
  2. Strain thoroughly
  3. Infuse this oil with honey in 1:1 ratio (instead of water)
  4. Never heat honey above 120°F (damages medicinal compounds)
  5. Shelf life extends to 6+ months refrigerated

Honey Syrup Advantages

Why honey is best sweetener for syrups:

✓ Natural antimicrobial (preserves longer)
✓ Medicinal in itself (cough soothing, throat coating)
✓ Contains enzymes (survived extraction methods)
✓ Doesn't crystallize like sugar
✓ Works at lower temps (preserves herbs better)
✓ Pairs well with herbal flavors

Cautions:

  • Don't heat honey above 120°F (kills enzymes)
  • Not for infants under 1 year (botulism risk)
  • Check honey source (raw better than refined)

Glycerin-Based Syrup (Alcohol-Free Alternative)

Best for: No honey desire, vegan preference

Recipe

Ingredients:

  • 1 cup strong herbal infusion or decoction
  • ½–¾ cup vegetable glycerin

Process:

  1. Make cool or warm infusion (decoction for roots)
  2. Cool completely
  3. Mix 1 cup infusion + ½ cup glycerin
  4. Stir thoroughly (glycerin is viscous)
  5. Bottle, label, refrigerate

Dose: Same as honey syrup (1 tsp)

Taste: Sweet, less herbal than honey version

Shelf life: 1–2 months refrigerated (less preservative than honey)

Gifting Syrups

Herbal syrups make excellent gifts:

Packaging:

  • Glass jars with pour spouts
  • Label with herb(s), date, use
  • Include dosage instructions
  • Suggest storage (refrigerate)

Gift sets:

  • Cough syrup + Digestive syrup
  • Immune syrup + Cough syrup
  • All three (Cough, Digestive, Immune)

For children: Immune syrup labeled with pediatric dosing

Syrup Troubleshooting

Problem Cause Prevention/Fix
Too thin (watery) Not enough honey/sweetener Add more honey (reheat, stir in)
Too thick (difficult to spoon) Too much honey, or reduction Add small amount infusion
Crystallized solid Very old honey crystallizing (normal) Warm gently to dissolve, stir
Moldy Contamination or low sugar Discard; ensure clean bottles, good ratio next time
Separated liquid + settled solids Honey + herb not blended well Shake before use; use less herb material
Fermented smell Bacterial growth (low honey %) Discard; increase honey:infusion ratio

🔗 Related Pages

Individual Herb Profiles: [[Master Plant Index]]

Medicinal Actions:

  • [[Respiratory Support]]
  • [[Digestive Support]]
  • [[Nervine & Calming]]

Other Preparations:

  • [[Tea Infusion Guide]]
  • [[Tincture Guide]]
  • [[Salve Guide]]

Summary: Syrups are delicious, medicinally-effective preparations that make herbal medicine palatable for entire families. Perfect for kids and adults alike.
raw:
# 🥤 Syrup Guide

A complete guide to making and using herbal syrups, sweet preparations that preserve herbs and make medicine palatable.

---

## What is an Herbal Syrup?

An herbal syrup is a concentrated herbal preparation combined with sweetener (honey, sugar, or glycerin), creating a thick, spoon-able medicine that's easier to take than tea, especially for children.

**Advantages:**
- Taste-masked (sweet covers herbal bitterness)
- Easy for children to take
- Concentrated (high herbal content)
- Good shelf life (several months refrigerated)
- Spoon-able dosing (easier than tea)

**Best for:** 
- Respiratory support (cough syrups)
- Digestive support (after-meal remedy)
- Getting children to take medicine

**Not ideal for:**
- Long-term storage (3–5 years like tinctures)
- Diabetic individuals (sugar content)
- Very heat-sensitive herbs

---

## Basic Syrup Formula

### Simple Ratio

**1 cup strong herbal infusion + 2 tbsp–½ cup sweetener**

---

## How Sweetener Works

**Sugar/Honey Role:**
- Preserves (osmotic effect draws water from bacteria)
- Masks bitter taste
- Creates proper consistency
- Adds energy + nourishment

**Sweetener Options:**
- **Honey:** Best (antimicrobial, nourishing, medicinal)
- **Sugar (cane):** Traditional, good preservation
- **Maple syrup:** Milder taste, more glycemic
- **Agave:** Mild, less crystallization
- **Glycerin:** For non-sugar option (less preservative)

---

## Syrup Making: Step-by-Step

### Method 1: Herbal Tea Syrup

**Time:** 30 minutes (fastest method)

#### Ingredients
- 1 cup strong herbal infusion
- 2–4 tbsp honey (or up to ½ cup for thicker syrup)
- Optional: 1 tbsp apple cider vinegar (enhances preservation, flavor)

#### Process

**Step 1: Make Strong Infusion**
1. Brew strong herbal tea: 2–3 tbsp herb per 8 oz water
2. Steep 15–20 minutes (longer = more extraction)
3. Strain thoroughly

**Step 2: Combine with Sweetener**
1. Heat infusion gently to warm (not boiling, which harms honey)
2. Add honey while warm (dissolves easier)
3. Stir until completely dissolved
4. Optional: Add apple cider vinegar

**Step 3: Cool & Bottle**
1. Cool to room temperature
2. Pour into glass bottles with tight lid
3. Label with herb name(s), date, suggested use

#### Shelf Life
3–4 months refrigerated (honey's antimicrobial action)

---

### Method 2: Decoction Syrup (Stronger)

**For roots, bark, tougher herbs requiring simmering**

#### Ingredients
- 1 cup strong herbal decoction (simmered)
- 3–4 tbsp honey

#### Process

**Step 1: Make Decoction**
1. Use roots/barks: [[Horehound]], [[Licorice]] (if growing), dried ginger
2. Simmer in water 20–30 minutes
3. Strain thoroughly, measure 1 cup liquid remaining

**Step 2: Reduce (Optional but Recommended)**
1. Pour liquid into pot
2. Simmer gently until reduced to ¾ cup
3. This concentrates herbs + helps honey preservation

**Step 3: Add Honey**
1. Cool slightly
2. Add 3–4 tbsp honey, stir until dissolved

**Step 4: Bottle**
1. Pour into glass bottles
2. Label, refrigerate

#### Shelf Life
3–4 months refrigerated

---

## Popular Herbal Syrups

### Classic Cough Syrup

**Best for:** Cough, respiratory comfort, especially nighttime

**Ingredients:**
- 1 tbsp [[Thyme]]
- 1 tbsp [[Horehound]]
- ½ tbsp [[Ginger]] (dried, from spice cupboard)
- 1 cup water

**Making:**
1. Decoction: Simmer herbs 20 minutes in 1 cup water
2. Strain, measure 1 cup liquid
3. Add 4 tbsp honey (thick consistency for cough comfort)
4. Optional: Add 1 tbsp apple cider vinegar (enhances)
5. Cool, bottle, refrigerate

**Dose:** 1 tsp every 2–4 hours as needed for cough

**Use:** Best bedtime before sleep

**Shelf life:** 3–4 months refrigerated

---

### Digestive Sweet Syrup

**Best for:** After meals, digestive support, pleasant flavor

**Ingredients:**
- 1 tbsp [[Fennel]] seed
- 1 tbsp [[Ginger]] (dried)
- ½ tbsp [[Lemon Balm]]
- 1 cup water

**Making:**
1. Infusion: Steep lighter herbs ([[Lemon Balm]]) in hot water first (10 min)
2. Add hardier herbs early: Simmer [[Fennel]] + [[Ginger]]15 minutes more
3. Strain, collect 1 cup
4. Add 3 tbsp honey
5. Cool, bottle, refrigerate

**Dose:** 1 tsp after meals

**Taste:** Warm, sweet, digestive-pleasant

**Shelf life:** 3–4 months refrigerated

---

### Nervine Calming Syrup

**Best for:** Bedtime, stress support, gentle action

**Ingredients:**
- 1 tbsp dried [[Lavender]]
- ½ tbsp dried [[Lemon Balm]]
- ½ tbsp dried [[Chamomile]]
- 1 cup water

**Making:**
1. Infusion: Steep all herbs in hot water 15–20 minutes
2. Strain, collect 1 cup
3. Add 3 tbsp honey
4. Cool, bottle, refrigerate

**Dose:** 1 tsp before bed or 2–3x daily during stress

**Taste:** Floral, sweet, calming-pleasant

**Shelf life:** 3–4 months refrigerated

---

### Immune Support Syrup

**Best for:** General immune support, especially children

**Ingredients:**
- 1 tbsp dried [[Echinacea]] root (or leaf if root unavailable)
- ½ tbsp dried [[Thyme]]
- ½ tbsp [[Ginger]]
- 1 cup water

**Making:**
1. Decoction: Simmer all herbs 20 minutes
2. Strain, collect 1 cup
3. Add 4 tbsp honey
4. Optional: Add 1 tbsp apple cider vinegar
5. Cool, bottle, refrigerate

**Dose:** 1 tsp, 2–3x daily during cold season, or at first symptoms

**For children:** 1 tsp per year of age, 1–2x daily

**Shelf life:** 3–4 months refrigerated

---

## Syrup Consistency Guide

| Consistency | Honey/Sugar Amount | Best For | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thin (like maple syrup)** | 2–3 tbsp per cup infusion | Drizzle, mix into food | Easier to pour |
| **Medium (like honey)** | 3–4 tbsp per cup infusion | Spoon, coating throat | Ideal for cough |
| **Thick (like molasses)** | 4–6 tbsp per cup infusion | Slow drip, adheres well | Maximum medicinal feel |
| **Extra-thick (paste-like)** | More heating, some reduction | Lozenge-like consistency | Effort to consume |

---

## Storage & Shelf Life

### Ideal Storage
- **Refrigerator:** Cool, dark shelf (preferred)
- **Cool cupboard:** If airtight container, short-term
- **NOT room temperature:** Accelerates spoilage

### Signs of Good Syrup
✓ Nice color (darker/herb-hued)  
✓ No mold
✓ Smooth consistency  
✓ Pleasant herbal smell  
✓ No separation  

### Signs of Spoilage
✗ Mold (blue/white/fuzzy)  
✗ Fermented smell (yeasty, off)  
✗ Separation into liquid + solids  
✗ Crystallization (very old honey)  

**If moldy:** Discard entirely (don't attempt to salvage)

### Shelf Life by Storage Method
- **Refrigerated, honey-based:** 3–4 months
- **Refrigerated, sugar-based:** 2–3 months  
- **Refrigerated, glycerin-based:** 1–2 months
- **Frozen:** 6+ months

---

## Dosage Guide

### Children
- **Under 1 year:** Consult healthcare provider (honey caution < 1 yr)
- **1–3 years:** ¼ tsp, 1–2x daily
- **4–6 years:** ½ tsp, 1–2x daily
- **7–12 years:** 1 tsp, 1–2x daily
- **Adolescent/adult:** 1 tsp, 1–3x daily

### Adults
- **Maintenance:** 1 tsp, 1x daily
- **Therapeutic:** 1 tsp, 2–3x daily
- **Acute cough:** 1 tsp every 2–4 hours as needed

### Application Methods
- **Direct:** Spoon directly in mouth
- **In water:** Dissolve in warm water, drink as "medicine tea"
- **On toast:** Spread like jam (pleasant for children)
- **In warm milk:** Mix for bedtime drink
- **On oatmeal:** Drizzle for breakfast

---

## Making Thicker Syrup (Oil Infusion Instead)

**Alternative method for longer shelf life:**

1. Make herbal-infused oil (4-week process)
2. Strain thoroughly
3. Infuse this oil with honey in 1:1 ratio (instead of water)
4. Never heat honey above 120°F (damages medicinal compounds)
5. Shelf life extends to 6+ months refrigerated

---

## Honey Syrup Advantages

**Why honey is best sweetener for syrups:**

✓ Natural antimicrobial (preserves longer)  
✓ Medicinal in itself (cough soothing, throat coating)  
✓ Contains enzymes (survived extraction methods)  
✓ Doesn't crystallize like sugar  
✓ Works at lower temps (preserves herbs better)  
✓ Pairs well with herbal flavors  

**Cautions:**
- Don't heat honey above 120°F (kills enzymes)
- Not for infants under 1 year (botulism risk)
- Check honey source (raw better than refined)

---

## Glycerin-Based Syrup (Alcohol-Free Alternative)

**Best for:** No honey desire, vegan preference

### Recipe

**Ingredients:**
- 1 cup strong herbal infusion or decoction
- ½–¾ cup vegetable glycerin

**Process:**
1. Make cool or warm infusion (decoction for roots)
2. Cool completely
3. Mix 1 cup infusion + ½ cup glycerin
4. Stir thoroughly (glycerin is viscous)
5. Bottle, label, refrigerate

**Dose:** Same as honey syrup (1 tsp)

**Taste:** Sweet, less herbal than honey version

**Shelf life:** 1–2 months refrigerated (less preservative than honey)

---

## Gifting Syrups

Herbal syrups make excellent gifts:

**Packaging:**
- Glass jars with pour spouts
- Label with herb(s), date, use
- Include dosage instructions
- Suggest storage (refrigerate)

**Gift sets:**
- Cough syrup + Digestive syrup
- Immune syrup + Cough syrup
- All three (Cough, Digestive, Immune)

**For children:** Immune syrup labeled with pediatric dosing

---

## Syrup Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause | Prevention/Fix |
|---|---|---|
| **Too thin (watery)** | Not enough honey/sweetener | Add more honey (reheat, stir in) |
| **Too thick (difficult to spoon)** | Too much honey, or reduction | Add small amount infusion |
| **Crystallized solid** | Very old honey crystallizing (normal) | Warm gently to dissolve, stir |
| **Moldy** | Contamination or low sugar | Discard; ensure clean bottles, good ratio next time |
| **Separated liquid + settled solids** | Honey + herb not blended well | Shake before use; use less herb material |
| **Fermented smell** | Bacterial growth (low honey %) | Discard; increase honey:infusion ratio |

---

## 🔗 Related Pages

**Individual Herb Profiles:** [[Master Plant Index]]

**Medicinal Actions:**
- [[Respiratory Support]]
- [[Digestive Support]]
- [[Nervine & Calming]]

**Other Preparations:**
- [[Tea Infusion Guide]]
- [[Tincture Guide]]
- [[Salve Guide]]

---

**Summary:** Syrups are delicious, medicinally-effective preparations that make herbal medicine palatable for entire families. Perfect for kids and adults alike.

title: Salve%20Guide
html:
🧴 Salve Guide

A complete guide to making and using herbal salves, topical preparations with beeswax for sustained healing.

What is a Salve?

A salve (also called balm or ointment) is a topical preparation combining infused oil with beeswax, creating a semi-solid consistency that adheres to skin and allows prolonged herbal contact.

Advantages:

  • Long-lasting topical contact with affected area
  • Moisture barrier (protects while and healing)
  • Pleasant texture (often adds aromatics)
  • Shelf-stable (months to years)
  • Synergistic herb + beeswax benefits

Best for: Wounds, skin irritations, muscle soreness, topical healing

Not ideal for: Large-area treatment (cost), internal use

Basic Salve Formula

Ingredients

Oil Base (choose one):

  • Olive oil (most common, mild)
  • Coconut oil (mild, pleasant smell)
  • Almond oil (light, absorbs well)
  • Calendula-infused oil (adds healing action)

Beeswax:

  • Raw beeswax (natural, unrefined)
  • Beeswax pastilles (easier to measure)

Herbal Extract:

  • Dried herbs (infused in oil)
  • Essential oils (optional, for scent/extra action)

Optional Additions:

  • Vitamin E oil (antioxidant, preservative)
  • Honey (antimicrobial)
  • Lanolin (if not vegan, adds plasticity)

Salve Making: Step-by-Step

Equipment Needed

Essential:

  • Glass jars for oil infusion
  • Double boiler or makeshift (glass bowl over pot of water)
  • Thermometer (optional but helpful)
  • Measuring cups/spoons
  • Wooden spoon for stirring
  • Glass or metal pour containers (warmed)
  • Small tins or jars for finished salve
  • Cheesecloth or fine strainer
  • Labels

Safety:

  • Apron (hot oil can splash)
  • Heat-resistant gloves
  • Paper towels for cleanup

Method 1: Traditional Salve (Infused Oil + Beeswax)

Time: Oil infusion 4 weeks + 30 minutes salve-making

Step 1: Infuse Oil with Herbs (4 weeks)

Choose herbs:

  • [[Lavender]] (soothing, antimicrobial)
  • [[Calendula]] (wound healing, skin-supportive)
  • [[White Yarrow]] (tissue healing)
  • [[Plantain]] (if available, wound-healing)

Infusion method:

  1. Fill jar with dried herb (loosely packed, 1/3–1/2 jar)

  2. Pour oil until herb is covered by 2 inches

  3. Seal jar with lid

  4. Infuse in warm sunny location 4–6 weeks

    • Sun infusion (preferred): Full sun window
    • Warm-spot infusion: Near heater, warm cupboard
    • Cool infusion: Room temperature (longer, gentler)

  5. Check weekly for moisture:

    • If condensation inside, prop open briefly to dry
    • Moisture + oil = rancidity risk

  6. Strain thoroughly after 4 weeks:

    • Line strainer with cheesecloth
    • Pour through carefully
    • Press herb lightly (don't squeeze hard)
    • Discard herb matter
    • Filter through fine strainer if cloudy

  7. Store oil:

    • Glass jar, cool place
    • Will last 6+ months
    • Use fresh oil for best results

Step 2: Make Salve (30 minutes)

Ingredients:

  • 1 cup infused herbal oil
  • 1 oz beeswax (approximately; adjust for consistency)

Ratio Guide:

  • 4:1 oil to wax = soft, spreadable salve
  • 3:1 oil to wax = medium-firm
  • 2:1 oil to wax = firm, less greasy

Start with 1 oz wax, can adjust after first batch

Process:

  1. Set up double boiler:

    • Fill bottom pot with water (not touching top pot when water boils)
    • Place glass bowl/top pot on bottom
    • Heat water to gentle simmer

  2. Measure ingredients:

    • 1 cup infused oil
    • 1 oz beeswax (weighed or roughly by appearance)

  3. Add oil & beeswax to double boiler

    • Pour oil into glass bowl
    • Add beeswax broken into small pieces
    • Stir occasionally until wax melts (10–15 minutes)
    • Don't exceed 170–180°F (preserves herbal compounds)

  4. Test consistency (before adding to jars):

    • Drop small amount onto cool plate
    • Let sit 2 minutes
    • Check texture (should be soft but hold shape)
    • Too soft? Add more wax
    • Too hard? Add more oil
    • Return to heat, adjust, test again

  5. Prepare containers:

    • Warm glass jars or tins (place near heat source)
    • Line up on clean surface
    • Prepare labels

  6. Pour salve:

    • Once consistency is right, remove from heat
    • Carefully pour into warm containers (fills better, prevents air bubbles)
    • Don't fill to brim (air space prevents moisture)
    • Let cool completely before capping (30 minutes to 2 hours)

  7. Label & store:

    • Label with herb(s), date made, suggested use
    • Store in cool place
    • Avoid direct sunlight

Shelf Life

6–12 months (or longer with vitamin E oil added)

Method 2: Quick Salve (Pre-Made Oil)

If you don't have time for 4-week infusion:

Use:

  • Buy pre-infused herbal oil (calendula oil available at natural food stores)
  • OR use high-quality carrier oil with essential oil added

Ingredients:

  • 1 cup pre-infused oil
  • 1 oz beeswax

Method: Same as Method 1, Step 2 above

Benefit: Same-day creation possible

Popular Herbal Salves

Lavender Healing Salve

Best for: Minor wounds, skin irritation, soothing

Making:

  1. Infuse 1 cup olive oil with 1 oz dried [[Lavender]] flowers, 4 weeks
  2. Strain thoroughly
  3. Make salve: 1 cup infused oil + 1 oz beeswax
  4. Optional: Add 10 drops lavender essential oil for scent (after cooling slightly)

Use: Apply 2–3x daily to clean skin

Shelf life: 8–12 months

Calendula Wound-Healing Salve

Best for: Wounds, skin repair, scarring

Making:

  1. Infuse 1 cup olive oil with 1 oz dried [[Calendula]] petals, 4 weeks
  2. Strain thoroughly (calendula petals can be sticky)
  3. Make salve: 1 cup infused oil + 1 oz beeswax (may need slightly more wax)
  4. Optional: Add 1 tbsp raw honey (extra antimicrobial) while warm

Use: Apply 2–3x daily to clean wounds or minor skin damage

Shelf life: 8–12 months

Herbal Anti-Itch Salve

Best for: Dry skin, minor itching, eczema-prone skin

Making:

  1. Infuse 1 cup coconut oil (more soothing) with:

    • ½ oz [[Lavender]]
    • ½ oz [[Plantain]] (if available)
    • ¼ oz [[Chamomile]] 4 weeks

  2. Make salve: 1 cup infused oil + 1 oz beeswax

  3. Optional: Add 5 drops chamomile essential oil + 5 drops lavender

Use: Apply to itchy areas as needed

Shelf life: 6–9 months

Warming Muscle Salve

Best for: Muscle tension, soreness, joint stiffness

Making:

  1. Infuse 1 cup olive oil with:

    • ½ oz dried [[Rosemary]]
    • ½ oz dried [[Ginger]] (if growing, or dried)
    • ¼ oz dried [[Cayenne]] pepper (if comfortable handling) 4 weeks

  2. Make salve: 1 cup infused oil + 1 oz beeswax

  3. Optional: Add warming essential oil (black pepper, ginger, or eucalyptus)

Use: Massage into sore muscles/joints 2–3x daily

Caution: Test on small area first (warming herbs may cause slight warming sensation)

Shelf life: 6–9 months

Salve Consistency Guide

Consistency Ratio Best For Application
Very soft (almost like thick oil) 5:1 oil:wax Massage, large areas Rubs on easily, spreads far
Soft/spreadable (butter-like) 4:1 oil:wax Most wounds, daily use Ideal for most purposes
Medium (holds shape) 3:1 oil:wax Targeted application, lips Stays where applied
Firm (waxy) 2:1 oil:wax Lip balms, difficult areas Lasts longer on skin
Very firm 1:1 oil:wax Extreme durability Difficult to apply, very waxy

Essential Oil Additions (Optional)

If adding essential oils to finished salve:

  1. Let salve cool to about 140°F (still pourable, not hot)
  2. Add essential oil: 10–20 drops per 1 oz salve
  3. Stir thoroughly
  4. Pour into containers
  5. Let cool completely before capping

Common additions:

  • Lavender (5 drops): Extra soothing, pleasant scent
  • Chamomile (5 drops): Calming, anti-inflammatory
  • Eucalyptus (3 drops): respiratory opening (not on wounds)
  • Tea Tree (2–3 drops): Antimicrobial (use cautiously, can irritate)

Storage & Shelf Life

Ideal Storage

  • Temperature: Cool (60–75°F), stable
  • Light: Cool, dark place (avoid heat/direct sun)
  • Container: Glass jars or metal tins (not plastic)
  • Seal: Tight-fitting lids

How to Tell When Salve Has Gone Bad

  • ✗ Moldy appearance or smell
  • ✗ Rancid oil smell (gone off)
  • ✗ Separated into layers
  • ✗ Unusual color change

Safe to use:

  • ✓ Slight color variation (normal)
  • ✓ Powder on top (beeswax can separate; stir to combine)
  • ✓ Slightly harder with age (normal)

Shelf Life

  • Without vitamin E: 6–9 months
  • With vitamin E oil: 9–12 months
  • Refrigerated: Up to 18 months

Application Guide

For Wounds

  1. Clean area gently
  2. Pat dry with sterile cloth
  3. Apply thin layer of salve
  4. Can cover with sterile gauze or bandage
  5. Reapply 2–3x daily

For General Skin

  1. Clean hands/area
  2. Apply small amount salve
  3. Rub gently into skin
  4. Wash hands after (unless treating hands)

For Lips

  1. Apply small amount
  2. Rub in gently
  3. Reapply as needed throughout day

For Muscle/Joint

  1. Apply generous amount to affected area
  2. Massage gently but firmly
  3. Can cover with warm cloth to retain warmth/moisture
  4. Wash hands after application
  5. Use 2–3x daily

Common Issues

Problem Cause Solution
Too soft/oily Too much oil, not enough wax Add more beeswax (melt with small amount old salve)
Too hard/waxy Too much wax, not enough oil Add more oil (warm both together)
Grainy texture Wax cooled unevenly Reheat and scoop into smooth container
Won't solidify Oil temperature too low when cooling Reheat higher, ensure consistent cooling
Separated into layers Ingredients didn't blend well Reheat and stir more thoroughly
White powder on top Beeswax separation (normal) Stir back in; doesn't affect quality
Grainy after months Natural wax separation Stir or reheat; doesn't indicate spoilage

Batch Size Converter

Base recipe: 1 cup oil + 1 oz wax

Desired Amount Oil Wax Containers Needed
Small (½ cup salve) ½ cup ½ oz 2–3 small jars (1 oz)
Regular (1 cup salve) 1 cup 1 oz 4–6 medium tins (0.5 oz)
Large (2 cups salve) 2 cups 2 oz 1 large jar or 8–10 tins

Gifting Salve

Making herbal salves is excellent for thoughtful gifts:

Packaging ideas:

  • Small glass jars (lovely presentation)
  • Metal tins (practical, travel-friendly)
  • Wrap with tissue paper, ribbon
  • Custom label with herb, date, suggested use
  • Include note on how to store/use

Gift combinations:

  • Set of three salves (Lavender + Calendula + Warming)
  • For gardeners: Lavender + Calendula wound salve
  • For athletes: Warming muscle salve
  • For sensitive skin: Chamomile-Calendula salve

🔗 Related Pages

Individual Herb Profiles: [[Master Plant Index]]

Medicinal Actions:

  • [[Wound & Circulation]]
